entries.description: Check text against a brand voice profile using an LLM and score brand compliance.

schema.title: AI Brand Voice Check
schema.description: Check text against a brand voice profile using an LLM provider

toolMeta.description: Check text against a brand voice profile using an LLM provider

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

profileId.title: Profile ID
profileId.description: Brand voice profile to resolve from the store

doc.overview:
The **AI Brand Voice Check** tool evaluates each block's source text against a
brand voice profile using a configured LLM provider. The model returns
findings across voice dimensions — tone, style, clarity, and brand compliance —
each with a severity, message, and suggestion. The tool computes an overall
brand-compliance score, records the score and any findings on the block's
properties, and attaches a brand-voice annotation to the block. Blocks with
empty source text are skipped.

Provider credentials are required, since the tool makes an external API call per
block. The voice profile that defines the brand's guidelines is supplied by a
profile identifier resolved from the store, or programmatically by the host
(for example the brand command or a project's brand binding). This is the
LLM-based companion to the rule-based brand vocabulary check.

apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the selected provider. Local providers such as Ollama may not
require a key. Prefer the credential store or environment over committing
keys to a recipe.

model.description: The model name to request from the provider.
model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's default
model is used.

profileId.description: Brand voice profile to resolve from the store.
profileId.help:
The identifier of the voice profile whose guidelines the text is checked
against. The profile may instead be supplied programmatically by the host,
in which case this field is not needed.

provider.description: The LLM provider backend to use.
provider.help: Selects which model backend performs the brand voice check.

limitations: Requires AI provider credentials; makes an external API call per block.
Without a resolved voice profile, the prompt has no brand guidelines to check against.
Evaluates source text; findings are model output and reflect the chosen model's judgement.
processingNotes: Operates on blocks; blocks with empty source text are skipped.
Blocks are processed in parallel by default for throughput.
A brand-voice score and findings are stored on the block, and a brand-voice annotation is attached.

examples.title: Check against a stored profile
examples.description: Resolve a named brand voice profile and evaluate text against it.

entries.description: Detect named entities and terminology candidates in source content using an LLM.

schema.title: AI Entity Extract
schema.description: Detect named entities (people, organizations, products, locations) using an LLM

toolMeta.description: Detect named entities (people, organizations, products, locations) using an LLM

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

batchConcurrency.description: Number of concurrent batch calls (0 or 1 = sequential)

batchSize.description: Number of blocks per LLM call (0 or 1 = one block per call)

knownTerms.description: Terms to exclude from extraction (already in termbase)

doc.overview:
The **AI Entity Extract** tool analyses a block's source text with a large
language model and records two kinds of stand-off annotation: named entities
(people, organizations, products, locations, and also dates, times,
currencies, and measurements) and terminology candidates (domain-specific
terms that would benefit from a termbase entry). Each entity carries a
suggested do-not-translate flag; each term candidate carries a category and a
translatability classification (do-not-translate, consistent, or free). It is
read-only — it writes annotations only and never changes the source or target.

Extraction can optionally combine the LLM with a NER provider for fast entity
detection; the LLM classification is preferred where the two overlap. Blocks
can be analysed one at a time or grouped into batches sent in a single
structured call, and batches can run concurrently. Known terms already in the
termbase can be supplied so they are not re-proposed. A provider and, for
hosted providers, credentials are required.

apiKey.description: API key for the provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the chosen hosted provider. Required for hosted providers;
not needed for a local Ollama backend.

batchConcurrency.description: Number of batch calls to run in parallel.
batchConcurrency.help:
How many batches are analysed concurrently. A value of 0 or 1 runs batches
sequentially.

batchSize.description: Number of blocks per LLM call.
batchSize.help:
Blocks are grouped into batches of this size and analysed in a single
structured call per batch. A value of 0 or 1 analyses one block per call.

knownTerms.description: Terms to exclude from extraction (already in the termbase).
knownTerms.help:
Terms the LLM is told not to re-propose, so the candidate list focuses on
genuinely new terminology. Matching is case-insensitive.

locale.help:
The locale of the text being analysed, passed to the LLM and any NER
provider so detection is language-aware.

model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's
default model is used.

limitations: Requires a provider and, for hosted providers, valid credentials; hosted providers make billed, rate-limited network calls.
The NER provider is optional and supplied programmatically; with no NER provider, extraction is LLM-only.
Entity and term suggestions (including the do-not-translate flag and translatability) are model proposals and should be reviewed before acting on them.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks with non-empty source; other parts pass through unchanged.
Read-only — writes entity and term-candidate annotations and never modifies source or target.
When both an LLM and a NER provider produce an entity at the same span, the LLM classification is kept.
Dates, times, currencies, and measurements are not defaulted to do-not-translate, since they need locale-specific formatting.

examples.description: Analyse source blocks one at a time with an Anthropic model.

examples.title: Batched extraction
examples.description: Analyse blocks in batches of 20, four batches at a time.

entries.description: Check translation quality with an LLM and record the issues it finds.

schema.title: AI QA Check
schema.description: Check translation quality using an LLM provider

toolMeta.description: Check translation quality using an LLM provider

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

checks.title: Quality Checks
checks.description: Quality checks to perform (e.g. terminology fluency accuracy consistency)

doc.overview:
The **AI QA Check** tool sends each source/target pair to a configured LLM
provider and asks it to identify quality issues — by default terminology,
fluency, and accuracy. The model returns structured findings, each with a type,
severity, description, and suggestion, which are recorded on the block's
properties as QA issues along with the provider name and the checks performed.
Blocks without a translation for the target locale are skipped.

Both a target locale and provider credentials are required, since the tool makes
an external API call per block. It is the LLM-based counterpart to the
rule-based QA check: it can catch meaning and fluency problems that pattern
rules cannot, at the cost of latency and API usage.

apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the selected provider. Local providers such as Ollama may not
require a key. Prefer the credential store or environment over committing
keys to a recipe.

checks.description: The quality dimensions the model should check.
checks.help:
A list of dimensions for the model to evaluate (for example `terminology`,
`fluency`, `accuracy`, `consistency`). When omitted, the tool checks
terminology, fluency, and accuracy.

model.description: The model name to request from the provider.
model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's default
model is used.

provider.description: The LLM provider backend to use.
provider.help: Selects which model backend performs the quality check.

limitations: Requires AI provider credentials and a target locale; makes an external API call per block.
Findings are model output and reflect the chosen model's judgement; review before acting on them.
Blocks without a translation for the target locale are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on blocks that have a translation for the target locale.
Blocks are processed in parallel by default for throughput.
Findings are stored on the block as a JSON array of QA issues.

examples.description: Restrict the model to the dimensions you care about.

examples.description: Run the check against a locally hosted model.

entries.description: Review each translation with an LLM, producing a score, assessment, and suggestion.

schema.title: AI Review
schema.description: Review translations with scoring using an LLM provider

toolMeta.description: Review translations with scoring using an LLM provider

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

doc.overview:
The **AI Review** tool asks a configured LLM provider to review each
translation and return a concise assessment: a score from 1 to 10, a brief
judgement of accuracy and fluency, and a suggested improvement where warranted.
The review text is recorded on the block's properties along with the provider
name. Blocks without a translation for the target locale are skipped.

Both a target locale and provider credentials are required, since the tool makes
an external API call per block. Unlike the AI QA check, which returns structured
issues, this tool produces a free-form review intended for human reviewers
deciding whether a translation needs revision.

apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the selected provider. Local providers such as Ollama may not
require a key. Prefer the credential store or environment over committing
keys to a recipe.

model.description: The model name to request from the provider.
model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's default
model is used.

provider.description: The LLM provider backend to use.
provider.help: Selects which model backend performs the review.

limitations: Requires AI provider credentials and a target locale; makes an external API call per block.
The review is free-form model output, not structured QA issues.
Blocks without a translation for the target locale are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on blocks that have a translation for the target locale.
Blocks are processed in parallel by default for throughput.
The review text and provider name are stored on the block's properties.

examples.description: Score and assess each translation using Anthropic.

entries.description: Extract candidate terminology from source text using an LLM provider.

schema.title: AI Terminology Extraction
schema.description: Extract candidate terminology from content using an LLM provider

toolMeta.description: Extract candidate terminology from content using an LLM provider

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

domain.title: Domain
domain.description: Subject domain for terminology extraction (e.g. medical legal technology)

doc.overview:
The **AI Terminology Extraction** tool sends each block's source text to a
configured LLM provider and asks it to return notable terms, each with a short
definition and domain. The extracted entries are recorded on the block's
properties as a terminology list. Blocks whose source is empty are skipped, and
blocks with no terms found are left unchanged.

Because the tool calls an external model, it requires provider credentials. An
optional subject domain can be supplied to bias extraction toward that field
(for example medical, legal, or technology terminology). The results are
candidate terms intended for review and curation into a termbase, not an
authoritative glossary.

apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the selected provider. Local providers such as Ollama may
not require a key. Prefer supplying credentials through the credential store
or environment rather than committing them to a recipe.

domain.description: Subject domain to bias terminology extraction.
domain.help:
An optional hint naming the subject area (for example `medical`, `legal`, or
`technology`). When set, it is included in the prompt so the model favours
terms relevant to that domain.

model.description: The model name to request from the provider.
model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's
default model is used.

provider.description: The LLM provider backend to use.
provider.help: Selects which model backend performs the extraction.

limitations: Requires AI provider credentials and makes an external API call per block.
Extracted terms are model output and should be reviewed before being treated as authoritative.
Operates on source text only.
processingNotes: Operates on blocks; blocks with empty source text are skipped.
Blocks are processed in parallel by default for throughput.

examples.description: Bias extraction toward a particular subject area.

entries.description: Translate block content using a configured LLM provider.

schema.title: AI Translate
schema.description: Translate content using an LLM provider

toolMeta.description: Translate content using an LLM provider

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the AI provider

batchConcurrency.title: Batch Concurrency
batchConcurrency.description: Number of concurrent batch calls (0 or 1 = sequential)

batchSize.title: Batch Size
batchSize.description: Number of blocks per LLM call

skipMatched.title: Skip Matched
skipMatched.description: Skip blocks that already have a target translation

doc.overview:
The **AI Translate** tool translates untranslated block content with a large
language model. It supports several providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini,
Azure OpenAI, Ollama) and writes the result as the target text, recording an
alternate-translation annotation with the originating provider. A target
language is required, and provider credentials are required for hosted
providers.

Blocks that contain inline codes are sent through a placeholder-preserving
prompt so tags survive translation, then reconstructed from the response.
Blocks can be translated one at a time or grouped into batches sent in a
single structured call; batches can run concurrently. When run with a session
cache, already-translated targets are reused and fresh translations are
written back, so repeat runs skip work already done.

apiKey.description: API key for the provider.
apiKey.help:
Credential for the chosen hosted provider. Required for hosted providers;
not needed for a local Ollama backend.

batchConcurrency.description: Number of batch calls to run in parallel.
batchConcurrency.help:
How many batches are translated concurrently. A value of 0 or 1 runs
batches sequentially.

batchSize.description: Number of blocks per LLM call.
batchSize.help:
Blocks are grouped into batches of this size and translated in a single
structured call per batch. A value of 1 translates block by block.

model.help:
The provider-specific model identifier. When omitted, the provider's
default model is used.

skipMatched.description: Skip blocks that already have a target translation.
skipMatched.help:
When enabled, a block that already has a target for the locale is left
untouched, so only untranslated content is sent to the model.

limitations: Requires a target language and, for hosted providers, valid credentials.
Hosted providers make network API calls; cost and latency scale with content volume.
Session-cache reuse applies on the block-by-block path; the batched path writes overlays on output but does not pre-skip via the per-block cache.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks with non-empty source; other parts pass through unchanged.
Blocks with inline codes are translated through a placeholder-preserving prompt and reconstructed from the response.
Each translation is recorded as an alternate-translation annotation tagged with the provider.

examples.title: Translate with Anthropic
examples.description: Translate untranslated blocks using an Anthropic model.

examples.title: Translate only new content
examples.description: Skip blocks that already have a translation.

entries.description: Collect blocks into fixed-size batches before forwarding them downstream.

size.title: Batch Size
size.description: Number of blocks to collect before forwarding as a batch

doc.overview:
The **Batch Collector** buffers translatable blocks and forwards them in
groups of a configured size, instead of one at a time. It exists so that a
downstream tool which is cheaper to call in bulk — for example a batch
translation request — can receive several blocks together rather than block
by block.

Non-block parts (layer markers, data, media) pass through immediately and are
never buffered. Blocks accumulate until the batch reaches the configured size
and are then released in order; any remaining blocks are flushed when the
input stream ends. Stream order is preserved.

size.description: Number of blocks to collect before forwarding a batch.
size.help:
The tool buffers blocks until this many have accumulated, then forwards
them as a group. A larger size means fewer, larger handoffs to the next
tool; a size of `1` effectively forwards each block as soon as it arrives.

limitations: Only blocks are batched; all other part types pass through without buffering.
processingNotes: Remaining buffered blocks are flushed when the input stream closes.
Block order within and across batches is preserved.

examples.title: Collect blocks in groups of 50
examples.description: Buffer 50 blocks per handoff for a bulk-friendly downstream tool.

toolMeta.description: Creates translations from an external program for a given input document. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: The Batch Translation step creates translation memory entries from text units extracted from a raw document, using an external command-line tool to provide translations. It extracts content into a temporary HTML document, invokes the external translator, then aligns source and translated segments into a Pensieve TM and/or TMX document. Large documents are handled by processing text units in configurable blocks.

blockSize.help:
Controls how many text units are grouped into each temporary HTML file sent to the external tool. This allows processing of very large documents even when the external tool has size limitations.

For example, a document with 5000 text units and a block size of 1000 will result in 5 separate invocations of the external tool.

checkExistingTm.description: Skips entries that already have translations in an existing Pensieve TM
checkExistingTm.help:
Looks up each candidate entry in an existing Pensieve TM before sending it for translation. Entries that already have a translation in the existing TM are **skipped** — they are not sent to the external tool and are not included in the optional TMX output.

This avoids re-translating content you already have translations for.

command.help:
The command line that invokes your external translation tool. The step creates a temporary HTML file where each paragraph is a source segment, and your tool must read it and produce a translated HTML file in the same format.

Available variables:

| Variable | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `${inputPath}` | Full path of the temp input HTML | |
| `${inputURI}` | URI of the temp input HTML | |
| `${outputPath}` | Full path where the tool must write translated HTML | |
| `${outputURI}` | URI of the expected output | |
| `${srcLangName}` | English name of source language | `de-ch` → `German` |
| `${trgLangName}` | English name of target language | `ja-jp` → `Japanese` |
| `${srcLang}` | Source language code | `de-ch` → `de` |
| `${trgLang}` | Target language code | `ja-jp` → `ja` |
| `${rootDir}` | Project/batch root directory | |
| `${inputRootDir}` | Root directory for input files | |

Locale-related variables are also available.
command.values: A shell command string with `${variable}` placeholders. The external tool **must** read from `${inputPath}` and write translated HTML to `${outputPath}`.
examples: `apertium -f html ${srcLang}-${trgLang} ${inputPath} ${outputPath}` — open-source Apertium MT under Linux

existingTm.help: Directory of the existing Pensieve TM to check for pre-existing translations. Supports `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables in the path.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this path to be used

makeTM.description: Imports translated entries into a Pensieve TM, creating it if necessary
makeTM.help:
Imports the translated entries into a Pensieve TM at the specified directory. If the TM does not exist, it is created. If it already exists, new entries are appended.

Entries are indexed at the end of each input document, so downstream pipeline steps can only access entries generated from **previous** documents, not the current one.
notes: The TM directory path supports `${rootDir}` and `${inputRootDir}` variables, plus locale variables.

makeTMX.description: Creates a TMX document with all translated entries across all input documents
makeTMX.help: Creates a TMX document containing all translated entries. A **single** TMX file is generated across all input documents. The file is not written until processing of the last document completes, so it **cannot** be used by downstream pipeline steps.
notes: If the output file already exists, it will be overwritten.

markAsMT.description: Marks generated TM entries as machine translation by setting creationId to "MT!"
markAsMT.help: Marks all generated TM entries as machine translation results. When enabled, the `creationId` attribute of the target is set to `"MT!"` in the output. This allows downstream tools and editors to distinguish MT output from human translations.

origin.help: An optional identifier string stored as a property on each translated entry. In TMX output, it appears as `<prop type="Txt::Origin">value</prop>`. Useful for tracking which translation tool or run produced the entries.

segment.description: Segments text units into sentences using SRX rules before translation
segment.help:
Segments extracted text units using SRX rules before building the temporary HTML. When enabled, each HTML paragraph corresponds to a **sentence**. When disabled, each paragraph is an unsegmented text unit.

Segmentation affects granularity of both the translation and the resulting TM/TMX entries.
notes: Only entries processed by the external tool are placed in the TMX output. Entries that already exist in the TM being populated or in the existing TM are **not** copied into the TMX output.

sendTMX.description: Sends the generated TMX document to the next step instead of the original input document
sendTMX.help: Sends the generated TMX document as the raw document to the next pipeline step, **instead of** the original input document. Without this option, the step passes the unmodified input document through.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when makeTMX is enabled, since there is no TMX document to send otherwise

srxPath.help: Full path to the SRX segmentation rules file. Supports `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables in the path.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this path to be used

tmDirectory.help: Directory of the Pensieve TM to create or populate. If the directory does not exist, a new TM is created. Supports `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables in the path.

tmxPath.help: Full path of the TMX file to generate. If the file exists, it will be overwritten. Supports `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables in the path.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes:
The step extracts content into temporary HTML files where each `

` element is one source segment or text unit. The external tool must preserve this paragraph structure in its output.
Large documents are split into multiple temporary HTML files based on the **block size** setting, with the external tool invoked once per block.
The raw input document passes through the step unmodified (unless **Send TMX to next step** is enabled), so downstream steps see the original document.
Pensieve TM entries are indexed per-document, so downstream steps can only leverage TM entries from previously processed documents in the batch.

examples.title: Apertium MT on Linux
examples.description: Uses the open-source Apertium machine translation system to translate documents. Language pair is derived automatically from the pipeline's source and target locales.
examples.config:
```yaml
command: "apertium -f html ${srcLang}-${trgLang} ${inputPath} ${outputPath}"
blockSize: 1000
markAsMT: true
makeTMX: true
tmxPath: "${rootDir}/output.tmx"
```

examples.title: ProMT on Windows
examples.description: Uses the commercial ProMT application to translate temporary HTML documents on Windows.
examples.config:
```yaml
command: "\"C:\\Program Files\\PRMT9\\FILETRANS\\FileTranslator.exe\" ${inputPath} /as /ac /d:${srcLangName}-${trgLangName} /o:${outputPath}"
blockSize: 500
markAsMT: true
origin: "promt-9"
makeTM: true
tmDirectory: "${rootDir}/tm"
```

entries.description: Add or remove Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) in a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: BOM Conversion
schema.description: Add or remove Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) in a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

removeBOM.title: Remove BOM If Present
removeBOM.description: Remove the Byte-Order-Mark from input files if detected; when disabled, adds a BOM if not already present.

alsoNonUTF8.title: Remove UTF-16 BOMs Also
alsoNonUTF8.description: Also remove the BOM from UTF-16 files, not just UTF-8.

toolMeta.description: Add or remove Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) in a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Adds or removes the Unicode Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) to or from UTF-8 and UTF-16 input files. The BOM is a special Unicode mark indicating byte order: little-endian (U+FFFE) or big-endian (U+FEFF). This step operates on raw documents and passes them through as raw documents.

alsoNonUTF8.description: Also remove the BOM from UTF-16 files, not just UTF-8.
alsoNonUTF8.help: Extends BOM removal to also cover UTF-16 files. By default, only UTF-8 BOMs are removed when `removeBOM` is enabled.
notes: **Warning:** Removing the BOM from UTF-16 files is not recommended. UTF-16 files must have a BOM to indicate byte order.

dependsOn.condition: must be true — only meaningful when removing BOMs

removeBOM.description: Remove the Byte-Order-Mark from input files if detected; when disabled, adds a BOM if not already present.
removeBOM.help:
Controls whether the step **removes** or **adds** the BOM:

- **Enabled**: Removes the BOM from input files if one is detected. By default, only UTF-8 BOMs are removed.
- **Disabled**: Adds a BOM to input files if one is not already present. The input files must already be in UTF-8 or UTF-16, and you must specify the encoding of each file so the correct type of BOM is added.
notes: When adding a BOM (option disabled), you must specify the encoding of each file so the utility can add the proper type of BOM.

limitations: Only UTF-8 and UTF-16 files are supported — UTF-32 is not currently handled.
processingNotes: The step operates on raw documents — it does not parse or segment content.
When adding BOMs, the correct BOM type (UTF-8 vs UTF-16 LE/BE) is determined from the file's encoding setting.

examples.title: Remove UTF-8 BOM
examples.description: Remove the BOM from UTF-8 files only (the default removal behavior).

examples.title: Add BOM to files missing one
examples.description: Add a BOM to UTF-8 or UTF-16 input files that do not already have one. Ensure the encoding is specified for each file.

entries.description: Add or remove the Unicode byte-order mark (BOM) on document layers.

addBOM.title: Add BOM
addBOM.description: When true the Unicode BOM is added; when false it is removed

alsoNonUTF8.title: Remove UTF-16 BOMs Also
alsoNonUTF8.description: Also remove the BOM from UTF-16 files not just UTF-8

doc.overview:
The **BOM Convert** tool controls whether the output document carries a
Unicode byte-order mark. It operates on layer-start parts: when a layer
begins, the tool sets that layer's `HasBOM` flag, and the writer honours it
when serializing the document.

Use it to add a BOM where a downstream consumer expects one, or to strip a
BOM that would otherwise interfere with parsing. The tool changes only the
BOM flag on layers; it does not alter block text.

addBOM.description: Add the BOM when true; remove it when false.
addBOM.help:
When enabled, the document layer is marked to be written with a Unicode
BOM. When disabled, the layer is marked to be written without one.

alsoNonUTF8.description: Also handle the BOM on UTF-16 documents, not only UTF-8.
alsoNonUTF8.help:
By default the BOM handling targets UTF-8 documents. Enable this to also
apply the BOM setting to UTF-16 encoded files.

processingNotes: Operates on layer-start parts; the layer's BOM flag is set and the writer applies it.
Block, data, and media parts pass through unchanged.

examples.title: Remove the BOM
examples.description: Mark the document to be written without a byte-order mark.

examples.title: Add a BOM, including UTF-16
examples.description: Ensure a BOM is present on both UTF-8 and UTF-16 output.

entries.description: Flag forbidden, competitor, and preferred-term violations from a brand voice profile.

doc.overview:
The **Brand Vocabulary Check** tool scans each block's source text for
vocabulary violations defined by a brand voice profile. Forbidden terms are
flagged at major severity, competitor terms at critical severity, and where a
forbidden term has a preferred replacement, the finding includes that
suggestion. When a termbase is provided, the tool also looks up brand-vocabulary
terms in it, flagging competitor and forbidden entries it finds. Matches are
case-insensitive.

Findings are recorded on the block's properties and an overall brand-voice score
is attached as an annotation. This is a rule-based check that needs no LLM
credentials, and it is designed to run before the LLM-based brand voice check.
The voice profile and termbase are supplied programmatically rather than as
schema parameters; the profile can also be resolved lazily from an
organizational context hierarchy.
limitations: The voice profile and termbase are configured programmatically; they are not exposed as schema parameters.
Term matching is case-insensitive substring matching; it does not account for inflection or morphology.
Evaluates source text.
processingNotes: Operates on blocks; blocks with empty source text are skipped.
Without a resolved profile or termbase, no findings are produced.
Findings are stored on the block and a brand-voice annotation with an overall score is attached.

examples.title: Add to a flow
examples.description: The voice profile and termbase are supplied programmatically; reference the tool with an empty configuration.

entries.description: Change the letter case of source and/or target text to upper, lower, or title case.

doc.overview:
The **Case Transform** tool rewrites the case of text in translatable blocks.
It can uppercase, lowercase, or title-case the source text, the target text,
or both. It is useful for normalizing display strings — for example forcing
UI labels to upper case — or for preparing case-insensitive comparisons.

When applying to the target, a target locale must be set; the tool only
transforms locales that already have a target. Non-translatable blocks pass
through unchanged.

applySource.description: Apply the transformation to source text.
applySource.help: When enabled, the source text of each translatable block is transformed.

applyTarget.description: Apply the transformation to target text.
applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the target text is transformed for the configured target
locale. A target locale must be set; only existing targets are touched.

mode.help:
Selects the transformation. `upper` converts text to upper case, `lower`
to lower case, and `title` to title case (every letter uppercased, using
the Unicode title-case mapping).

limitations: Title case uses the Unicode title-case mapping applied to every letter, not word-initial capitalization.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Target transformation requires a target locale and only affects locales that already have a target.

examples.title: Uppercase source text
examples.description: Force all source strings to upper case.

examples.title: Lowercase the target translation
examples.description: Normalize target text to lower case for a locale.
examples.config:
mode: lower
applySource: false
applyTarget: true

entries.description: Count the characters in each block's source and target text, with and without spaces.

countInline.title: Count Inline Codes
countInline.description: Include inline code content in character counts

doc.overview:
The **Character Count** tool tallies the number of Unicode characters in every
translatable block. For each scope it records two figures: the total character
count and a count that excludes spaces. The results are stored on the block's
properties so downstream tools and reports can read them.

Counts are taken over the block's segmented text. By default both the source
and the active target locale are counted; the target is only counted when a
target locale is set and the block has a translation for it. Character counting
is useful for length budgeting (for example, UI strings with a fixed width) and
for character-based pricing.

countInline.description: Include inline-code content in the character counts.
countInline.help:
By default, inline codes — placeholders, variables, and tag content
represented as coded text — are **excluded** from the count, since they are
markup rather than translatable characters. Enable this only when you want
inline-code text reflected in the totals.

countSource.description: Count characters in the source text.
countSource.help:
When enabled, the characters in each block's **source** text are counted,
both including and excluding spaces. When neither source nor target is
explicitly requested, the tool counts both.

countTarget.description: Count characters in the target text.
countTarget.help:
When enabled, the characters in each block's **target** text are counted
for the active target locale, both including and excluding spaces. The
target count is only recorded when a target locale is configured and the
block has a translation for it.

limitations: Counting follows the active segmentation; results depend on how the text was segmented.
Target counts require a configured target locale and an existing translation for the block.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
For each counted scope, both a total and a no-space character count are recorded on the block.

examples.title: Count source characters only
examples.description: Measure source-side character volume, ignoring any existing translations.

examples.title: Include inline codes
examples.description: Reflect placeholder and tag text in the character totals.

entries.description: Count the number of characters in the text units of a set of documents or/and in its parts. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Character Count
schema.description: Count the number of characters in the text units of a set of documents or/and in its parts. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

countInTextUnits.title: Text Units
countInTextUnits.description: Create character count annotations for text unit resources.

countInBatch.title: Batches
countInBatch.description: Add character count annotation aggregated per batch

countInBatchItems.title: Batch items
countInBatchItems.description: Add character count annotation for each batch item

countInDocuments.title: Documents
countInDocuments.description: Add character count annotation for each document

countInSubDocuments.title: Sub-documents
countInSubDocuments.description: Add character count annotation for each sub-document

countInGroups.title: Groups
countInGroups.description: Add character count annotation for each group

bufferSize.title: Size of text buffer:
bufferSize.description: Size of the internal text buffer used during counting (0 for default)

simplifierRules.description: Code simplifier rules for simplifying inline codes before counting.
simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules

toolMeta.description: Count the number of characters in the text units of a set of documents or/and in its parts. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Performs character counts on different parts of a set of documents, following the [GMX-V 2.0 standard](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html). Character counts are saved as annotations on text units and optionally on higher-level resources (batches, documents, groups). These annotations can be consumed by other steps such as the Scoping Report Step to generate reports.

bufferSize.description: Size of the internal text buffer used during counting (0 for default)
bufferSize.help: Controls the size of the internal text buffer used during counting. A value of `0` uses the default buffer size. Increase for very large documents if memory permits.
bufferSize.values: `0` for default size, or a positive integer specifying the buffer size in characters.

countInBatch.description: Add character count annotation aggregated per batch
countInBatch.help: Adds a character count annotation aggregated across the entire batch. Useful for getting a total count across all documents processed in a pipeline run.

countInBatchItems.description: Add character count annotation for each batch item
countInBatchItems.help: Adds a character count annotation for each batch item. A batch item typically corresponds to a single input/output document pair in the pipeline.

countInDocuments.description: Add character count annotation for each document
countInDocuments.help: Adds a character count annotation for each document. Enable this when you need per-document character counts, e.g. for per-file reporting.

countInGroups.description: Add character count annotation for each group
countInGroups.help: Adds a character count annotation for each group resource. Groups are logical collections of text units defined by the filter (e.g. table rows, list items).

countInSubDocuments.description: Add character count annotation for each sub-document
countInSubDocuments.help: Adds a character count annotation for each sub-document. Sub-documents occur when a filter extracts embedded content (e.g. HTML inside an XML file).

processingNotes: Character counts always include a per-text-unit annotation regardless of parameter settings; the boolean options control additional aggregation levels.
Counts follow the [GMX-V 2.0 standard](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html).
Count annotations are stored as resource-level annotations consumable by downstream steps such as the Scoping Report Step.

examples.title: Character count for scoping report
examples.description: Enable batch and document-level counts, then feed the results into the Scoping Report Step to generate a per-document word/character count report.

entries.description: Check target text for forbidden, missing, out-of-charset, or corrupted characters.

charset.title: Character Set Encoding
charset.description: Name of the character set encoding to check against (e.g. ISO-8859-1)

checkCharset.title: Check Against Charset Encoding
checkCharset.description: Warn if a character is not included in the specified character set encoding

checkCorrupted.title: Check Corrupted Characters
checkCorrupted.description: Check for common corruption patterns such as mojibake

forbiddenChars.title: Forbidden Characters
forbiddenChars.description: Characters that should not appear in target text (e.g. {}[])

requiredChars.title: Required Characters
requiredChars.description: Characters that must appear in target if present in source (e.g. punctuation)

doc.overview:
The **Characters Check** tool inspects each translation for character-level
problems and records findings on the block's properties as QA issues. It can
flag forbidden characters that must not appear in the target, required
characters that are present in the source but missing from the target, and
characters that cannot be encoded in a named character set. It also detects
common corruption: mojibake (UTF-8 misread as Latin-1), the Unicode replacement
character, and control characters. A target locale is required.

Corruption checking is on by default. The forbidden, required, and charset
checks are opt-in: the forbidden and required checks activate when their
character lists are supplied, and the charset check activates when explicitly
enabled.

charset.description: Name of the character set encoding to check against.
charset.help:
The IANA character set name used by the charset check (for example
`ISO-8859-1`). An unknown name produces a warning rather than failing the
run.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this to take effect

checkCharset.description: Check target characters against a named character set encoding.
checkCharset.help:
When enabled, every target character is tested for encodability in the
character set named by `charset`; characters that cannot be encoded are
flagged with a warning.

dependsOn.condition: names the character set used when this check is enabled

checkCorrupted.description: Check for common corruption patterns such as mojibake.
checkCorrupted.help:
When enabled, the target is scanned for sequences that indicate encoding
corruption — common mojibake pairs, the Unicode replacement character
(U+FFFD), and control characters other than tab, newline, and carriage
return.

forbiddenChars.description: Characters that must not appear in the target.
forbiddenChars.help:
A string whose characters are each treated as forbidden. The target is
flagged with an error for every forbidden character it contains. Leave empty
to skip this check.

requiredChars.description: Characters that must appear in the target if present in the source.
requiredChars.help:
A string whose characters must appear in the target whenever they appear in
the source. A warning is raised for each such character missing from the
target. Leave empty to skip this check.

limitations: Forbidden and required character lists are matched per character, not as substrings or patterns.
Corruption detection covers a fixed set of common mojibake patterns plus replacement and control characters.
Requires a configured target locale; blocks without a translation for it are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Findings are stored on the block as a JSON array of QA issues.

examples.title: Forbid specific characters
examples.description: Flag any target containing curly braces or square brackets.

examples.title: Restrict to a legacy encoding
examples.description: Warn about characters that cannot be encoded in ISO-8859-1.

entries.description: Collect the distinct characters used across source and target text, with their frequencies.

includeSource.title: Include Source
includeSource.description: Include source text in character listing

includeTarget.title: Include Target
includeTarget.description: Include target text in character listing

doc.overview:
The **Characters Listing** tool scans translatable blocks and accumulates the
set of distinct characters used, together with how often each one occurs. It
also records the number of unique characters per block on the block's
properties. The accumulated character set is typically used for font subsetting
— determining which glyphs a font must provide for a given body of content.

By default both source and target text are scanned. When target text is
included, a target locale must be supplied so the tool knows which translation
to read.

includeSource.description: Include source text when collecting characters.
includeSource.help:
When enabled, every character in each block's **source** text is added to
the character set and its frequency tally. At least one of source or target
must be included.

includeTarget.description: Include target text when collecting characters.
includeTarget.help:
When enabled, characters from each block's **target** text are added to the
set for the active target locale. A target locale is required when this is
enabled.

dependsOn.condition: requires a configured target locale when enabled

limitations: At least one of source or target inclusion must be enabled.
Including target text requires a configured target locale.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Frequencies are accumulated across all blocks; the per-block unique-character count is recorded on each block.

examples.title: List source characters only
examples.description: Build the character set from source text alone, for example before any translation exists.

entries.description: Compare source and target for character inconsistencies. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Characters Checker
schema.description: Compare source and target for character inconsistencies. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

checkCharacters.title: Warn if a character is not included in the following character set encoding
checkCharacters.description: Warn if a character is not in the specified character set encoding.

checkAllowedCharacters.title: Verify the ITS Allowed Characters property
checkAllowedCharacters.description: Check target characters against the ITS Allowed Characters property.

charset.title: Character Set Encoding
charset.description: Name of the character set encoding to check against (e.g. ISO-8859-1).

extraCharsAllowed.title: Allow the characters matching the following regular expression pattern
extraCharsAllowed.description: Regular expression matching characters to allow beyond the character set.

corruptedCharacters.title: Warn if some possibly corrupted characters are found in the target entry
corruptedCharacters.description: Check for patterns indicating corrupted characters.

toolMeta.description: Compare source and target for character inconsistencies. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: The Characters Checker step inspects the target of each text unit for character-level problems. It can detect patterns that commonly indicate encoding corruption (for example a UTF-8 file decoded as ISO-8859-1) and can validate that every target character is representable in a chosen charset, with an optional regular expression listing additional characters to allow. It expects filter events and passes them through unchanged, attaching the detected issues for a downstream consumer to report. It is one of the granular quality checks that the aggregate Quality Check step combines into a single pass.
limitations: Corrupted-character detection recognises only common patterns and will not catch every possible encoding fault.
processingNotes: Operates on filter events; events are forwarded unchanged and the detected issues are accumulated per document.
Delegates to the verification library's `CharactersChecker`; its step parameters are a subset of the full Quality Check / CheckMate configuration.

Corrupted-character detection and the allowed-characters check are enabled by default, while the charset validation (`checkCharacters`) is disabled by default; the default charset is `ISO-8859-1` and the extra-characters regex is empty.
Requires source and target locales; these are supplied by the pipeline rather than configured on the step.

examples.title: Detect corrupted characters and restrict the target to a charset
examples.description: Leaves corrupted-character detection on (catching mojibake from encoding mismatches) and enables the allowed-character check so any target character outside ISO-8859-1 — apart from those listed in `extraCharsAllowed` — is reported.
examples.config:
```yaml
corruptedCharacters: true
checkAllowedCharacters: true
charset: "ISO-8859-1"
extraCharsAllowed: "€"
```

entries.description: Cleans strings by normalizing quotes, punctuation, etc. ready for further processing. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Cleanup
schema.description: Cleans strings by normalizing quotes, punctuation, etc. ready for further processing. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

normalizeQuotes.title: Normalize quotation marks
normalizeQuotes.description: Replace all quotation marks with straight double quotes and apostrophes with single straight quotes.

checkCharacters.title: Check for corrupt or unexpected characters
checkCharacters.description: Detect and remove text units containing common corrupt character strings.

matchRegexExpressions.title: Mark segments matching default regular expressions for removal
matchRegexExpressions.description: Mark segments matching built-in regular expressions for removal. This option is not currently used.

matchUserRegex.title: Mark segments matching user defined regular expressions for removal
matchUserRegex.description: Remove text units containing text that matches user-defined regular expressions.

userRegex.title: User Defined Regex
userRegex.description: Custom regular expression pattern for matching segments to mark for removal.

pruneTextUnit.title: Remove unnecessary segments from text unit
pruneTextUnit.description: Remove text units marked for removal or that have no target text.

normalizePunctuations.title: Standardize spaces before and after certain punctuation marks to English writing style.
normalizePunctuations.description: Standardize spaces around punctuation marks to English writing style conventions.

keepCrNlIntact.title: Keep CR and NL characters intact. They are not normalized to an ASCII space with spaces and tabs.
keepCrNlIntact.description: Preserve CR and NL characters instead of normalizing them to spaces.

toolMeta.description: Cleans strings by normalizing quotes, punctuation, etc. ready for further processing. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: The Cleanup step cleans strings by normalizing quotes, punctuation, and whitespace ready for further processing. By default, all whitespace is normalized before any further processing — all multiple space, tab, etc. characters are replaced with a single instance. It operates on filter events and requires both source and target language to be set.

checkCharacters.description: Detect and remove text units containing common corrupt character strings.
checkCharacters.help:
Detects and removes text units that contain common corrupt character strings.

This can catch encoding issues such as mojibake or other garbled text that would otherwise propagate through the pipeline.

keepCrNlIntact.description: Preserve CR and NL characters instead of normalizing them to spaces.
keepCrNlIntact.help:
Preserves carriage return (CR) and newline (NL) characters as-is during whitespace normalization.

By default, the step normalizes all whitespace (including CR/NL) to ASCII spaces. Enable this to keep line breaks in the text while still normalizing spaces and tabs.

matchRegexExpressions.description: Mark segments matching built-in regular expressions for removal. This option is not currently used.
matchRegexExpressions.help: Intended to mark segments matching built-in regular expressions for removal.
notes: **Note:** This option is not currently used by the step. Enabling or disabling it has no effect.

matchUserRegex.description: Remove text units containing text that matches user-defined regular expressions.
matchUserRegex.help:
Removes text units that contain text matching a user-defined regular expression.

Use this to filter out segments with specific patterns (e.g., placeholder-only strings, test data) before further processing.

normalizePunctuations.description: Standardize spaces around punctuation marks to English writing style conventions.
normalizePunctuations.help:
Standardizes spaces before and after certain punctuation marks to conform to English writing style conventions.

For example, ensures no space before periods or commas, and a single space after them.
notes: **Warning:** This applies English punctuation spacing rules, which may not be appropriate for all languages (e.g., French uses a space before `:`, `!`, `?`).

normalizeQuotes.description: Replace all quotation marks with straight double quotes and apostrophes with single straight quotes.
normalizeQuotes.help:
Replaces all quotation marks with straight double quotes (`"`) and all apostrophes with single straight quotes (`'`).

This is useful for normalizing curly/smart quotes and other typographic quote variants into a consistent ASCII form before further processing.

pruneTextUnit.description: Remove text units marked for removal or that have no target text.
pruneTextUnit.help:
Removes text units that have been marked for removal by other checks in this step, or that have no target text.

This is the final cleanup pass — segments flagged by the character check or regex matching are actually removed when this option is enabled.

limitations: Does not work with Asian languages.
Does not work with bi-directional (RTL) languages.
The `matchRegexExpressions` option is not currently implemented and has no effect.
processingNotes: All whitespace is normalized before any further processing — multiple spaces, tabs, etc. are replaced with a single space instance.
CR and NL characters are also normalized to spaces by default unless `keepCrNlIntact` is enabled.
Text units are only actually removed when `pruneTextUnit` is enabled, even if other options flag them for removal.

examples.title: Normalize quotes and whitespace only
examples.description: Tidy typographic quotes and collapse whitespace without removing any text units. Disabling the removal-oriented options keeps every segment in place while still cleaning quotes and spacing.
examples.config:
```yaml
normalizeQuotes: true
checkCharacters: false
matchUserRegex: false
pruneTextUnit: false
```

examples.title: Drop placeholder-only segments
examples.description: Flag segments matching a user-supplied pattern and actually remove them. `matchUserRegex` only marks text units for removal; `pruneTextUnit` must also be enabled for the marked units to be deleted.

entries.description: TM concordance or phrase match (usually a word or term only) Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Concordance Character Count
schema.description: TM concordance or phrase match (usually a word or term only) Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: TM concordance or phrase match (usually a word or term only) Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Counts the characters in text units whose top alternate translation is a `CONCORDANCE` match — a translation-memory concordance or phrase hit, typically covering only a word or term rather than a full segment. It is the character-counting counterpart of the Concordance Word Count Step, differing only in that it tallies characters rather than words.
limitations: The Okapi source notes that, as of its writing, no leveraging step yet annotates segments with the `CONCORDANCE` match type, so in practice this counter typically reports zero unless an upstream component supplies such matches.
processingNotes: Operates per segment: it inspects the first (top-ranked) `AltTranslation` on each target segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` and counts a segment only when that match type is exactly `CONCORDANCE`.
Counting is performed on the **source** text, not the target — the character total reflects the source volume leveraged at this match level.
Uses the GMX-V character counter; the only behavioural difference from the word-count variant is words versus characters.
Counts only translatable text units; empty text units and non-translatable ones are skipped.

Results are stored as annotations on filter events and aggregated to the configured levels (text unit, group, document, sub-document, batch item, batch); the step produces no output document of its own.

Belongs to the `OKAPI_CHARACTER_COUNTS` category group. The `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked matching category per segment — `CONCORDANCE` is the lowest-ranked of the leveraging match categories, below `MT` and all TM-derived categories.

entries.description: TM concordance or phrase match (usually a word or term only) Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Concordance Word Count
schema.description: TM concordance or phrase match (usually a word or term only) Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: TM concordance or phrase match (usually a word or term only) Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Counts the words in text units whose top alternate translation is a `CONCORDANCE` match — a translation-memory concordance or phrase hit, typically covering only a word or term rather than a full segment. The count is recorded as a word-count metric in a `MetricsAnnotation` so that scoping and pricing steps can report concordance-level leverage as its own, lowest-confidence category.
limitations: The Okapi source notes that, as of its writing, no leveraging step yet annotates segments with the `CONCORDANCE` match type, so in practice this counter typically reports zero unless an upstream component supplies such matches.
processingNotes: Operates per segment: it inspects the first (top-ranked) `AltTranslation` on each target segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` and counts a segment only when that match type is exactly `CONCORDANCE`.
Counting is performed on the **source** text, not the target — the word total reflects the source volume leveraged at this match level.
Words are counted with the GMX-V word counter; the parallel character-count variant counts characters instead.
Counts only translatable text units; empty text units and non-translatable ones are skipped.

Results are stored as annotations on filter events and aggregated to the configured levels (text unit, group, document, sub-document, batch item, batch); the step produces no output document of its own.

Belongs to the `OKAPI_WORD_COUNTS` category group. The `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked matching category per segment — `CONCORDANCE` is the lowest-ranked of the leveraging match categories, below `MT` and all TM-derived categories.

entries.description: Copies or moves the listed files to the specified location. Expects: raw documents. Sends back: raw documents.

schema.title: Copy Or Move
schema.description: Copies or moves the listed files to the specified location. Expects: raw documents. Sends back: raw documents.

toolMeta.description: Copies or moves the listed files to the specified location. Expects: raw documents. Sends back: raw documents.

doc.overview: This step copies or moves files from one location to another within the pipeline. It operates on raw documents and passes them through unchanged. Files are copied or moved into the location specified in the output settings tabs. Copy protection options control how conflicts with existing files are handled.
limitations: None known per the Okapi documentation.
processingNotes: Files are copied or moved into the location specified in the **output settings tabs**, not a parameter on this step itself.
This step passes through raw documents unchanged — it only affects file locations on disk.

examples.title: Copy files to output directory
examples.description: Use the step to copy source files to a target output directory, overwriting any existing files. Configure the output location in the pipeline's output settings tab.

doc.overview: Copies the source content into the target of a text unit when that target is missing or has no text. For each text unit whose source contains text, the step creates the target from the source (preserving segmentation) if no target exists, or sets the target content from the source if the target exists but is empty. Text units whose source has no text are left untouched, and existing non-empty targets are never overwritten. It expects filter events and passes them through unchanged apart from the targets it populates.
processingNotes: A target is considered empty when it is absent or contains no text (codes and whitespace alone do not count as text).
When the target is absent it is created from the source as a copy of the segmented content; when present but empty its content is set from the source as a string.
Text units whose source has no text are skipped, and targets that already contain text are preserved.
Requires the target locale, which is supplied by the pipeline rather than configured on the step.

entries.description: Create target segment containers for a locale, optionally seeded with the source text.

copySource.title: Copy Source
copySource.description: Copy the source runs into the new target

createOnNonTranslatable.title: Create on Non-Translatable
createOnNonTranslatable.description: Create a target even for non-translatable text units

overwrite.title: Overwrite Existing
overwrite.description: Overwrite the existing target if one already exists

doc.overview:
The **Create Target** tool ensures every block has a target segment for a
given locale. It is the standard first step when preparing content for
translation: it creates the empty (or source-seeded) target containers that
later tools — translation, leverage, QA — fill and check.

A target locale is required. By default existing targets are left untouched;
enable overwriting to replace them. Targets can be created for
non-translatable blocks as well, which keeps bilingual structure complete
across an entire document.

copySource.description: Seed the new target with the source text.
copySource.help:
When enabled, the source text is copied into the new target segment so it
starts as a duplicate of the source. When disabled, the target is created
empty.

createOnNonTranslatable.description: Also create targets on non-translatable blocks.
createOnNonTranslatable.help:
When enabled, target containers are created even for blocks marked
non-translatable, keeping the bilingual structure complete. Disable it to
skip non-translatable blocks entirely.

overwrite.help:
By default a block that already has a target for the locale is left
unchanged. Enable this to overwrite the existing target with a new
(empty or source-seeded) one.

targetLocale.description: The locale to create a target for.
targetLocale.help:
The BCP-47 locale identifier for the target to create (for example
`fr-FR`). On the CLI and in flows this is typically supplied by the
`--target-lang` value.

processingNotes: Existing targets are skipped unless overwrite is enabled.

examples.title: Create empty targets for a locale
examples.description: Prepare empty target containers ready for translation.

examples.title: Pre-fill targets with the source
examples.description: Seed targets with source text as a starting draft.

entries.description: Create target segment container. Optionally copy source content to the target. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Create Target
schema.description: Create target segment container. Optionally copy source content to the target. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

copyContent.title: Copy the source content to the target
copyContent.description: Copy the source text into the newly created target container.

copyProperties.title: Copy the source properties to the target
copyProperties.description: Copy the source properties (metadata) into the created target.

overwriteExisting.title: Overwrite the current target content
overwriteExisting.description: Overwrite existing target content and/or properties with source values.

createOnNonTranslatable.title: Creates target for non-translatable text units
createOnNonTranslatable.description: Create a target container even for non-translatable text units.

toolMeta.description: Create target segment container. Optionally copy source content to the target. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Creates the target segment container for each text unit in the pipeline. Optionally copies source content and/or source properties into the newly created target. This step operates on filter events and is typically placed early in a pipeline before any translation or leveraging steps.

copyContent.description: Copy the source text into the newly created target container.
copyContent.help:
Copies the source text into the newly created target container. This is useful when you want the target to start as a copy of the source — for example, before applying translation memory leveraging or machine translation.

If disabled, the target container is created empty.

copyProperties.description: Copy the source properties (metadata) into the created target.
copyProperties.help:
Copies source-level properties (metadata attributes on the text unit) into the target. Properties may include state information, notes, or other metadata attached to the source entry.

If disabled, the target is created with no properties.

createOnNonTranslatable.description: Create a target container even for non-translatable text units.
createOnNonTranslatable.help:
Controls whether a target container is created for text units marked as non-translatable. Some downstream steps may still need a target entry to exist even for non-translatable segments (e.g., for consistent output structure).

Disable this to skip target creation for non-translatable text units.

overwriteExisting.description: Overwrite existing target content and/or properties with source values.
overwriteExisting.help:
Forces the source content and/or properties to be copied into the target **even when a target already exists**. Without this option, existing targets are left untouched.

Useful when re-processing bilingual files where you want to reset all targets back to source content.
notes: Warning: Enabling this will discard any existing translations in the target. Use with care on bilingual files that already contain translated content.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Operates on filter events — must be placed after a filter step in the pipeline.
Requires both source and target languages to be set in the pipeline context.

examples.title: Seed targets with source content
examples.description: Create a target for each text unit, pre-filled with the source text and its properties. This is the common preparation before machine translation or TM leveraging that expects an existing target to write into.

examples.title: Create empty targets, skip non-translatable units
examples.description: Create empty target containers for translatable units only, leaving non-translatable units without a target. Disable content and property copying when a later step supplies the target text from scratch.
examples.config:
```yaml
copyContent: false
copyProperties: false
createOnNonTranslatable: false
```

entries.description: Translate block content using DeepL.

schema.title: DeepL Translate
schema.description: Translate content using DeepL

toolMeta.description: Translate content using DeepL

apiKey.title: API Key
apiKey.description: API key for the MT provider

email.title: Email
email.description: Account email for higher rate limits (mymemory)

doc.overview:
The **DeepL Translate** tool translates untranslated block content with the
DeepL API. The provider is fixed by the tool name, so there is no provider
field — only the DeepL credential applies. A target language is required and a
DeepL API key must be supplied (resolved from the OS keychain by the CLI, or
set inline in a recipe step).

Blocks that carry inline codes are round-tripped through semantic HTML so DeepL
preserves the tags natively, then reconstructed from the response. With a
session cache, targets already translated in a previous run are reused, so
repeat runs skip blocks that are already done. When constructed without
credentials the tool falls back to an offline demo provider so it remains
listable and runnable for illustration.

apiKey.description: DeepL API key.
apiKey.help:
The DeepL authentication key. Required to call the real DeepL API; without
it the tool falls back to the offline demo provider. Resolved from the OS
keychain by the CLI credential preprocessor, or set inline.

email.help: Part of the shared MT credential schema; consumed only by the MyMemory tool.

projectId.help: Part of the shared MT credential schema; consumed only by the Google Translate tool.

region.help: Part of the shared MT credential schema; consumed only by the Microsoft Translator tool.

subscriptionKey.help: Part of the shared MT credential schema; consumed only by the Microsoft Translator tool.

limitations: Requires a target language and a DeepL API key; without a key the tool runs the offline demo provider, not DeepL.
DeepL makes a network round-trip per block; cost and latency scale with content volume.
The provider is fixed to DeepL by the tool name and cannot be changed through configuration.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks with non-empty source; other parts pass through unchanged.
Blocks with inline codes round-trip through semantic HTML so tags survive translation.
With a session cache, already-translated targets are reused and fresh translations are cached.

examples.title: Translate with DeepL
examples.description: Translate untranslated blocks using a DeepL API key.

entries.description: Joins all segments into a single content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Desegmentation
schema.description: Joins all segments into a single content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

desegmentSource.title: Join all segments of the source text
desegmentSource.description: Joins all segments of each source text unit into a single content.

desegmentTarget.title: Join all segments of the target text
desegmentTarget.description: Joins all segments of each target text unit into a single content, if a target exists.

renumberCodes.title: Restore original IDs to renumbered codes
renumberCodes.description: Restores original code IDs that were renumbered during segmentation.

toolMeta.description: Joins all segments into a single content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: This step joins all segments of each source and/or target text unit into a single content, effectively reversing the segmentation process. Non-segmented entries and text units flagged as non-translatable are left untouched. It operates on filter events and is typically used after processing to recombine segments that were split by the Segmentation step.

desegmentSource.description: Joins all segments of each source text unit into a single content.
desegmentSource.help:
Joins all segments within each source text unit back into a single content.

Non-segmented entries and non-translatable text units are left untouched regardless of this setting.

desegmentTarget.description: Joins all segments of each target text unit into a single content, if a target exists.
desegmentTarget.help:
Joins all segments within each target text unit back into a single content. Only applies when a target text exists.

Non-segmented entries and non-translatable text units are left untouched regardless of this setting.

renumberCodes.description: Restores original code IDs that were renumbered during segmentation.
renumberCodes.help: Restores the original inline code IDs after desegmentation. When content was segmented using the **Renumber code IDs** option in the Segmentation step, code IDs were renumbered per-segment starting at `1`. This option reverses that renumbering by reconstructing the original single sequence of IDs across the joined content.
notes: This option is only meaningful when content was previously segmented with the **Renumber code IDs** option in the Segmentation step.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when at least one of desegmentSource or desegmentTarget is enabled

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Non-segmented entries are passed through untouched.
Text units flagged as non-translatable are passed through untouched.

examples.title: Rejoin segments before writing output
examples.description: Join the source and target segments of each text unit back into single content. Run this after translation and any segment-level processing, so the output document is written with whole paragraphs rather than per-sentence segments.

examples.title: Desegment and restore original code IDs
examples.description: When the upstream Segmentation step renumbered inline-code IDs per segment, enable `renumberCodes` so the joined content is rebuilt with a single original ID sequence. This option is only meaningful when at least one of `desegmentSource`/`desegmentTarget` is enabled.
examples.config:
```yaml
desegmentSource: true
desegmentTarget: true
renumberCodes: true
```

entries.description: Reuse translations from a previous document version for unchanged source blocks.

fuzzyMatch.title: Fuzzy Match
fuzzyMatch.description: Enable fuzzy matching for similar texts

doc.overview:
The **Diff Leverage** tool compares each block's source against the same
block in a previous version of the document and carries forward the previous
translation when the source is unchanged. It avoids re-translating content
that has not changed between versions, and tags each block with its diff
status — `new`, `unchanged`, `modified`, or `leveraged`. A target language is
required.

The previous version's source and target texts are supplied programmatically
(keyed by block ID); they are not part of the declarative schema. The two
schema-visible options control how the source comparison is performed.

caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, two sources must match exactly, including letter case, to
count as unchanged. When disabled, the comparison ignores case.

fuzzyMatch.description: Carry forward translations for closely similar sources.
fuzzyMatch.help:
When enabled, a changed source that is sufficiently similar to the
previous version (above an internal similarity threshold) still reuses the
previous translation and is tagged `leveraged` with a similarity score.
When disabled, only exact matches are reused.

limitations: Requires a target language and a previous-version corpus (supplied programmatically); the previous texts are not configurable via the schema.
Fuzzy matching uses a fixed internal similarity threshold.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Each block is tagged with a diff-status property; leveraged fuzzy matches also record a similarity score.

examples.title: Reuse exact matches only
examples.description: Carry forward translations where the source is identical.

examples.title: Allow fuzzy leverage
examples.description: Also reuse translations for closely similar sources.

entries.description: Compare two source documents (i.e., different versions) and copy the old target content when we find a match. Can be a monolingual and bi-lingual input or three monolingual inputs. Paragraphs (TextUnits) must align in all cases

schema.title: Diff Leverage
schema.description: Compare two source documents (i.e., different versions) and copy the old target content when we find a match. Can be a monolingual and bi-lingual input or three monolingual inputs. Paragraphs (TextUnits) must align in all cases

fuzzyThreshold.title: Leverage only if the match is equal or above this score
fuzzyThreshold.description: Fuzzy Thresholds are between 1 and 100. A score of 100 emans exact match (codes and text) only

codesensitive.title: Include inline codes in the comparison

diffOnly.title: Diff only and mark the TextUnit as matched
diffOnly.description: Diff only and do not copy the match or create a leverage annotation

copyToTarget.title: Copy to/over the target? (WARNING: Copied target will not be segmented!)
copyToTarget.description: Copy to/over the target (a leverage annotation will still be created). WARNING: Copied target will not be segmented and any exisiting target will be lost.

toolMeta.description: Compare two source documents (i.e., different versions) and copy the old target content when we find a match. Can be a monolingual and bi-lingual input or three monolingual inputs. Paragraphs (TextUnits) must align in all cases

doc.overview: The Diff Leverage step compares the source text of two (or three) documents to leverage existing translations for new source content. It matches text units between an old bilingual document and a new document, copying old target translations when source segments match above a configurable threshold. Supports both bilingual input (two documents) and monolingual input (three documents with aligned text units).

codesensitive.help:
Controls whether inline codes (formatting tags, placeholders) are included when comparing the old and new source text. When enabled, codes must also match for the text unit to reach the leverage threshold. When disabled, only the textual content is compared, ignoring any differences in inline codes.

Disabling this can increase match rates when inline codes have changed but the text remains the same.
notes: When `fuzzyThreshold` is set to `100` and this option is enabled, both text and codes must match exactly for leveraging to occur.

copyToTarget.help:
Copies the matched old target content directly into the target of the new document. A leverage annotation is still created.

**Use with caution:** the copied target will **not** be segmented, and any existing target content will be overwritten and lost.
notes: **Warning:** Copied target will not be segmented and any existing target will be lost. This is a destructive operation on existing target content.

dependsOn.condition: has no effect when diffOnly is true

diffOnly.help:
Runs the comparison without copying translations or creating leverage annotations. Text units that match above the threshold are marked as matched, but no target content is modified.

Useful for previewing what would be leveraged before committing to changes.

dependsOn.condition: mutually exclusive — diffOnly skips copying entirely

fuzzyThreshold.help:
Controls the minimum match quality required before an old translation is leveraged. A score of **100** means only exact matches (both text and inline codes) are leveraged. Lower values allow fuzzy matches — the old target is copied even when the new source differs slightly from the old source.

The score represents a percentage of similarity between the old and new source text units.
