fuzzyThreshold.values: Integer between **1** and **100**. A value of `100` requires an exact match; lower values allow increasingly fuzzy matches.
examples: `80` — Allow moderate differences for rough leveraging

limitations: The comparison process is done on the full list of text units **in memory** — very large documents may not be processable.
When using three monolingual inputs, text units between the old source and old translation must be aligned (same number, same order).
processingNotes: Accepts two or three input documents: (1) new source + old bilingual, or (1) new source + (2) old source + (3) old translation.
Takes filter events as input and sends filter events as output — designed for use within a pipeline.
Text units (paragraphs) must align across all input documents for correct comparison.

examples.title: Two-document bilingual leveraging
examples.description: The most common usage: compare a new source document against an old bilingual document (containing both old source and old translation). Text units that match at or above the threshold get their old translations leveraged.

examples.title: Three-document monolingual leveraging
examples.description: When old source and old translation are in separate monolingual files, provide all three as inputs. The second (old source) and third (old translation) documents must have the same number of text units in the same order.

entries.description: Verify that do-not-translate terms survive verbatim into the target.

caseInsensitive.title: Case-insensitive preservation
caseInsensitive.description: Accept a case-folded match in the target instead of requiring exact case

doc.overview:
The **Do-Not-Translate Check** tool guards terms that must reach the target
unchanged — product names, trademarks, code identifiers. For each configured
term that appears in the source as a whole word, it verifies the term is
present verbatim in the target; when it is missing, the block is flagged with a
critical finding (the "the AI translated your product name" case). It is
read-only: it annotates findings and never rewrites content.

The list of protected terms is supplied programmatically rather than as a
schema field — a project, recipe, or checkset provides them, and the termbase
can contribute more. A target locale is required; blocks without a target for
that locale are skipped. The only schema-level option controls whether a
case-folded match in the target is accepted.

caseInsensitive.description: Accept a case-folded match in the target instead of requiring exact case.
caseInsensitive.help:
Off by default, because do-not-translate is usually case-sensitive —
"iPhone" must not become "Iphone". When enabled, a case-insensitive match
in the target satisfies the check.

limitations: The list of protected terms is configured programmatically; it is not exposed as a schema parameter.
A term is only checked when it occurs in the source as a whole word; substrings of larger words are ignored.

When a term is absent from the target the finding records the verbatim term to preserve but proposes no automatic replacement — a miss needs a human or a re-translation, not a blind substring substitution.
Requires a configured target locale; blocks without a target for that locale are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks that have a target for the configured locale; other parts pass through unchanged.
Findings are emitted in the "do-not-translate" category at critical severity.

examples.title: Case-insensitive preservation
examples.description: Accept a case-folded match for protected terms in the target.

entries.description: Convert the character set encoding of a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: Encoding Conversion
schema.description: Convert the character set encoding of a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

unescapeNCR.title: Unescape Numeric Character References
unescapeNCR.description: Un-escape numeric character references (e.g., á) when reading the input document.

unescapeCER.title: Unescape Character Entity References
unescapeCER.description: Un-escape HTML character entity references (e.g., á) when reading the input document.

unescapeJava.title: Unescape Java-style Notation
unescapeJava.description: Un-escape Java-style \uXXXX escape sequences when reading the input document.

escapeAll.title: Escape All Extended Characters
escapeAll.description: Escape all extended (non-ASCII) characters, not just those unsupported by the output encoding.

escapeNotation.title: Escape Notation
escapeNotation.description: Select the notation format used when escaping characters in the output (NCR, CER, Java-style, or user-defined).

userFormat.title: User-Defined Format
userFormat.description: Java format expression (e.g., %d, %x) used for escaping when user-defined notation is selected.

useBytes.title: Use Byte Values
useBytes.description: Use byte values of the output encoding instead of Unicode code points in the user-defined format.

BOMonUTF8.title: BOM on UTF-8 Output
BOMonUTF8.description: Add a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) at the beginning of UTF-8 output files.

reportUnsupported.title: Report Unsupported Characters
reportUnsupported.description: Log all characters not supported by the output encoding.

toolMeta.description: Convert the character set encoding of a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Converts the character set encoding of an input document and optionally updates encoding declarations in XML and HTML files. The step can un-escape various character reference notations (NCR, CER, Java-style) on input, and re-escape characters using configurable notation on output. It automatically detects and updates XML encoding declarations and HTML charset declarations in the first 1024 characters of each document.

BOMonUTF8.description: Add a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) at the beginning of UTF-8 output files.
BOMonUTF8.help:
Adds a **Byte-Order-Mark** (U+FEFF) at the beginning of UTF-8 output files. The BOM is a zero-width no-break space that some applications use to identify the file as UTF-8.

Only applies when the output encoding is UTF-8.
notes: The BOM is only relevant for UTF-8 output. Some applications (notably older Windows programs) expect a BOM to identify UTF-8 files, while others may display it as an unwanted character.

escapeAll.description: Escape all extended (non-ASCII) characters, not just those unsupported by the output encoding.
escapeAll.help:
Controls which characters are escaped in the output:

- **false** — Only characters not supported by the output encoding are escaped
- **true** — All extended characters (non-ASCII) are escaped, regardless of whether the output encoding supports them

escapeNotation.description: Select the notation format used when escaping characters in the output (NCR, CER, Java-style, or user-defined).
escapeNotation.help:
Selects the escape notation used when characters must be escaped in the output.

| Value | Notation | Example (`á`) |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| 0 | Uppercase hex NCR | `á` |
| 1 | Lowercase hex NCR | `á` |
| 2 | Decimal NCR | `ÿ` |
| 3 | Character entity reference | `á` |
| 4 | Uppercase Java-style | `\u00E1` |
| 5 | Lowercase Java-style | `\u00e1` |
| 6 | User-defined notation | Uses `userFormat` |

When using CER notation (3), if no entity is defined for a character, uppercase hex NCR is used as fallback.
escapeNotation.values: Integer `0`–`6` selecting the escape notation. `0` = uppercase hex NCR, `1` = lowercase hex NCR, `2` = decimal NCR, `3` = CER (with hex NCR fallback), `4` = uppercase Java, `5` = lowercase Java, `6` = user-defined format.
notes: When set to `3` (CER), characters without a corresponding entity definition fall back to uppercase hexadecimal NCR form.

reportUnsupported.description: Log all characters not supported by the output encoding.
reportUnsupported.help: Logs all characters that cannot be represented in the output encoding. Each unsupported character is listed in the pipeline log, helping identify potential data loss during encoding conversion.

unescapeCER.description: Un-escape HTML character entity references (e.g., á) when reading the input document.
unescapeCER.help:
Un-escapes standard **HTML character entity references** (CERs) when reading the input document. Converts named entities to their actual characters.

The five XML-reserved entities are **never** un-escaped regardless of this setting:
- `&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, `"`

These are preserved because they may need to remain escaped in HTML and XML documents.
notes: The entities `&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, and `"` are never un-escaped, as they may need to be preserved regardless of encoding in HTML and XML documents.

unescapeJava.description: Un-escape Java-style \uXXXX escape sequences when reading the input document.
unescapeJava.help:
Un-escapes **Java-style** `\uXXXX` escape sequences when reading the input document. Both uppercase and lowercase hex digits are recognized.

If disabled, Java-style escapes remain in their original form in the output.

unescapeNCR.description: Un-escape numeric character references (e.g., á) when reading the input document.
unescapeNCR.help:
Un-escapes all types of **numeric character references** (NCRs) when reading the input document. Converts decimal and hexadecimal forms to their actual characters.

- `ÿ` → `ÿ`
- `á` → `á`
- `&#e1;` → `á`

If disabled, NCRs in the input remain in their original form in the output.

useBytes.description: Use byte values of the output encoding instead of Unicode code points in the user-defined format.
useBytes.help:
Changes the values passed to the `userFormat` expression from **Unicode code points** to **byte values** of the output encoding.

This is useful for encodings like Shift-JIS where multi-byte sequences need to be expressed as individual byte escapes rather than single code point values.
examples: With format `\'%x` and Shift-JIS output: `日本語` → `\'93\'fa\'96\'7b\'8c\'ea` (byte values) instead of `\'65e5\'672c\'8a9e` (code points)

dependsOn.condition: must be 6 (user-defined) for this to take effect
the format expression that receives the byte values

userFormat.description: Java format expression (e.g., %d, %x) used for escaping when user-defined notation is selected.
userFormat.help:
A **Java format expression** that takes a single integer argument, used when `escapeNotation` is set to `6` (user-defined). The integer value is the Unicode code point of the character being escaped (or the byte values if `useBytes` is enabled).

Any valid `String.format()` pattern accepting one integer works. Use `%d` for decimal, `%x` for lowercase hex, `%X` for uppercase hex.
userFormat.values: Java `String.format()` expression with one integer placeholder. Common specifiers: `%d` (decimal), `%x` (lowercase hex), `%X` (uppercase hex).

dependsOn.condition: must be 6 (user-defined) for this to take effect
when true, values are byte values of the output encoding instead of Unicode code points

limitations: Encoding declaration detection operates on raw text (first 1024 chars) and is not XML/HTML-aware — it cannot distinguish declarations inside comments from real ones.
HTML charset insertion requires a `<head>` element or at minimum an `<html` string to anchor the insertion point.
The five XML-reserved character entities (`&`, `<`, `>`, `'`, `"`) are never un-escaped by the CER option.
processingNotes: Encoding declarations are detected and updated by scanning the **first 1024 characters** of each document — the routine is not XML/HTML-aware and cannot distinguish between normal code and comments.
XML documents are detected by encoding declarations, XML declarations, or `.xml` file extension — missing declarations may be inserted or added.
HTML documents are detected by charset declarations or file extensions starting with `.htm` — missing charset declarations may be inserted after `<head>` or `<html>`.
A document can be both XML and HTML and have both types of encoding/charset declarations updated.

examples.title: User-defined escape for Shift-JIS byte values
examples.description: Using a custom format expression with byte values to produce Shift-JIS byte-level escapes for CJK characters.
examples.config:
```yaml
escapeNotation: 6
userFormat: "'\'%x'"
useBytes: true
# Output encoding: Shift-JIS
```

examples.title: User-defined escape with decimal brackets
examples.description: Using a bracketed decimal format for characters unsupported by the output encoding.

entries.description: Normalize text through a target character encoding and unescape escape sequences on input.

applySource.title: Apply to Source
applySource.description: Apply encoding conversion to source text

applyTarget.title: Apply to Target
applyTarget.description: Apply encoding conversion to target text

escapeAll.title: Escape All Extended Characters
escapeAll.description: Escape all extended (non-ASCII) characters in output

reportUnsupported.title: Report Unsupported Characters
reportUnsupported.description: Report characters not supported by the target encoding

targetEncoding.title: Target Encoding
targetEncoding.description: Target encoding name (e.g. utf-8 or iso-8859-1 or shift-jis)

unescapeCER.title: Unescape Character Entity References
unescapeCER.description: Unescape HTML character entity references (e.g. á) when reading input

unescapeJava.title: Unescape Java-style Notation
unescapeJava.description: Unescape Java-style \\uXXXX escape sequences when reading input

unescapeNCR.title: Unescape Numeric Character References
unescapeNCR.description: Unescape numeric character references (e.g. á) when reading input

doc.overview:
The **Encoding Convert** tool validates and normalizes block text against a
target character encoding. It round-trips the text — encoding to the target
charset and decoding back to UTF-8 — which surfaces and replaces characters
the target cannot represent. The chosen encoding name is recorded in a block
property so a downstream writer can emit the document in that encoding.

Before converting, the tool can decode escape sequences found in the input —
numeric character references, HTML character entity references, and
Java-style `\uXXXX` escapes — so that the real characters are evaluated
against the target encoding. A target encoding name is required, and a target
locale is required when applying to the target.

applySource.help: When enabled, the source text is normalized through the target encoding.

applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the target text is normalized through the target encoding
for the configured locale. Only existing targets are touched.

dependsOn.condition: required when applyTarget is enabled

escapeAll.description: Escape all extended (non-ASCII) characters in output.
escapeAll.help:
When enabled, extended (non-ASCII) characters are escaped in the output
rather than emitted directly.

reportUnsupported.description: Report characters the target encoding cannot represent.
reportUnsupported.help:
When enabled, characters that the target encoding does not support are
reported, so unmappable content can be reviewed.

targetEncoding.description: The character encoding to normalize text through.
targetEncoding.help:
An IANA encoding name such as `utf-8`, `iso-8859-1`, or `shift-jis`. The
text is encoded to this charset and decoded back to UTF-8, replacing any
characters the encoding cannot represent. The name is stored on the block
for downstream writers. Unknown encodings are rejected.

targetLocale.help:
The locale whose target text is converted. Required when `applyTarget` is
enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this to take effect

unescapeCER.description: Decode HTML character entity references on input.
unescapeCER.help:
When enabled, named HTML character entity references such as `á`
are decoded to their characters before conversion.

unescapeJava.description: Decode Java-style escape sequences on input.
unescapeJava.help:
When enabled, Java-style `\uXXXX` escape sequences are decoded to their
characters before conversion.

unescapeNCR.description: Decode numeric character references on input.
unescapeNCR.help:
When enabled, numeric character references such as `á` or `á`
are decoded to their characters before the text is converted.

limitations: Conversion validates representability by round-tripping through the encoding; characters the encoding cannot represent are replaced.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
The target encoding name is written to a block property for downstream writers.

examples.title: Normalize targets to ISO-8859-1
examples.description: Validate target text against Latin-1 before writing.

examples.title: Decode entities, then normalize to Shift-JIS
examples.description: Unescape HTML entities in input and convert to a Japanese encoding.
examples.config:
targetEncoding: shift-jis
targetLocale: ja-JP
unescapeCER: true

entries.description: Classify the source text of each block as ASCII, UTF-8, or unknown.

doc.overview:
The **Encoding Detect** tool inspects the source text of every translatable
block and records its encoding characteristics on the block's properties: a
detected label (`ascii`, `utf-8`, or `unknown`) plus boolean flags for whether
the text is valid UTF-8 and whether it is ASCII-only. Text that is ASCII-only is
labelled `ascii`; otherwise valid UTF-8 is labelled `utf-8`; anything else is
`unknown`.

The tool takes no configuration. It is useful for spotting non-ASCII content
that may need particular care in downstream formats or fonts, and for
confirming that text is well-formed UTF-8.
limitations: Examines source text only; target translations are not classified.
Detection is limited to ASCII / UTF-8 validity, not a full charset-detection heuristic.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.

examples.title: Detect source encoding
examples.description: The tool takes no parameters; add it to a flow with an empty configuration.

entries.description: Applies Enrycher ITS annotations to the source content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Enrycher
schema.description: Applies Enrycher ITS annotations to the source content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

baseUrl.title: URL of the Enrycher Web service
baseUrl.description: Endpoint URL of the Enrycher Web service to use for disambiguation annotations

maxEvents.title: Events buffer
maxEvents.description: Number of events to store before sending a query

toolMeta.description: Applies Enrycher ITS annotations to the source content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Invokes the [Enrycher Web service](http://enrycher.ijs.si/) to provide ITS disambiguation annotations in the source text content. The step sends groups of source entries to the Enrycher service, receives ITS-annotated results, and maps them back to Okapi inline annotations usable by downstream steps. Implements the [ITS 2.0 Text Analysis data category](http://www.w3.org/TR/its20/#Disambiguation).

baseUrl.description: Endpoint URL of the Enrycher Web service to use for disambiguation annotations
baseUrl.help: The endpoint URL of the Enrycher Web service instance to use for disambiguation annotations. The default points to the public demo service hosted at IJS (`http://aidemo.ijs.si/mlw/`).
notes: The public Enrycher service (as of Aug-18-2013) supports only **English** and **Slovenian**.

maxEvents.help: Controls how many filter events are buffered before sending a batch query to the Enrycher Web service. Higher values send more segments at once, which generally **improves speed** but increases **memory usage**. Tune this based on document size and available memory.
maxEvents.values: Positive integer. Default `20`. Larger values improve throughput at the cost of higher memory consumption.
notes: The more events are buffered, the more memory is required. For large documents, consider reducing this value if you encounter memory issues.

limitations: This step is **BETA**.
The Enrycher Web service (as of Aug-18-2013) supports only **English** and **Slovenian**.
The amount of annotations added to the content can be very high depending on the type of text.
processingNotes: Events are buffered and sent in batches to the Web service rather than one at a time, controlled by the `maxEvents` parameter.
Annotations are mapped back to Okapi inline annotations that can be consumed by any downstream pipeline steps.
Requires both `source-language` and `target-language` to be set in the pipeline.

examples.title: Annotate with the default public service
examples.description: Use the public IJS demo endpoint with the default batch size. Suitable for small English or Slovenian documents where the demo service's language support is sufficient.

examples.title: Point at a private endpoint with a smaller batch
examples.description: Send to a self-hosted Enrycher instance and reduce the number of events buffered per request. Lowering `maxEvents` trades throughput for lower memory use, which helps with large documents.

entries.displayName: Exact Document Context Match Character Count
entries.description: Matches EXACT and comes from a repeated segment in the same document or a different version of the same document. See also EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Exact Document Context Match Character Count
schema.description: Matches EXACT and comes from a repeated segment in the same document or a different version of the same document. See also EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.displayName: Exact Document Context Match Character Count
toolMeta.description: Matches EXACT and comes from a repeated segment in the same document or a different version of the same document. See also EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies character counts for the text units whose leveraged top match was categorised as `EXACT_DOCUMENT_CONTEXT` — an exact match that originates from a repeated segment elsewhere in the same document (an internal repetition rather than a previous-version match; compare `EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION`). It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and, for each target segment or text container whose highest-ranked match is of this type, counts the characters in the corresponding source. The counts are stored as `EXACT_DOCUMENT_CONTEXT_CHARACTERS` metrics annotations on the segment, text container, and aggregate resources, and consumed downstream by the Scoping Report Step. This is the character-count variant; the word-count variant counts words instead of characters.
processingNotes: Operates on the categorised top match only — the first entry of each segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` must have match type `EXACT_DOCUMENT_CONTEXT`.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Where a category group ranks several matches, the `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked category and removes lower-ranked overlapping counts so totals are not double-counted.

toolMeta.description: Matches EXACT_TEXT_ONLY and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies word counts for the text units whose leveraged top match was categorised as `EXACT_TEXT_ONLY_UNIQUE_ID` — an `EXACT_TEXT_ONLY` match (text matches exactly, codes may differ) that additionally matches on a unique identifier, confirming it is the same segment by id. It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and, for each target segment or text container whose highest-ranked match is of this type, counts the words in the corresponding source. The counts are stored as `EXACT_TEXT_ONLY_UNIQUE_ID` metrics annotations on the segment, text container, and aggregate resources, and consumed downstream by the Scoping Report Step. This is the word-count variant; the character-count variant counts characters instead of words.
processingNotes: Operates on the categorised top match only — the first entry of each segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` must have match type `EXACT_TEXT_ONLY_UNIQUE_ID`.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Where a category group ranks several matches, the `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked category and removes lower-ranked overlapping counts so totals are not double-counted.

entries.displayName: Exact Unique Id Match Character Count
entries.description: Matches EXACT and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Exact Unique Id Match Character Count
schema.description: Matches EXACT and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.displayName: Exact Unique Id Match Character Count
toolMeta.description: Matches EXACT and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies character counts for the text units whose leveraged top match was categorised as `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` — an `EXACT` match (text and codes match exactly) that additionally matches on a unique identifier, confirming it is the same segment by id. It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and, for each target segment or text container whose highest-ranked match is of this type, counts the characters in the corresponding source. The counts are stored as `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID_CHARACTERS` metrics annotations on the segment, text container, and aggregate resources, and consumed downstream by the Scoping Report Step. This is the character-count variant; the word-count variant counts words instead of characters.
processingNotes: Operates on the categorised top match only — the first entry of each segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` must have match type `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID`.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Where a category group ranks several matches, the `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked category and removes lower-ranked overlapping counts so totals are not double-counted.

entries.displayName: Exact Unique Id Match Word Count
entries.description: Matches EXACT and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Exact Unique Id Match Word Count
schema.description: Matches EXACT and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.displayName: Exact Unique Id Match Word Count
toolMeta.description: Matches EXACT and matches a unique id. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies word counts for the text units whose leveraged top match was categorised as `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` — an `EXACT` match (text and codes match exactly) that additionally matches on a unique identifier, confirming it is the same segment by id. It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and, for each target segment or text container whose highest-ranked match is of this type, counts the words in the corresponding source. The counts are stored as `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` metrics annotations on the segment, text container, and aggregate resources, and consumed downstream by the Scoping Report Step. This is the word-count variant; the character-count variant counts characters instead of words.
processingNotes: Operates on the categorised top match only — the first entry of each segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` must have match type `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID`.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Where a category group ranks several matches, the `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked category and removes lower-ranked overlapping counts so totals are not double-counted.

entries.description: Run an external command-line program on block text and use its stdout as the result.

args.title: Arguments
args.description: Command arguments; use ${source} and ${target} as placeholders

sendAsStdin.title: Send as Stdin
sendAsStdin.description: Send text via stdin instead of command arguments

doc.overview:
The **External Command** tool pipes each block's text through an external
command-line program and captures the program's standard output as the new
text. It lets a pipeline reuse an existing CLI filter — a custom normalizer,
a spell-fixer, a transliterator — without writing a native tool.

Text can be sent to the command on standard input or substituted into the
argument list via the `${source}` and `${target}` placeholders. A command is
required, and a target locale is required when applying to the target. Each
invocation has a timeout; the exit code is recorded on the block, and a
non-zero exit leaves the text unchanged and records the error.

applySource.help: When enabled, the source text is sent through the command.

applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the target text is sent through the command for the
configured locale. Only existing targets are touched.

dependsOn.condition: required when applyTarget is enabled

args.help:
The argument list for the command. Use `${source}` and `${target}` as
placeholders; both are replaced with the text being processed.

command.help:
Path or name of the command-line program to execute for each block. Its
standard output replaces the block text.

sendAsStdin.description: Send the text on standard input rather than as an argument.
sendAsStdin.help:
When enabled, the text is written to the command's standard input. When
disabled, the text reaches the command only through the `${source}` /
`${target}` argument placeholders.

targetLocale.help: The locale whose target text is processed. Required when `applyTarget` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this to take effect

timeout.help:
Maximum time to wait for the command to finish for a single block. The
command is cancelled when the timeout elapses.

limitations: A non-zero exit code leaves the text unchanged and records the exit code and error message as block properties.
The command runs once per block per scope (source and/or target), which can be slow for large documents.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Trailing newlines are trimmed from the captured output.

examples.title: Pipe target text through a formatter
examples.description: Run an external program on the target, sending text on stdin.
examples.config:
command: my-formatter
applyTarget: true
targetLocale: fr-FR

examples.title: Pass text as an argument
examples.description: Substitute the text into the argument list instead of stdin.
examples.config:
command: transform
args: ["--text", "${source}"]
applySource: true
sendAsStdin: false

entries.description: Execute an external command line.

schema.title: External Command
schema.description: Execute an external command line.

timeout.title: Timeout
timeout.description: Timeout in seconds after which the command is cancelled (use -1 for no timeout)

toolMeta.description: Execute an external command line.

doc.overview: This step runs a given external command on a raw document. It takes a raw document as input, executes the specified command line (with variable substitution for paths and locale information), and sends the resulting raw document downstream. The command must reference `${inputPath}` and `${outputPath}` to read and write the document.

command.help:
The command line to execute. Must reference `${inputPath}` and `${outputPath}` so the step knows where to read and write the document.

Available variables (case-sensitive):

| Variable | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `${inputPath}` | Full path of the input document | |
| `${outputPath}` | Full path of the output document | |
| `${srcLangName}` | English name of source language | `de-ch` → `German` |
| `${trgLangName}` | English name of target language | `ja-jp` → `Japanese` |
| `${srcLang}` | Language code of source locale | `de-ch` → `de` |
| `${trgLang}` | Language code of target locale | `ja-jp` → `ja` |
| `${srcBCP47}` | BCP-47 tag of source locale | `de-CH` → `de-CH` |
| `${trgBCP47}` | BCP-47 tag of target locale | `ja-JP` → `ja-JP` |
| `${rootDir}` | Root directory for the project/batch | In Rainbow: the parameters folder |
command.values: A shell command string with `${variable}` placeholders. Variable names are **case-sensitive**.
notes: The command **must** use `${inputPath}` and `${outputPath}` to correctly read input and produce output.
Variable names are case-sensitive — `${inputpath}` will not work.
examples: `cmd /C "sort ${inputPath} /O ${outputPath}"` — Windows command that sorts the input file and writes the result to the output path

timeout.help: Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before cancelling it. Use `-1` to let the command run indefinitely.
timeout.values: Integer seconds, or `-1` for no timeout.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The step operates on **raw documents** — it does not parse or segment content. The external command receives the file as-is and must produce a complete output file.
The `${srcBCP47}` and `${trgBCP47}` variables were introduced in milestone M37.

examples.title: Sort file contents (Windows)
examples.description: Uses the Windows `sort` command to sort the lines of the input document and write the sorted result to the output path.

entries.description: Verifies a raw document can be extracted, merged, then extracted again and produces the same set of events during both extractions. Expects: raw document. Sends back: unmodified raw document.

schema.title: Extraction Verification
schema.description: Verifies a raw document can be extracted, merged, then extracted again and produces the same set of events during both extractions. Expects: raw document. Sends back: unmodified raw document.

compareSkeleton.title: Compare Skeleton
compareSkeleton.description: Include skeleton (non-extractable) parts of events in the verification comparison.

stepEnabled.title: Perform Extraction Verification
stepEnabled.description: Enable or disable the verification step; when disabled, the document passes through unmodified.

allEvents.title: Verify All Events
allEvents.description: Compare all filter events between extractions, not just text units.

limit.title: Maximum Warnings Per Document
limit.description: Maximum number of verification warnings allowed per document before stopping or moving on.

interrupt.title: Interrupt on Warning Limit
interrupt.description: Stop the pipeline when the maximum number of warnings is reached for a document.

monolingual.title: Monolingual Mode
monolingual.description: Operate in monolingual mode for source-only documents.

toolMeta.description: Verifies a raw document can be extracted, merged, then extracted again and produces the same set of events during both extractions. Expects: raw document. Sends back: unmodified raw document.

doc.overview: This step verifies that an input document can be safely round-tripped through the extraction and merging process. It extracts the raw document, merges it, then extracts again — comparing the events from both extractions to detect discrepancies. Any differences indicate potential problems with merging files back after translation. This is especially useful as a first pipeline step when working with newly developed or less-tested filters.

allEvents.description: Compare all filter events between extractions, not just text units.
allEvents.help:
Controls the scope of event comparison between the two extractions.

- **Enabled**: Every event generated by the filter is compared (text units, document parts, skeleton, etc.)
- **Disabled**: Only text unit events are compared

Verifying only text units is often sufficient — if the merge introduced errors, text units will typically differ between the two extractions. Disable this to reduce processing time on large documents.
notes: Verifying only text units is usually a reliable indicator of merge correctness, since merge errors typically manifest as text unit differences.

compareSkeleton.description: Include skeleton (non-extractable) parts of events in the verification comparison.
compareSkeleton.help:
Controls whether skeleton parts of verified events are included in the comparison. Skeletons represent the non-extractable portions of the input document (markup, formatting, structural elements surrounding translatable content).

Comparing skeletons provides a more thorough verification but may flag differences that don't affect translatable content.

interrupt.description: Stop the pipeline when the maximum number of warnings is reached for a document.
interrupt.help:
Controls whether the entire pipeline stops when the warning limit is reached for a document.

- **Enabled**: The pipeline is interrupted (halted) when the maximum number of warnings is reached
- **Disabled**: The step moves on to the next document after reaching the limit, allowing the pipeline to continue

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when the warning limit is reached

limit.description: Maximum number of verification warnings allowed per document before stopping or moving on.
limit.help:
Caps the number of warning messages generated per document. Once this limit is reached, the step either moves on to the next document or interrupts the pipeline (depending on the **interrupt** setting).

Keep this value reasonable to avoid flooding logs when a filter produces many mismatches.

stepEnabled.description: Enable or disable the verification step; when disabled, the document passes through unmodified.
stepEnabled.help:
Master switch for this step. When disabled, the input document passes through to the next step without any verification processing.

Useful for temporarily bypassing verification in a pipeline without removing the step.

limitations: Support for multilingual documents (XLIFF, TMX, PO, TTX, etc.) is **BETA**.
processingNotes: The step performs a full extract → merge → extract cycle internally, so processing time is roughly 3x a single extraction pass.
The raw document is sent to the next step **unmodified** — the verification is purely a side-effect check.
Requires both source and target language to be set in the pipeline for the merge step.

examples.title: Pipeline guard for untested filters
examples.description: Place the Extraction Verification step as the **first step** in a pipeline that uses a newly developed or less-tested filter. Any warnings indicate that the filter's merge implementation may produce incorrect output after translation.
examples.config:
```yaml
steps:
  - id: extraction-verification
    params:
      stepEnabled: true
      allEvents: true
      compareSkeleton: true
      limit: 10
      interrupt: true
```

examples.title: Lightweight text-unit-only check
examples.description: For faster processing on large document sets, verify only text units and allow the pipeline to continue past warnings.
examples.config:
```yaml
steps:
  - id: extraction-verification
    params:
      stepEnabled: true
      allEvents: false
      compareSkeleton: false
      limit: 20
      interrupt: false
```

entries.description: Combine filter events into a full document and pass it along as a raw document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: raw document or NO_OP if writing to a stream.

schema.title: Filter Events to Raw Document
schema.description: Combine filter events into a full document and pass it along as a raw document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: raw document or NO_OP if writing to a stream.

toolMeta.description: Combine filter events into a full document and pass it along as a raw document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: raw document or NO_OP if writing to a stream.

doc.overview: This step merges filter events back into their original document format, effectively reversing the extraction performed by the Raw Document to Filter Events step. It takes filter events as input and produces a raw document as output. It is commonly paired with the Raw Document to Filter Events step to form a minimal extract-then-merge pipeline.
limitations: None known.
processingNotes: This step has no user-configurable parameters — all parameters are provided by the calling application.
If writing to a stream, the step sends `NO_OP` instead of a raw document.
Requires both source and target language to be set in the pipeline.

examples.title: Minimal extract-merge pipeline
examples.description: The simplest pipeline that extracts filter events from a document and immediately merges them back. This is useful as a roundtrip test to verify that a filter correctly reconstructs the original document format.
examples.config:
```
Raw Document to Filter Events Step
  +
Filter Events to Raw Document Step
```

entries.description: Write out filter events into a document or stream.

schema.title: Filter Events Writer
schema.description: Write out filter events into a document or stream.

toolMeta.description: Write out filter events into a document or stream.

doc.overview: The Filter Events Writer step turns a stream of filter events back into an output document or stream using a filter writer. It is the write counterpart of extraction: as start-document, text-unit, group, and other events flow past, the writer reconstructs the document in the original format, applying any translations carried on the events. Each event and its resource are forwarded to the next step so the pipeline can continue after the document has been written.
processingNotes: On the start-document event the step obtains a filter writer — either a writer supplied to it directly, or one created from the filter configuration mapper and configuration id — and sets its target locale and output encoding.

Output is directed to a supplied output stream when present, otherwise to the path from the output URI; when writing to an existing file the step may prompt before overwriting unless prompting is suppressed by the execution context.
All event types between start-document and end-document are passed to the filter writer; on the end-document event the writer is closed.
Filter configuration, target locale, output encoding, output URI, and execution context are supplied by the pipeline rather than configured as step parameters.

entries.description: Converts the output of a filter into a specified file format. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Format Conversion
schema.description: Converts the output of a filter into a specified file format. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

singleOutput.title: Single Output Document
singleOutput.description: Generates one combined output document for all input documents instead of one per input

autoExtensions.title: Append Format Extension
autoExtensions.description: Output paths are the input paths with the new format extension appended (e.g., .tmx, .po)

outputPath.title: Output path
outputPath.description: Full path of the single output document to generate

targetStyle.title: Target content
targetStyle.description: Type of content to put in the target

formatOptions.title: Format Options
formatOptions.description: Format-specific options for the selected output format writer.

useGenericCodes.title: Generic Inline Codes
useGenericCodes.description: Replaces original inline codes with generic placeholders like <N>, </N>, and <N/>

skipEntriesWithoutText.title: Skip Empty Entries
skipEntriesWithoutText.description: Suppresses output of entries that contain no text (empty or inline-codes only)

approvedEntriesOnly.title: Approved Entries Only
approvedEntriesOnly.description: Restricts output to entries with the target approved property set to yes

overwriteSameSource.title: Overwrite Same Source
overwriteSameSource.description: For Pensieve TM output, replaces existing entries when the source text matches

toolMeta.description: Converts the output of a filter into a specified file format. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Converts input documents from one format to another using extracted filter events. Supports output to PO, TMX, tab-delimited, Pensieve TM, parallel corpus files, and Word table (RTF) formats. The generated documents are not meant to be merged back into their original format — use the Raw Document to Filter Events / Filter Events to Raw Document step pair for round-trip scenarios.

approvedEntriesOnly.description: Restricts output to entries with the target approved property set to yes
approvedEntriesOnly.help: Restricts output to entries where the target `approved` property is set to `yes`. For example, "fuzzy" entries in a PO file have this property set to `no`.
notes: Entries that lack the `approved` property entirely are treated as **non-approved** and will be excluded.

autoExtensions.description: Output paths are the input paths with the new format extension appended (e.g., .tmx, .po)
autoExtensions.help: Output files use the same path as the input file with an additional format extension appended (e.g., `.po`, `.tmx`). When disabled, the output paths are determined by the calling application.

dependsOn.condition: must be false (disabled) for this option to be available

outputFormat.help:
Select the output file format. Available formats:

- **PO File** — Gettext PO format, UTF-8 encoded
- **TMX Document** — Translation Memory eXchange format
- **Tab-Delimited Table** — UTF-8 tab-delimited text; source in first column, target in second; literal tabs escaped as `\t`
- **Pensieve TM** — Pensieve TM database (output path must be a directory)
- **Parallel Corpus Files** — Two plain text files (inline codes stripped), one entry per line, locale code appended to filename. Used by systems like Moses MT
- **Word Table** — RTF table with generic inline codes; one entry per row. For TTX input, match percentage appears in the Type column
outputFormat.values: String value identifying the format: `tmx`, `po`, etc.

outputPath.help: Full path of the single output document to generate. For a **Pensieve TM**, this must be a directory rather than a file path. The variable `${rootDir}` can be used in the path.
outputPath.values: File path string. Supports the `${rootDir}` variable.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this field to be active

overwriteSameSource.description: For Pensieve TM output, replaces existing entries when the source text matches
overwriteSameSource.help: When importing into a Pensieve TM, if an entry has the same source text as one or more existing TM entries, all matching entries are replaced by the new one. Leave disabled to allow multiple different translations for the same source text.

dependsOn.condition: only applicable when output format is Pensieve TM

singleOutput.description: Generates one combined output document for all input documents instead of one per input
singleOutput.help: Generates one combined output document for all input documents. When disabled, each input document produces its own separate output file.
notes: When enabled, the **Output path** field becomes active and must be specified.

skipEntriesWithoutText.description: Suppresses output of entries that contain no text (empty or inline-codes only)
skipEntriesWithoutText.help: Suppresses output of entries that have no actual text content — i.e., entries that are empty or contain only inline codes with no surrounding text.

targetStyle.help: Controls what content is placed in the target side of the output. The integer value selects between different target content strategies (e.g., source copied to target, target translation, empty target).

useGenericCodes.description: Replaces original inline codes with generic placeholders like <N>, </N>, and <N/>
useGenericCodes.help: Replaces original inline codes with generic placeholders in the form `<N>`, `</N>`, and `<N/>` where `N` is the code's numeric identifier. When disabled, inline codes are output in their original format (except for references).
notes: If the output format is **TMX**, this option is ignored — inline codes always use TMX format regardless of this setting.

limitations: None known (per Okapi wiki).
processingNotes: Documents generated by this step are **not** round-trippable — they cannot be merged back into the original format. For round-trip conversion, use the **Raw Document to Filter Events** step followed by the **Filter Events to Raw Document** step.
Requires both source and target language to be set in the pipeline context.
For Pensieve TM output, the output path must point to a directory, not a file.

examples.title: Convert to single TMX file
examples.description: Merge all input documents into a single TMX output using `${rootDir}` variable for the output path.
examples.config:
```yaml
singleOutput: true
outputFormat: tmx
outputPath: "${rootDir}/output/combined.tmx"
skipEntriesWithoutText: true
```

examples.title: Convert to PO with auto extensions
examples.description: Generate one PO file per input document, with `.po` appended to the original filename. Only approved entries are included.
examples.config:
```yaml
singleOutput: false
autoExtensions: true
outputFormat: po
approvedEntriesOnly: true
```

examples.title: Export to parallel corpus for MT training
examples.description: Create parallel corpus files suitable for Moses MT or similar systems. Inline codes are automatically stripped. Each entry appears on its own line with locale-specific filenames.
examples.config:
```yaml
singleOutput: false
autoExtensions: true
outputFormat: corpus
skipEntriesWithoutText: true
```

entries.description: Convert between half-width and full-width Latin characters and punctuation for CJK content.

includeKatakana.title: Include Katakana
includeKatakana.description: Also convert Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation to half-width forms

includeLLS.title: Include Letter-Like Symbols
includeLLS.description: Also convert characters from the Letter-Like Symbols block to character sequences

includeSLA.title: Include Squared Latin Abbreviations
includeSLA.description: Also convert Squared Latin Abbreviations from the CJK Compatibility block to non-CJK character sequences

mode.title: Conversion Mode
mode.description: Conversion direction between half-width and full-width characters

doc.overview:
The **Full Width Convert** tool maps characters between their half-width and
full-width forms. In CJK text, ASCII letters, digits, and punctuation are
often written in full-width forms so they align visually with ideographs;
this tool converts in either direction. The core mapping covers ASCII
printable characters (`!`–`~`, full-width range `！`–`～`) and the space
(regular space ↔ ideographic space).

applySource.description: Apply the conversion to source text.
applySource.help: When enabled, the source text is converted.

applyTarget.description: Apply the conversion to target text.
applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the target text is converted for the configured locale.
Only existing targets are touched.

dependsOn.condition: required when applyTarget is enabled

includeKatakana.help:
When enabled, Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation are also
converted to their half-width forms.

includeLLS.help:
When enabled, characters from the Letter-Like Symbols block are also
converted to character sequences.

includeSLA.help:
When enabled, Squared Latin Abbreviations from the CJK Compatibility
block are also converted to non-CJK character sequences.

mode.help:
`to-half` converts full-width characters to their half-width (ASCII)
forms; `to-full` converts half-width characters to full-width forms.

targetLocale.help: The locale whose target text is converted. Required when `applyTarget` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this to take effect

processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
The core mapping covers ASCII printable characters and the space; the extended-block toggles add further character ranges.

examples.title: Convert full-width to half-width
examples.description: Normalize full-width Latin characters in the target to ASCII.
examples.config:
mode: to-half
applyTarget: true
targetLocale: ja-JP

examples.title: Convert half-width to full-width
examples.description: Render ASCII characters in their full-width forms.
examples.config:
mode: to-full
applyTarget: true
targetLocale: ja-JP

entries.description: Convert the text units content of a document to or from full-width characters (zenkaku). Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Full-Width Conversion
schema.description: Convert the text units content of a document to or from full-width characters (zenkaku). Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toHalfWidth.title: Convert to Half-Width
toHalfWidth.description: Convert full-width characters to half-width or ASCII equivalents; when false, converts in the opposite direction.

asciiOnly.title: ASCII Only
asciiOnly.description: When converting to full-width, restrict conversion to ASCII characters only.

katakanaOnly.title: Katakana Only
katakanaOnly.description: When converting to full-width, restrict conversion to Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation only.

includeSLA.title: Include Squared Latin Abbreviations
includeSLA.description: Also convert Squared Latin Abbreviations from the CJK Compatibility block to non-CJK character sequences.

includeLLS.title: Include Letter-Like Symbols
includeLLS.description: Also convert characters from the Letter-Like Symbols block to character sequences.

includeKatakana.title: Include Katakana
includeKatakana.description: Also convert Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation to half-width forms.

normalizeOutput.title: Normalize Output (NFC)
normalizeOutput.description: Apply Unicode NFC normalization to the output text after conversions.

toolMeta.description: Convert the text units content of a document to or from full-width characters (zenkaku). Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Converts characters in text units between full-width (zenkaku) and half-width (hankaku) forms, targeting Asian character sets that historically have dual display forms. The conversion operates on the target locale text of text units; if no target text exists, source text is copied to the target before processing. Supports both directions: full-width → half-width/ASCII and half-width/ASCII → full-width, with fine-grained control over which character classes are affected.

asciiOnly.description: When converting to full-width, restrict conversion to ASCII characters only.
asciiOnly.help:
When converting **to full-width** (`toHalfWidth` = false), restricts conversion to ASCII characters only. Half-width Katakana and other half-width characters are left unchanged.

Useful when you want full-width alphanumeric rendering but need to preserve half-width Katakana as-is.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when toHalfWidth is false (converting to full-width)

includeKatakana.description: Also convert Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation to half-width forms.
includeKatakana.help:
When converting **to half-width** (`toHalfWidth` = true), also converts Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation (`。、「」`, etc.) into their half-width forms.

Off by default to facilitate normalizing modern Japanese text: typically full-width alphanumerics should be normalized to half-width ASCII, while Katakana should remain full-width.
notes: Off by default because in modern Japanese text normalization, Katakana is typically kept full-width while only alphanumeric characters are converted to half-width.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when toHalfWidth is true (converting to half-width)

includeLLS.description: Also convert characters from the Letter-Like Symbols block to character sequences.
includeLLS.help:
When converting **to half-width** (`toHalfWidth` = true), also converts several characters from the Letter-Like Symbols block to character sequences:

| Symbol | Result |
|--------|--------|
| U+2100 | `a/c` |
| U+2101 | `a/s` |
| U+2105 | `c/o` |
| U+2103 | `°C` |
| U+2109 | `°F` |
| U+2116 | `No` |
| U+212A | `K` |
| U+212B | `Å` |

These are additional conversions beyond the standard full-width → half-width mapping.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when toHalfWidth is true (converting to half-width)

includeSLA.description: Also convert Squared Latin Abbreviations from the CJK Compatibility block to non-CJK character sequences.
includeSLA.help:
When converting **to half-width** (`toHalfWidth` = true), also converts Squared Latin Abbreviations from the CJK Compatibility block (U+3300–U+33FF) into sequences of non-CJK characters.

Example: `㏀` (U+33C0) → `kΩ` (U+006B, U+03A9).

These are additional conversions beyond the standard full-width → half-width mapping.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when toHalfWidth is true (converting to half-width)

katakanaOnly.description: When converting to full-width, restrict conversion to Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation only.
katakanaOnly.help:
When converting **to full-width** (`toHalfWidth` = false), restricts conversion to Japanese Katakana and associated punctuation (`｡､｢｣`, etc.) only. Alphanumeric characters remain half-width.

Useful for normalizing Japanese text where half-width Katakana should become full-width but ASCII/alphanumeric characters should stay as-is.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when toHalfWidth is false (converting to full-width)

normalizeOutput.description: Apply Unicode NFC normalization to the output text after conversions.
normalizeOutput.help:
Applies Unicode NFC normalization to the output text after conversions are made.

Converting half-width Katakana to full-width can produce decomposed character sequences. For example, `ﾌﾟ` (U+FF8C U+FF9F) converts to `プ` (U+30D5 U+309A, decomposed), but NFC normalization produces the standard precomposed form `プ` (U+30D7).

Enabled by default to ensure consistent Unicode representation.
notes: Only applied when at least one conversion is made. If no characters are converted, the text passes through unchanged.

toHalfWidth.description: Convert full-width characters to half-width or ASCII equivalents; when false, converts in the opposite direction.
toHalfWidth.help:
Controls the conversion direction.

- **true** (default): Converts full-width characters to half-width or ASCII equivalents. E.g., `Ｑ` (U+FF31) → `Q` (U+0051), `サ` (U+30B5) → `ｻ` (U+FF7B).
- **false**: Converts half-width and ASCII characters to full-width equivalents. E.g., `Q` (U+0051) → `Ｑ` (U+FF31), `ｻ` (U+FF7B) → `サ` (U+30B5).

The `asciiOnly`, `katakanaOnly`, `includeSLA`, `includeLLS`, and `includeKatakana` options modify behavior differently depending on this direction setting.
examples: `true` — Normalize Japanese text by converting full-width alphanumerics to standard ASCII
`false` — Convert ASCII characters to full-width for CJK-style typesetting

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Operates on the target locale text of text units. If no target text exists, the source text is copied to the target before processing.
The step handles two distinct conversion directions controlled by `toHalfWidth`, each with its own set of sub-options.
Unicode NFC normalization (when enabled) only runs if at least one character conversion was made.

examples.title: Normalize Japanese full-width alphanumerics to half-width
examples.description: Convert full-width ASCII-range characters to standard half-width while keeping Katakana full-width — a common normalization for modern Japanese text.
examples.config:
```yaml
toHalfWidth: true
includeKatakana: false
normalizeOutput: true
```

examples.title: Convert ASCII to full-width for CJK typesetting
examples.description: Convert ASCII characters to full-width equivalents while leaving Katakana unchanged.

examples.title: Normalize half-width Katakana to full-width
examples.description: Convert half-width Katakana to full-width while keeping alphanumerics as-is, with NFC normalization for consistent output.
examples.config:
```yaml
toHalfWidth: false
katakanaOnly: true
normalizeOutput: true
```

entries.description: Matches both text and/or codes partially. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Fuzzy Match Character Count
schema.description: Matches both text and/or codes partially. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Matches both text and/or codes partially. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Counts the characters in text units whose top alternate translation is a plain fuzzy match — a `FUZZY` match type, where the leveraged result matches the source text and/or its inline codes only partially. The count is recorded as a character-count metric in a `MetricsAnnotation` so that scoping and pricing steps can report leveraged volume by match category. It is the character-counting counterpart of the Fuzzy Match Word Count Step, differing only in that it tallies characters rather than words.
processingNotes: Operates per segment: it inspects the first (top-ranked) `AltTranslation` on each target segment's `AltTranslationsAnnotation` and counts a segment only when that match type is exactly `FUZZY`.
Counting is performed on the **source** text, not the target — the character total reflects the source volume that was leveraged at this match level.
Uses the GMX-V character counter; the only behavioural difference from the word-count variant is words versus characters.
Counts only translatable text units; empty text units and non-translatable ones are skipped.

Results are stored as annotations on filter events and aggregated to the configured levels (text unit, group, document, sub-document, batch item, batch); the step produces no output document of its own.

Belongs to the `OKAPI_CHARACTER_COUNTS` category group. When several categories in the same group match a segment, the `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked one — `FUZZY` outranks `FUZZY_REPAIRED`, `PHRASE_ASSEMBLED`, `MT`, and `CONCORDANCE`, but is outranked by the unique-id, previous-version, and all exact categories.

entries.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been fuzzy matched against a leveraged translation memory database. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Fuzzy Match Character Count
schema.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been fuzzy matched against a leveraged translation memory database. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been fuzzy matched against a leveraged translation memory database. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `FuzzyMatchedCharacterCount` category — the character count for text units fuzzy matched against a leveraged translation memory database. It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and counts a target segment or text container only when its top match falls into the fuzzy band: `FUZZY`, `FUZZY_UNIQUE_ID`, `FUZZY_PREVIOUS_VERSION`, `FUZZY_REPAIRED`, or one of the text-only exact types (`EXACT_TEXT_ONLY`, `EXACT_TEXT_ONLY_UNIQUE_ID`, `EXACT_TEXT_ONLY_PREVIOUS_VERSION`, `EXACT_REPAIRED`). Characters are counted in the corresponding source in canonical form — whitespace and punctuation are excluded (hyphens and apostrophes inside words are kept) — and stored as GMX-V metrics annotations for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the character-count counterpart of the fuzzy-match word-count step.
processingNotes: Only the top-ranked alternate translation of each segment is examined; it qualifies when its match type is one of the fuzzy or text-only/repaired-exact types.
Counts are computed over the source content of qualifying units; only translatable text units are counted, and a target must be present.
Character counting excludes whitespace and punctuation per GMX-V, but retains hyphens and apostrophes that occur inside a word.

Counts only translatable text units; empty text units and non-translatable ones are skipped.

Results are stored as annotations on filter events and aggregated to the configured levels (text unit, group, document, sub-document, batch item, batch); the step produces no output document of its own.

Belongs to the `OKAPI_CHARACTER_COUNTS` category group. The `CategoryResolver` keeps only the highest-ranked matching category per segment — `FUZZY_PREVIOUS_VERSION` ranks above plain `FUZZY` but below `FUZZY_UNIQUE_ID` and all exact categories.

entries.description: Compare source and target for general quality. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: General Checker
schema.description: Compare source and target for general quality. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

leadingWS.title: Warn if a target entry has a difference in leading white spaces
leadingWS.description: Flag text units where leading whitespace differs between source and target.

trailingWS.title: Warn if a target entry has a difference in trailing white spaces
trailingWS.description: Flag text units where trailing whitespace differs between source and target.

emptyTarget.title: Warn if a target segment is empty when its source is not empty
emptyTarget.description: Flag segments where the target is empty while the source is not.

emptySource.title: Warn if a target segment is not empty when its source is empty
emptySource.description: Flag segments where the target is not empty while the source is empty.

targetSameAsSource.title: Warn if a target segment is the same as its source (for segments with text)
targetSameAsSource.description: Flag segments where target is identical to source.

targetSameAsSourceForSameLanguage.title: Verify for same language family (i.e. en vs. en-GB, or fr-FR vs. fr-CA)
targetSameAsSourceForSameLanguage.description: Flag segments where target equals source when source and target languages are identical.

targetSameAsSourceWithCodes.title: Include the codes in the comparison
targetSameAsSourceWithCodes.description: Include inline codes when comparing source and target for same-as-source check.

doubledWord.title: Warn on doubled words (e.g. "is is" in "This is is an example")
doubledWord.description: Flag target segments with sequential repetition of the same word.

doubledWordExceptions.title: Exceptions (words separated by ';' e.g. "vous;nous")
doubledWordExceptions.description: Semicolon-separated list of words allowed to be repeated sequentially.

toolMeta.description: Compare source and target for general quality. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: The General Checker step compares the source and target of each text unit and annotates segment-level issues that are not specific to inline codes, length, patterns, or character sets. It covers leading and trailing whitespace differences, empty source or empty target, target identical to source, and doubled (consecutively repeated) words in the target. It expects filter events and passes them through unchanged, attaching the issues it finds to the resources for a downstream consumer to report. It is one of the granular quality checks that the aggregate Quality Check step combines into a single pass.
processingNotes: Operates on filter events; events are forwarded unchanged and the detected issues are accumulated per document.
Delegates to the verification library's `GeneralChecker`; its step parameters are a subset of the full Quality Check / CheckMate configuration.

By default all of its checks are enabled: leading whitespace, trailing whitespace, empty target, empty source, target-same-as-source (including the same-language and with-codes variants), and doubled words.
The doubled-word check ships with the default exception list `sie;vous;nous`, allowing those words to repeat without being flagged.
Requires source and target locales; these are supplied by the pipeline rather than configured on the step.

examples.title: Catch empty targets, untranslated segments, and doubled words
examples.description: Enables the core verifications. Empty or whitespace-only targets and targets identical to their source are flagged, and accidental doubled words are reported — except the listed function words (here French `sie`/`vous`/`nous`) that may legitimately repeat.
examples.config:
```yaml
emptyTarget: true
targetSameAsSource: true
doubledWord: true
doubledWordExceptions: "sie;vous;nous"
```

entries.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been identified as containing only alphanumeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: GMX Alphanumeric Only Character Count
schema.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been identified as containing only alphanumeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been identified as containing only alphanumeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `AlphanumericOnlyTextUnitCharacterCount` category — the character count for text units that contain only alphanumeric words. Each text unit's source is tokenised and a unit qualifies only when every non-whitespace token is one of the recognised alphanumeric kinds (`ABBREVIATION`, `EMAIL`, `INTERNET`, `COMPANY`, `EMOTICON`, `MARKUP`); units mixing in any other token type contribute zero. Characters are counted in canonical form — whitespace and punctuation are excluded (hyphens and apostrophes inside words are kept) — and stored as GMX-V metrics annotations for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the character-count counterpart of the alphanumeric-only word-count step.
processingNotes: A text unit is counted only if all of its tokens are alphanumeric-kind tokens plus whitespace; otherwise the unit yields zero.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Character counting excludes whitespace and punctuation per GMX-V, but retains hyphens and apostrophes that occur inside a word.

entries.description: An accumulation of the word count for text units that have been identified as containing only alphanumeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: GMX Alphanumeric Only Word Count
schema.description: An accumulation of the word count for text units that have been identified as containing only alphanumeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: An accumulation of the word count for text units that have been identified as containing only alphanumeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `AlphanumericOnlyTextUnitWordCount` category — the word count for text units that contain only alphanumeric words. Each text unit's source is tokenised and a unit qualifies only when every non-whitespace token is one of the recognised alphanumeric kinds (`ABBREVIATION`, `EMAIL`, `INTERNET`, `COMPANY`, `EMOTICON`, `MARKUP`); units mixing in any other token type contribute zero. Counts are stored as GMX-V metrics annotations on segments, text containers, and aggregate resources for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the word-count variant; a companion step counts characters for the same category.
processingNotes: A text unit is counted only if all of its tokens are alphanumeric-kind tokens plus whitespace; otherwise the unit yields zero.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
For locales the GMX-V spec treats as logographic, the word count is derived from the character count divided by the per-language factor rather than from token counts.

entries.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been matched unambiguously with a prior translation and thus require no translator input. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been matched unambiguously with a prior translation and thus require no translator input. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been matched unambiguously with a prior translation and thus require no translator input. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `ExactMatchedCharacterCount` category — the character count for text units matched unambiguously with a prior translation and thus requiring no translator input. It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and counts a target segment or text container only when its top match is of type `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` or `EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION`, counting characters in the corresponding source. Characters are counted in canonical form — whitespace and punctuation are excluded (hyphens and apostrophes inside words are kept) — and stored as GMX-V metrics annotations for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the character-count counterpart of the exact-matched word-count step.
processingNotes: Only the top-ranked alternate translation of each segment is examined; it qualifies when its match type is `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` or `EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION`.
Counts are computed over the source content of qualifying units; only translatable text units are counted, and a target must be present.
Character counting excludes whitespace and punctuation per GMX-V, but retains hyphens and apostrophes that occur inside a word.

It reads the `AltTranslationsAnnotation` left by a leveraging step and counts a target segment or text container only when its top match is of type `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` or `EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION`, counting the words in the corresponding source. Counts are stored as GMX-V metrics annotations on segments, targets, and aggregate resources for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the word-count variant; a companion step counts characters for the same category.
processingNotes: Only the top-ranked alternate translation of each segment is examined; it qualifies when its match type is `EXACT_UNIQUE_ID` or `EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION`.
Counts are computed over the source content of qualifying units; only translatable text units are counted, and a target must be present.
Requires a prior leveraging step to have populated the alternate-translation annotations with categorised match types.

entries.description: An accumulation of the character count from measurement-only text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: GMX Measurement Only Character Count
schema.description: An accumulation of the character count from measurement-only text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: An accumulation of the character count from measurement-only text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `MeasurementOnlyTextUnitCharacterCount` category — the character count from measurement-only text units. Each text unit's source is tokenised and a unit qualifies as measurement-only based on its `DATE`, `TIME`, and `CURRENCY` tokens; units that also contain other content contribute zero, after which characters are counted over the `DATE`, `TIME`, and `CURRENCY` tokens. Characters are counted in canonical form — whitespace and punctuation are excluded (hyphens and apostrophes inside words are kept) — and stored as GMX-V metrics annotations for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the character-count counterpart of the measurement-only word-count step.
processingNotes: Qualification keys on the `DATE`, `TIME`, and `CURRENCY` token kinds; a unit containing other non-whitespace content yields zero.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Character counting excludes whitespace and punctuation per GMX-V, but retains hyphens and apostrophes that occur inside a word.

entries.description: An accumulation of the word count from measurement-only text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: GMX Measurement Only Word Count
schema.description: An accumulation of the word count from measurement-only text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: An accumulation of the word count from measurement-only text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `MeasurementOnlyTextUnitWordCount` category — the word count from measurement-only text units. Each text unit's source is tokenised and a unit qualifies as measurement-only based on its `DATE`, `TIME`, and `CURRENCY` tokens; units that also contain other content contribute zero, after which the count is taken over the `DATE`, `TIME`, and `CURRENCY` tokens. Counts are stored as GMX-V metrics annotations on segments, text containers, and aggregate resources for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the word-count variant; a companion step counts characters for the same category.
processingNotes: Qualification keys on the `DATE`, `TIME`, and `CURRENCY` token kinds; a unit containing other non-whitespace content yields zero.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
For locales the GMX-V spec treats as logographic, the word count is derived from the character count divided by the per-language factor rather than from token counts.

schema.description: An accumulation of the character count for text units that have been identified as containing only numeric words. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Tallies the [GMX-V 2.0](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) `NumericOnlyTextUnitCharacterCount` category — the character count for text units that contain only numeric words. Each text unit's source is tokenised and a unit qualifies only when every non-whitespace token is a `NUMBER` token; units containing any other token type contribute zero. Characters are counted in canonical form — whitespace and punctuation are excluded (hyphens and apostrophes inside words are kept) — and stored as GMX-V metrics annotations for downstream consumption (for example by the Scoping Report Step). This is the character-count counterpart of the numeric-only word-count step.
processingNotes: A text unit is counted only if all of its non-whitespace tokens are `NUMBER` tokens; otherwise the unit yields zero.
Counts are computed over the source content; only translatable text units are counted.
Character counting excludes whitespace and punctuation per GMX-V, but retains hyphens and apostrophes that occur inside a word.
