message.help: Optional message or note to include with the translation package. Can be used to provide instructions or context for translators receiving the kit.

outputManifest.description: Generate a manifest file required for merging the translated kit back into the original format.
outputManifest.help: Controls whether a manifest file is generated with the package. The manifest is required by the Rainbow Translation Kit Merging Step to correctly merge translated files back into the original format.
notes: Disabling the manifest will prevent the package from being merged back using the Rainbow Translation Kit Merging Step.

packageDirectory.description: Root directory where the translation package will be created; supports ${inputRootDir} and locale variables.
packageDirectory.help:
Root directory where the translation package will be created. The package folder (named by `packageName`) is placed inside this directory.

Supports variable substitution:
- `${rootDir}` — pipeline root directory
- `${inputRootDir}` — root directory of the input files
- Locale variables: `${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}`, etc.
packageDirectory.values: Directory path supporting `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables (`${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}`, etc.).

packageName.description: Name of the package directory to create inside the output root.
packageName.help: The name of the package directory created inside the output root directory. This becomes the folder name containing all extracted translation files, manifests, and support material.

sendOutput.description: Send prepared files as raw documents to the next pipeline step instead of the original filter events.
sendOutput.help:
Sends the prepared (extracted) files to the next pipeline step as raw documents, instead of forwarding the original filter events.

This allows downstream steps to process the extracted translation files (e.g., XLIFF documents) rather than the original input.
notes: This option has no effect if the selected package format does not support producing one output file per input file.

supportFiles.description: Additional files to include in the package as support material for translators.
supportFiles.help:
List of additional files to include in the translation package as support material. Each item specifies an origin path and a destination within the package.

**Origin** paths support `*` and `?` wildcards, plus variables (`${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, locale variables).

**Destination** paths must start with `/` or `\` (relative to package root). Use `<same>` to keep the original filename. Renaming is possible when the origin has no wildcards.
supportFiles.values: Semicolon-separated list of origin→destination file mappings. Origins support `*` and `?` wildcards.
examples: Origin: `C:\myProj\myExtra.*` — copies all files starting with `myExtra.`
Destination: `/<same>` — copies to package root with original filename
Destination: `/ref/glossary.txt` — copies and renames into a `ref` subfolder

writerClass.description: Java class of the package writer that determines the translation kit output format (e.g., XLIFF, PO, OmegaT).
writerClass.help:
Fully qualified Java class name of the package writer that determines the output format of the translation kit.

Available writers correspond to the package formats:
- **Generic XLIFF** — `net.sf.okapi.steps.rainbowkit.xliff.XLIFFPackageWriter`
- **PO Package** — extracts to PO files
- **Original with RTF** — adds an RTF layer to original files
- **XLIFF with RTF** — extracts to XLIFF then adds RTF layer
- **OmegaT Project** — creates a new OmegaT project
- **Transifex Project** — uploads to a Transifex project
- **ONTRAM XINI** — extracts to ONTRAM XINI format
- **Translation Table** — extracts to tab-delimited files
- **XLIFF 2.0** — extracts to XLIFF v2 documents

Each writer may have its own configuration options.
notes: Not all package formats support per-file output. The `sendOutput` option has no effect for formats that don't produce one output file per input file.

limitations: If this step is used in a pipeline that modifies the input file (e.g., with a Search and Replace Step), the "original" files saved in the package may not be usable for merging back translated files. Modifications like search and replace may change inline code counts, causing mismatches during merge.
Not all package formats support one output file per input file, which limits the `sendOutput` option.
processingNotes: The step expects filter events as input — typically preceded by a Raw Document to Filter Events Step.
Extracted packages can be merged back using the Rainbow Translation Kit Merging Step, provided the manifest was generated.
The step can be combined with segmentation and leveraging steps to pre-translate content before kit creation.

examples.title: Simple extraction pipeline
examples.description: Minimal pipeline that extracts documents into a Rainbow translation kit using the default XLIFF format.
examples.config:
```yaml
pipeline:
  - step: raw-document-to-filter-events
  - step: extraction
    params:
      packageName: pack1
      packageDirectory: ${inputRootDir}
```

examples.title: Pre-translated kit with segmentation and leveraging
examples.description: A more complete pipeline that segments text and attempts pre-translation using translation memory before creating the kit.
examples.config:
```yaml
pipeline:
  - step: raw-document-to-filter-events
  - step: segmentation
  - step: leveraging  # First TM
  - step: leveraging  # Second TM
  - step: extraction
    params:
      packageName: project-kit
      packageDirectory: ${inputRootDir}/output
      createZip: true
```

entries.description: Post-process a Rainbow translation kit. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events or raw documents.

schema.title: Rainbow Translation Kit Merging
schema.description: Post-process a Rainbow translation kit. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events or raw documents.

preserveSegmentation.title: Preserve the segmentation for the next steps
preserveSegmentation.description: Restore segmentation in text units for use by subsequent pipeline steps

returnRawDocument.title: Return raw documents instead of filter events
returnRawDocument.description: Send raw document events instead of filter events to downstream steps

forceTargetLocale.title: Specify the target locale from the tool instead of the manifest
forceTargetLocale.description: Use the target locale from the pipeline instead of the manifest file

overrideOutputPath.title: Override the output path (leave empty to use the path from the manifest)
overrideOutputPath.description: Directory for merged output; leave empty to use the manifest default

copyCodeMetadata.title: Copy the code metadata from the original file
copyCodeMetadata.description: Copy inline code metadata from the original file to the merged output

skipOriginalTargetCodeMetadata.title: Skip Original Target Code Metadata
skipOriginalTargetCodeMetadata.description: Skip the original target's code metadata when copying during merge, to avoid overwriting translated codes.

toolMeta.description: Post-process a Rainbow translation kit. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events or raw documents.

doc.overview: This step merges the files of a Rainbow translation kit back into their original format. It processes the manifest file (`manifest.rkm`) created by the Rainbow Translation Kit Creation Step, combining translated XLIFF documents from the `work` folder with originals from the `original` folder. The step can output either filter events or raw documents depending on configuration.

copyCodeMetadata.description: Copy inline code metadata from the original file to the merged output
copyCodeMetadata.help: Copies code metadata (inline code definitions, etc.) from the original source file into the merged output.

forceTargetLocale.description: Use the target locale from the pipeline instead of the manifest file
forceTargetLocale.help:
Forces the target locale to be taken from the tool/pipeline calling this step, rather than reading it from the manifest file.

Useful when you need to override the locale specified in the `manifest.rkm`.

overrideOutputPath.description: Directory for merged output; leave empty to use the manifest default
overrideOutputPath.help: Specify a directory where merged output files should be generated. When left empty, the output path defined in the manifest is used.
overrideOutputPath.values: A directory path. Leave empty to use the manifest's default output path.

preserveSegmentation.description: Restore segmentation in text units for use by subsequent pipeline steps
preserveSegmentation.help:
When merging a text unit, the content is un-segmented by default. Since this step is typically the **last step** in a pipeline, restoring segmentation would be unnecessary processing.

Enable this only if you have subsequent steps that need to work with segmented text units.
notes: Disabling this (the default) saves processing time when this step is the last in the pipeline.

preserveWhitespace.description: Preserve whitespace from the original content during merging
preserveWhitespace.help: Controls whether whitespace in the original content is preserved during the merging process.

returnRawDocument.description: Send raw document events instead of filter events to downstream steps
returnRawDocument.help:
Controls what type of events are sent to subsequent steps in the pipeline:

- **Disabled** (default): Sends the current filter events being processed
- **Enabled**: Sends a raw document event for each merged output file

Use this when downstream steps expect raw document input rather than filter events.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The input file must be the `manifest.rkm` file of the translation kit package.
Translated XLIFF documents must be in the `work` folder of the package.
Original documents must be in the `original` folder of the package.
Options in each manifest file can also be edited when starting processing — see the Rainbow Translation Kit Manifest page for details.

examples.title: Basic merging pipeline
examples.description:
The simplest pipeline to merge files of a translation kit uses the Raw Document to Filter Events Step followed by this merging step. The input file is the `manifest.rkm` file.

This is one of the pre-defined pipelines available in Rainbow.
examples.config:
```
= Raw Document to Filter Events Step
+ Rainbow Translation Kit Merging Step
```

entries.description: Convert a raw document into filter events. Expects: raw document. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Raw Document to Filter Events
schema.description: Convert a raw document into filter events. Expects: raw document. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Convert a raw document into filter events. Expects: raw document. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: This step extracts the content of a raw document into a set of filter events using an associated filter configuration. It is typically the first step in a pipeline, converting raw document input into structured filter events that downstream steps can process. To reverse the conversion, use the **Filter Events to Raw Document Step**.
limitations: No known limitations.
processingNotes: This step has no user-configurable parameters — all parameters are provided by the calling application (filter configuration, input encoding, etc.).
Can be placed after any step that sends a raw document, not just at the start of the pipeline.
Requires both source and target language to be set in the pipeline context.

examples.title: Minimal extract-then-merge pipeline
examples.description: The simplest pipeline that extracts a document into filter events and then merges them back into a raw document. This is useful as a baseline or when intermediate processing steps will be added between extraction and merging.

entries.description: Write a RawDocument to an output stream.

schema.title: RawDocument To OutputStream
schema.description: Write a RawDocument to an output stream.

toolMeta.description: Write a RawDocument to an output stream.

doc.overview: The Raw Document To Output Stream step copies the bytes of a raw document to a provided output stream. It is typically the last step in a pipeline that ends with a raw document and needs to be written somewhere other than a file on disk. After copying the content it consumes the raw-document event so nothing is forwarded to a following step.
processingNotes: Copies the raw document's stream verbatim to the destination output stream without interpreting or transforming the content.
Consumes (eats) the raw-document event after writing and emits a no-op event, so it should normally be the final step in the pipeline.
The destination output stream is supplied programmatically rather than through step parameters.

entries.description: Write a RawDocument to an output file.

schema.title: RawDocument Writer
schema.description: Write a RawDocument to an output file.

toolMeta.description: Write a RawDocument to an output file.

doc.overview: The Raw Document Writer step writes the bytes of a raw document to an output file. It is typically the last step in a pipeline that ends with a raw document and needs to produce a file on disk. The output location is taken from the pipeline's output URI; after copying the content it consumes the raw-document event so nothing is forwarded to a following step.
processingNotes: Copies the raw document's stream verbatim to the output file without interpreting or transforming the content.
Creates any missing parent directories for the output path before writing.
Consumes (eats) the raw-document event after writing and emits a no-op event, so it should normally be the final step in the pipeline.
The output location is supplied by the pipeline as an output URI rather than through step parameters.

entries.description: Replace sensitive spans with protected placeholders before processing.

schema.title: Redact
schema.description: Replace sensitive spans with protected placeholders before processing

toolMeta.description: Replace sensitive spans with protected placeholders before processing

detectors.title: Detectors
detectors.description: Detection backends to run: rules and/or entities

entityTypes.title: Entity Categories
entityTypes.description: Entity categories to redact when 'entities' detection is enabled

rulesPath.title: Rules File
rulesPath.description: Path to a redaction rules YAML file

doc.overview:
The **Redact** tool replaces sensitive spans in the source with protected
placeholders before the content is processed (for example, before it is sent
to a translation provider), and stashes the originals locally so a later
`unredact` can restore them. It never emits the original value into the
rewritten content. It is a recoverable source-transform: it runs before any
stand-off overlay is attached, rewriting the source runs in place.

Two detection backends can run, alone or together: `rules` matches literal
terms and regular expressions from a rules file or inline rules, and
`entities` redacts entity annotations already on the block (as produced by
`ai-entity-extract`). When no detector is configured, `rules` is used. The
originals are stored on the block as an in-process secret annotation by
default, or written to a sidecar vault when a vault path is supplied (the
external mode used by `extract` → `merge`).

detectors.description: Detection backends to run: rules and/or entities.
detectors.help:
`rules` matches literal terms and regular expressions from a rules file or
inline rules. `entities` redacts entity annotations already attached to the
block (e.g. by `ai-entity-extract`). Both may be listed; when none is set,
`rules` is used.

entityTypes.description: Entity categories to redact when the `entities` detector is enabled.
entityTypes.help:
Restricts entity redaction to the listed categories. When unset, the
default set is person, organization, product, and location — dates, times,
currencies, and measurements are intentionally excluded because they need
locale-specific formatting rather than hiding.
entityTypes.values: list of entity categories (default `[person, organization, product, location]`)

dependsOn.condition: the `entities` detector must be enabled

placeholder.description: Visible stand-in template for redacted spans.
placeholder.help:
The template used to render the visible placeholder that replaces each
redacted span. It supports `{category}` (the detected category) and `{n}`
(a per-category counter). When unset, the framework default template is
used.

rulesPath.description: Path to a redaction rules YAML file.
rulesPath.help:
Loads literal terms and regular expressions to redact. Only relevant when
the `rules` detector is enabled. A rules file may also define its own
placeholder template, which is used unless the tool's `placeholder` is set.

dependsOn.condition: the `rules` detector must be enabled

limitations: The `entities` detector only acts on entity annotations already present on the block; it does not detect entities itself — pair it with `ai-entity-extract`.
Inline rules and the sidecar vault path are supplied programmatically by callers (e.g. extract → merge) rather than through the schema fields documented here.
Operates on translatable source spans; dates, times, currencies, and measurements are excluded from the default entity set.
processingNotes: A recoverable source-transform — it rewrites the source runs and must run before any stand-off overlay is attached.
Originals are recorded as an in-process secret annotation by default, or in a sidecar vault when a vault path is supplied (external mode).
A later `unredact` reads the recovery record to restore the originals.

examples.title: Redact by rules file
examples.description: Replace spans matched by a rules file with the default placeholder.

examples.title: Redact extracted entities
examples.description: Redact person and organization entities already annotated on the block.

entries.description: Remove target segments from blocks, by locale or by selected text-unit IDs.

filterByIDs.title: Filter by IDs
filterByIDs.description: When true filter text units by ID; when false filter by target locale

removeBlockIfEmpty.title: Remove Empty Text Units
removeBlockIfEmpty.description: Remove the text unit entirely if it has no remaining targets after removal

targetLocale.title: Target Locale
targetLocale.description: Target locale to remove (if empty all targets are removed)

textUnitIDs.title: Text Unit IDs for Removal
textUnitIDs.description: Comma-delimited list of text unit IDs whose targets should be removed; empty removes all targets

doc.overview:
The **Remove Target** tool deletes target segments from blocks. With a target
locale set, it removes only that locale's targets; with no locale, it removes
all targets. It is useful for resetting translations before a fresh run, or
for clearing targets in a specific locale.

Removal can be scoped to specific text units by ID, which restricts the
operation to a comma-separated list of block IDs. Optionally, a block whose
targets are all removed can itself be dropped from the stream.

filterByIDs.description: Filter text units by ID rather than by locale alone.
filterByIDs.help:
When enabled, removal is restricted to the blocks listed in
`textUnitIDs`. When disabled, removal is driven by the target locale
across all blocks.

removeBlockIfEmpty.description: Drop a block when it has no targets left.
removeBlockIfEmpty.help:
When enabled, a block that has no remaining target segments after removal
is dropped from the stream entirely. When disabled, the now-empty block is
kept.

targetLocale.description: The locale whose targets to remove.
targetLocale.help:
The locale to clear. When empty, the targets for every locale on the
block are removed.

textUnitIDs.description: Comma-separated text-unit IDs to restrict removal to.
textUnitIDs.help:
A comma-delimited list of block (text-unit) IDs. When set and ID
filtering is enabled, only blocks whose ID appears in the list have their
targets removed. Leave empty to remove targets from all blocks.

dependsOn.condition: ID filtering must be enabled for this list to apply

processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.

examples.title: Clear one locale's targets
examples.description: Remove the French targets from every block.

examples.title: Remove targets for selected text units
examples.description: Restrict removal to specific block IDs.

entries.description: Remove targets in all or a given set of text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Remove Target
schema.description: Remove targets in all or a given set of text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

tusForTargetRemoval.title: Text Unit IDs for Removal
tusForTargetRemoval.description: Comma-delimited list of text unit IDs whose targets should be removed; empty removes all targets.

targetLocalesToKeep.title: Target Locales to Keep
targetLocalesToKeep.description: Comma-delimited list of target locales to preserve; targets with other locales are removed.

filterBasedOnIds.title: Filter by IDs
filterBasedOnIds.description: When true, filter text units by ID; when false, filter by target locale.

removeTUIfNoTarget.title: Remove Empty Text Units
removeTUIfNoTarget.description: Remove the text unit entirely if it has no remaining targets after removal.

toolMeta.description: Remove targets in all or a given set of text units. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: This step removes target-language content from text units in the pipeline. It can remove all targets from all text units, or selectively remove targets based on text unit IDs or target locales. The step operates on filter events and passes modified filter events downstream.

filterBasedOnIds.description: When true, filter text units by ID; when false, filter by target locale.
filterBasedOnIds.help:
Controls which filtering mode is active. The two modes are **mutually exclusive** — you cannot filter on both IDs and locales simultaneously.

- **true**: Uses `tusForTargetRemoval` to select text units by ID for target removal
- **false**: Uses `targetLocalesToKeep` to select which target locales to preserve

removeTUIfNoTarget.description: Remove the text unit entirely if it has no remaining targets after removal.
removeTUIfNoTarget.help: Controls whether a text unit should be completely removed from the output if it has no remaining targets after the removal process. When disabled, text units with no targets are passed through unchanged (source-only).

targetLocalesToKeep.description: Comma-delimited list of target locales to preserve; targets with other locales are removed.
targetLocalesToKeep.help:
Specify which target locales should be **kept** (not removed). Targets with locales not in this list will be removed.

When left empty, all targets are kept (none removed based on locale). This field is only used when filtering by locales rather than by IDs.
targetLocalesToKeep.values: Comma-separated locale codes (e.g., `fr-FR,de-DE`). Empty string means keep all targets.
examples: `"fr-FR,es-ES"` — keep only French and Spanish targets, remove all others

dependsOn.condition: must be false for this field to be used

tusForTargetRemoval.description: Comma-delimited list of text unit IDs whose targets should be removed; empty removes all targets.
tusForTargetRemoval.help:
Specify which text units should have their targets removed by listing their IDs. When left empty, **all targets on all text units** are removed.

IDs should be separated by commas with no spaces around delimiters.
tusForTargetRemoval.values: Comma-separated text unit IDs (e.g., `tu1,tu2,tu3`). Empty string means all text units.
examples: `"id1,id2,id3"` — remove targets only from these three text units
`""` (empty) — remove targets from all text units

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this field to be used

limitations: The wiki states no known limitations.
processingNotes: All target languages are removed from matching text units — not just a specific target language.
Filtering by IDs and filtering by locales are mutually exclusive; the `filterBasedOnIds` flag determines which mode is active.

examples.title: Remove all targets
examples.description: Remove targets from every text unit in the pipeline. Leave `tusForTargetRemoval` empty and keep `filterBasedOnIds` as `true`.

examples.title: Remove targets from specific text units
examples.description: Remove targets only from text units with IDs `tu1` and `tu2`, leaving all other text units untouched.

examples.title: Keep only specific locales
examples.description: Keep French and German targets, removing all other target locales. Switch to locale-based filtering by setting `filterBasedOnIds` to `false`.

entries.description: Identify repeated source segments across blocks for translation-memory leverage.

doc.overview:
The **Repetition Analysis** tool tracks source text as blocks stream through
the pipeline and tags each block according to whether its source has been seen
before. The first time a given source text appears it is marked as a
first occurrence; subsequent identical occurrences are marked as repetitions.
Each block also records a group key linking equal segments, the running count
of occurrences, and its 1-based index within the group.

Source text is trimmed of surrounding whitespace before comparison. The output
feeds scoping and pricing: repeated segments can be translated once and reused,
so identifying them quantifies the leverage available from repetition.

caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, segments that differ only in letter case are treated as
distinct. Disable it to fold case differences together, so that, for
example, `Save` and `save` count as the same segment.

limitations: Matches exact (trimmed) source text only; near-duplicates and fuzzy matches are not detected here.
Repetition state is tracked across the whole run; ordering of occurrences depends on block order in the pipeline.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Counts the source side only.

examples.title: Case-insensitive repetition
examples.description: Treat segments that differ only in case as repetitions.

entries.description: Analyzes repetitions in input documents. Adds AltTranslationsAnnotation and RepetitiveSegmentAnnotation to a repetitive segment. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Repetition Analysis
schema.description: Analyzes repetitions in input documents. Adds AltTranslationsAnnotation and RepetitiveSegmentAnnotation to a repetitive segment. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Analyzes repetitions in input documents. Adds AltTranslationsAnnotation and RepetitiveSegmentAnnotation to a repetitive segment. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Analyzes repetitions across input documents, performing either exact or configurable fuzzy matching on source segments. For each group of repetitive segments, it attaches `RepetitiveSegmentAnnotation` to the source and `AltTranslationsAnnotation` to the corresponding target, enabling downstream steps (like leveraging or counting) to act on repeated content.
limitations: No known limitations per the Okapi documentation.
processingNotes: Two annotation types are created: **RepetitiveSegmentAnnotation** (on the repetitive source segment) and **AltTranslationsAnnotation** (on the corresponding target segment).
The first segment in a repetition group does **not** receive an `AltTranslationsAnnotation` — this prevents counting steps from double-counting it as repetitive with itself.
Fuzzy threshold controls whether matching is exact (100) or fuzzy (lower values). Leave at 100 for exact repetitions only.

entries.description: Simplify resources. Simplification algorythm is format-specific. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Resource Simplifier
schema.description: Simplify resources. Simplification algorythm is format-specific. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Simplify resources. Simplification algorythm is format-specific. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Simplifies the resources of filter events by splitting generic skeletons into parts that contain no references. Original references are converted to skeleton parts or `TEXT_UNIT` events, and the resulting sequence is packed into a single `MULTI_EVENT` event. This step is format-specific and operates on filter events.
limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Skeleton parts are attached to newly created `DOCUMENT_PART` events.
Original references are converted either to skeleton parts or `TEXT_UNIT` events.
The resulting sequence of `DOCUMENT_PART` and `TEXT_UNIT` events is packed into a single `MULTI_EVENT` event.
The simplification algorithm is format-specific — behavior varies depending on the filter that produced the events.

entries.description: Remove the RTF layer from a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: RTF Conversion
schema.description: Remove the RTF layer from a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

lineBreak.title: Type of line-break to use
lineBreak.description: Select the type of line-break to use in the output.

bomOnUTF8.title: Use Byte-Order-Mark for UTF-8 output
bomOnUTF8.description: Add a Byte-Order-Mark at the beginning of the file when the output encoding is UTF-8.

updateEncoding.title: Try to update the encoding declarations (when detected)
updateEncoding.description: Automatically update encoding declarations in XML and HTML output files to match the output encoding.

toolMeta.description: Remove the RTF layer from a text-based file. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Removes the RTF layer from text-based input files, outputting only the visible text content. Formatting other than spaces, line-breaks, and tabs is discarded, along with hidden/deleted text, images, and special content. Can be used to post-process Trados-Tagged RTF files, extracting only the translated sections.

bomOnUTF8.description: Add a Byte-Order-Mark at the beginning of the file when the output encoding is UTF-8.
bomOnUTF8.help:
Adds a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM) at the beginning of the output file when the output encoding is UTF-8. Has no effect for non-UTF-8 output encodings.

See the [Unicode BOM FAQ](http://www.unicode.org/faq/utf_bom.html) for details on when a BOM is appropriate.
notes: Only applies when the output encoding is UTF-8 — ignored for other encodings.

lineBreak.help:
Controls which line-break sequence is written to the output file.

- **DOS/Windows** — `\r\n` (CR+LF, 0x0D+0x0A)
- **Unix/Linux** — `\n` (LF, 0x0A)
- **Macintosh** — `\r` (CR, 0x0D)
lineBreak.values: Platform-specific line-break codes: `$0d0a$` (DOS/Windows CR+LF), `$0a$` (Unix/Linux LF), `$0d$` (Macintosh CR)

updateEncoding.description: Automatically update encoding declarations in XML and HTML output files to match the output encoding.
updateEncoding.help:
Automatically updates encoding declarations in the output file to match the selected output encoding. Supports two file types:

- **XML files**: Updates the `encoding="..."` attribute in the XML declaration. If the declaration exists but has no encoding attribute, one is added. If there is no XML declaration at all, nothing is updated.
- **HTML files**: Looks for the pattern `content=... charset=...` and updates the `charset` value. If the pattern is not found, nothing is updated.
notes: Warning: The encoding update uses pattern matching, not full parsing. A commented-out declaration could be incorrectly updated.

limitations: Characters encoded as `SYMBOL` fields (e.g., drawing symbols from Word using Dingbats or Wingdings fonts) are not output.
Encoding declaration updates for XML and HTML use pattern matching rather than parsing, so commented-out declarations may be incorrectly updated.
All formatting beyond spaces, line-breaks, and tabs is discarded.
Hidden text, deleted text, images, and other special content are discarded.
processingNotes: A warning is issued for each line where one or more characters cannot be represented in the selected output encoding.
Can be used to post-process Trados-Tagged RTF files — only the translated sections are output.
For access to both source and target text in Trados-Tagged RTF files, use the Trados-Tagged RTF Filter instead.

examples.title: Post-process Trados-Tagged RTF
examples.description: Use the RTF Conversion step after reading a Trados-Tagged RTF file to extract only the translated text. The step strips the RTF layer and outputs plain text with only the translated sections. For full source+target access with inline codes, use the Trados-Tagged RTF Filter instead.

entries.description: Classify each block into a scoping category from upstream repetition and match status.

doc.overview:
The **Scoping Report** tool assigns each translatable block a scoping category
and records it on the block's properties. The category is derived from
properties set by upstream analysis tools: a block flagged as a repetition by
repetition analysis becomes a repetition; blocks marked unchanged or leveraged
by diff-leverage become an exact match or a fuzzy match respectively; everything
else is classified as new.

Because it reads properties produced earlier in the pipeline, scoping report is
meant to run after repetition analysis and any leverage step. The classification
underpins word-count breakdowns and pricing that distinguish new text from
reused or partially matched text.
limitations: Relies on properties set by upstream tools (repetition analysis, diff-leverage); without them, blocks are classified as new.
Produces a per-block category, not an aggregated cross-document summary by itself.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Run after repetition analysis and leverage steps so their status properties are available.

examples.title: Classify blocks after repetition analysis
examples.description: The tool takes no parameters; place it downstream of repetition analysis in a flow.

toolMeta.description: Create a template-based scoping report based on word count and leverage annotations. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: This step creates a template-based report on various counts (word count, character count, etc.) and optionally leveraged data. It acts as a presentation layer, displaying information provided by other steps such as the Word Count Step, Character Count Step, or Leveraging Step. Reports can be generated in plain text or HTML format using customizable templates with report field placeholders. To include leveraging statistics, the pipeline must include a leveraging step prior to this step.

countAsNonTranslatable_Concordance.help: Words from TM concordance or phrase matches (usually word- or term-level matches only).

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactDocumentCont.help: Words matching EXACT from the same document. Typically provided by the **Repetition Analysis Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactLocalContext.help: Words matching EXACT plus a small number of surrounding segments. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactMatch.help: Words matching text and codes exactly (100% match). Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactPreviousVers.help: Words matching EXACT from the preceding version of the same document. Note: must come from the immediately prior version (e.g. v3 for v4, not v2 or v1). Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactRepaired.help: Words matching text and codes exactly only after automated repair (e.g. number replacement, code repair, capitalization, punctuation). Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactStructuralMa.help: Words matching EXACT plus the structural type of the segment (title, paragraph, list element, etc.). Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactTextOnly.help: Words matching text exactly, but with a difference in one or more inline codes. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactTextOnlyPrev.help: Words matching text exactly (ignoring code differences) from a previous version of the same document. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactTextOnlyUniq.help: Words matching text exactly (ignoring code differences) with a unique ID match. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_ExactUniqueIdMatc.help: Words matching EXACT and a unique ID. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**. Affects the Okapi-category non-translatable/translatable split.

countAsNonTranslatable_FuzzyMatch.help: Words with a partial match on text and/or codes (fuzzy match). Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_FuzzyPreviousVers.help: Words with a fuzzy match from a previous version of the same document. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_FuzzyRepaired.help: Words with a fuzzy match where automated repair (number replacement, code repair, etc.) was applied to the target. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_FuzzyUniqueIdMatc.help: Words with a fuzzy match that also match a unique ID. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXAlphanumericOn.help: Words/characters in text units containing only alphanumeric words (no natural language content requiring translation).

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXExactMatched.help: Words/characters in text units matched unambiguously with a prior translation, requiring no translator input. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXFuzzyMatch.help: Words/characters in text units fuzzy-matched against a leveraged TM database. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXLeveragedMatch.help: Words/characters in text units matched against a leveraged translation memory database. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXMeasurementOnl.help: Words/characters from measurement-only text units (e.g. "10 cm", "5 kg").

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXNumericOnlyTex.help: Words/characters in text units containing only numeric words.

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXProtected.help: Words/characters in text marked as 'protected' or not translatable (e.g. XLIFF `<mrk mtype="protected">` elements). Affects the `PROJECT_GMX_NONTRANSLATABLE_WORD_COUNT` / `PROJECT_GMX_TRANSLATABLE_WORD_COUNT` split in the report.

countAsNonTranslatable_GMXRepetitionMatc.help: Words/characters in repeating text units not matched in any other form. Repetition matching takes precedence over fuzzy matching. Typically provided by the **Repetition Analysis Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_MT.help: Words from translations provided by a machine translation engine. Typically provided by the **Leveraging Step**.

countAsNonTranslatable_PhraseAssembled.help: Words from translations assembled from phrases in the TM or other resources. Different algorithms may be used for assembly.

customTemplateString.help:
Specify the report template directly as a string instead of referencing a file. Uses the same template syntax as file-based templates — text with `[FIELD]` placeholders and `[[row]]` table rows.

Useful when generating templates programmatically or embedding short templates inline.

dependsOn.condition: customTemplateURI takes precedence if both are specified

customTemplateURI.help:
Path or URI to a custom report template file. Templates contain text mixed with report field placeholders enclosed in brackets (e.g. `[PROJECT_NAME]`, `[ITEM_TOTAL_WORD_COUNT]`). Table rows use double brackets: `[[col1],[col2],]`.

If left empty or the URI is not found, the **default HTML template** is used.
notes: If the specified URI is not found, the step silently falls back to the default template rather than raising an error.

outputPath.help:
Full path for the generated report file. Supports variable substitution:

- `${rootDir}` — the pipeline root directory
- `${srcLoc}` — source locale
- `${trgLoc}` — target locale
- Other source/target locale variables
outputPath.values: Path string with optional `${rootDir}`, `${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}` variable substitution. Typically an `.html` file when using the default template.

projectName.help: The project name appears in the report title and is available as the `[PROJECT_NAME]` template field. This is a display-only label — it does not affect processing.

processingNotes: The step is a **presentation layer** — it does not calculate counts itself. Count data must be provided by preceding pipeline steps (Word Count Step, Character Count Step, Leveraging Step, Repetition Analysis Step). Missing steps result in zeros in the report.

The `countAsNonTranslatable_*` flags control which match categories are subtracted from the total to compute the **translatable** vs **non-translatable** split. They affect both GMX-category and Okapi-category calculations.
Character count equivalents for all report fields are available by replacing `WORD` with `CHARACTER` or adding `_CHARACTER` suffix. Character counts exclude whitespace and punctuation.
The step passes filter events through unchanged — it only reads annotations to generate the report.

examples.title: CSV-style plain text template
examples.description: A minimal plain text template producing a CSV-like scoping report with per-file leveraging breakdown. Table rows use double-bracket syntax `[[...]]` to iterate over items.
examples.input:
```
Project Name: [PROJECT_NAME]
Creation Date: [PROJECT_DATE]
Target Locale: [PROJECT_TARGET_LOCALE]

File,Exact Previous Version,Exact Local Context,100% Matches,Fuzzy,Repetitions,Total,
[[ITEM_NAME],[ITEM_EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION],[ITEM_EXACT_LOCAL_CONTEXT],[ITEM_EXACT],[ITEM_FUZZY],[ITEM_GMX_REPETITION_MATCHED_WORD_COUNT],[ITEM_TOTAL_WORD_COUNT],]
Total,[PROJECT_EXACT_PREVIOUS_VERSION],[PROJECT_EXACT_LOCAL_CONTEXT],[PROJECT_EXACT],[PROJECT_FUZZY],[PROJECT_GMX_REPETITION_MATCHED_WORD_COUNT],[PROJECT_TOTAL_WORD_COUNT]
```
examples.output:
```
Project Name: Community website
Creation Date: 17.03.2011 23:21:23 CET
Target Locale: fr-ca

File,Exact Previous Version,Exact Local Context,100% Matches,Fuzzy,Repetitions,Total,
aa324.html,10,23,12,57,132,23,
form.html,31,22,13,17,19,17,
Total,210,323,512,357,312,154
```

entries.description: Run a user-supplied ES5 JavaScript script over each part to inspect or modify block text.

allowSourceMutation.title: Allow Source Mutation
allowSourceMutation.description: Permit the script to modify the source text (off by default; source is read-only)

doc.overview:
The **Script** tool runs a user-provided ES5 JavaScript program for every
part in the stream. The script receives the current part as a `part` object
and can read and modify block source and target text. It is an escape hatch
for bespoke transformations that no built-in tool covers.

Inside the script, `part.type` identifies the part and `part.block` exposes
the block (with `id`, `translatable`, `source`, and `targets`). Helper
functions are available: `emit(part)` forwards a part, `skip()` drops the
current part, and `log(msg)` writes a message to standard error. When the
script neither emits nor skips, the part passes through with any in-place
text edits applied. The script is provided inline or read from a `.js` file.

code.help: The script body, used when `source` is `inline`. Required in that mode.

dependsOn.condition: used when source is `inline`

scriptFile.help:
Path to a `.js` file to load and run, used when `source` is `file`.
Required in that mode.

dependsOn.condition: used when source is `file`

source.help:
`inline` runs the script supplied in the `code` field; `file` loads the
script from the path in `scriptFile`.

limitations: The engine supports ES5 JavaScript only.
Only block source and target text changes are applied back; other modifications to the part are not persisted.
processingNotes: Each tool instance owns its own runtime and runs single-threaded over its input.
A part passes through unchanged when the script neither emits a part nor calls skip.

examples.title: Trim trailing whitespace from source text
examples.description: Modify source text in place with an inline script.

examples.title: Load a script from a file
examples.description: Run a script stored alongside the project.

toolMeta.description: Performs search and replace on the entire file or the text units. Expects raw document or filter events. Sends back: raw document or filter events.

doc.overview: This step performs search and replace actions on either the text units or the full content of input documents. It can process both raw documents (whole-file text replacement) and filter events (text unit content replacement). Patterns are processed in the order they are declared, and both literal and Java regular expression modes are supported.

count.help: Tracks the total number of replacements performed. Typically used as an output counter rather than a user-configured input.

dotAll.description: Make the period character match every character including line-feed.
dotAll.help:
Changes the meaning of the period character `.` so that it matches **every** character, including `\n` (line-feed).

By default, `.` matches every character **except** `\n`.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

ignoreCase.description: Ignore case when matching search patterns.
ignoreCase.help:
Makes pattern matching case-insensitive. Applies to **all** search expressions, not just the currently selected one.

For example, the pattern `bear` matches `Bearcat`, `BEARCAT`, and `bearcat`.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

logPath.description: Path where the log of performed replacements will be saved.
logPath.help: Path where the replacement log file will be written, recording which replacements were made during processing.
logPath.values: File path. Supports `${rootDir}` and `${inputRootDir}` path variables.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

multiLine.description: Make ^ and $ match at the beginning and end of each line.
multiLine.help:
Changes the meaning of `^` and `$` so they match at the beginning and end of **any line**, not just the beginning and end of the whole string.

Applies to all expressions, not just the selected one.

dependsOn.condition: must be true

regEx.description: Enable Java regular expression mode for all search patterns.
regEx.help:
Enables [Java regular expression](http://download.oracle.com/javase/6/docs/api/java/util/regex/Pattern.html) mode for all search patterns.

When enabled, use `$1`, `$2`, etc. in replacement strings to reference captured groups from the search pattern (see [`Matcher.replaceAll()`](http://java.sun.com/javase/6/docs/api/java/util/regex/Matcher.html#replaceAll(java.lang.String))).

In **both** modes, search and replace strings support these escape sequences:
- `\n` = line-feed
- `\r` = carriage-return
- `\t` = tab
- `\\` = literal backslash
- `\uHHHH` = Unicode character (hex)

Additionally, when regex is **on**, replacement strings support:
- `$N` = match of search group N
- `\$` = literal dollar sign
notes: For any escape sequence `\C` where `C` is not a recognized special character, the result is the literal character itself (e.g., `\*` produces `*`).
You must use `\\` to represent a literal backslash in both modes.

replaceALL.description: Replace all matches found instead of only the first match.
replaceALL.help:
Replaces **all** matches found. If disabled, only the **first** match is replaced.

- **Text unit mode**: replaces all or only one occurrence *per text unit*
- **Raw document mode**: replaces all or only one occurrence *for the whole file*

replacementsPath.description: Path to a tab-delimited UTF-8 file with search and replacement pairs.
replacementsPath.help:
Path to a **tab-delimited, UTF-8-encoded** text file with two columns: the first column contains search strings and the second contains replacement strings.

This file is processed **separately after** the table expressions have been processed.
replacementsPath.values: Tab-delimited UTF-8 text file. Supports `${rootDir}` and `${inputRootDir}` path variables.
notes: Replacements from this file are currently limited to **literal searches only** (non-regex, substring matching).
examples: `${rootDir}/my-replacements.tsv` — file relative to the pipeline root directory

saveLog.description: Save a log of all replacements performed to the specified file.
saveLog.help: Saves a log of all replacements performed during processing to the file specified in the log path.

source.description: Perform search and replace on the source content of text units.
source.help:
Performs search and replace on the **source** content of text units.

Ignored when the step processes a raw document (whole-file mode).

target.description: Perform search and replace on the target content of text units.
target.help:
Performs search and replace on the **target** content of text units.

Ignored when the step processes a raw document (whole-file mode).
notes: When working on target content, you may need to use the **Create Target Step** before this step to ensure there is content in the target.

limitations: When working with a raw document as input, the **entire content** is loaded into memory. This may cause problems with very large documents.
The external replacements file (`replacementsPath`) is limited to literal (non-regex) substring searches only.
Patterns are processed sequentially in declared order — longer patterns must be placed **first** if there is a chance of overlapping matches.
processingNotes: Patterns are processed in the order they appear in the list. Ensure longer patterns come before shorter overlapping ones.
When receiving **filter events**, search/replace operates on text unit content. When receiving a **raw document**, it operates on the entire file as plain text (no escaped character conversion).
The external replacements file is processed **after** all table expressions.
The `source` and `target` options are only relevant in filter events mode; they are ignored in raw document mode.

examples.title: Escape sequences in search and replace
examples.description: Both literal and regex modes support escape sequences in search and replace strings. The available sequences differ slightly between modes.
examples.config:
```yaml
# Literal mode escapes (regEx: false)
# Search: \n \r \t \\ \uHHHH
# Replace: \n \r \t \\ \uHHHH

# Regex mode additional replace escapes (regEx: true)
# Replace: $1 $2 ... $N (group references)
# Replace: \$ (literal dollar sign)
```

examples.title: External replacements file format
examples.description: The tab-delimited replacements file uses two columns separated by a tab character. Each line defines one search/replace pair.

entries.description: Find and replace text in blocks, with optional regular-expression matching and flags.

dotAll.title: Dot Also Matches Line-Feed
dotAll.description: Make the period character match every character including line-feed

ignoreCase.title: Ignore Case Differences
ignoreCase.description: Ignore case when matching search patterns

multiLine.title: Multiline Mode
multiLine.description: Make ^ and $ match at the beginning and end of each line

regEx.title: Use Regular Expressions
regEx.description: Enable regular expression mode for all search patterns

replaceAll.title: Replace All Instances
replaceAll.description: Replace all matches instead of only the first

source.title: Replace in Source Content
source.description: Perform search and replace on source content

target.title: Replace in Target Content
target.description: Perform search and replace on target content

doc.overview:
The **Search and Replace** tool performs find-and-replace on block text. The
search/replace pairs themselves are supplied programmatically or by the
configured command; the schema-visible options here control matching
behaviour — literal versus regular-expression mode, case sensitivity, and
whether the replacement scans source, target, or both.

When regular-expression mode is on, the case, dot-all, and multiline flags are
applied to every pattern. By default the tool replaces all matches in target
text only.

dotAll.description: Let the dot metacharacter match line feeds.
dotAll.help:
When enabled (regex mode), the `.` metacharacter also matches newline
characters, so a pattern can span lines.

dependsOn.condition: applies in regular-expression mode

ignoreCase.help:
When enabled, matching ignores letter case. In regex mode this adds the
case-insensitive flag to each pattern.

multiLine.description: Make anchors match at each line boundary.
multiLine.help:
When enabled (regex mode), `^` and `$` match at the start and end of each
line rather than only the whole text.

dependsOn.condition: applies in regular-expression mode

regEx.description: Treat search patterns as regular expressions.
regEx.help:
When enabled, every search pattern is compiled as a regular expression
and the case, dot-all, and multiline flags below apply.

replaceAll.description: Replace every match rather than only the first.
replaceAll.help:
When enabled, all occurrences of a pattern are replaced. When disabled,
only the first match in each text is replaced.

source.help: When enabled, search and replace runs over the block's source text.

target.help:
When enabled, search and replace runs over the target text of the
configured locale.

limitations: Regular expressions use Go's RE2 syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookaround.
The search/replace pairs are supplied programmatically; only the matching options appear in the schema.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
When neither source nor target is set, the tool applies to both for backward compatibility.

examples.title: Replace a literal string in targets
examples.description: Use literal matching on target text only.

examples.title: Case-insensitive regex over both scopes
examples.description: Enable regex mode with case folding across source and target.
examples.config:
regEx: true
ignoreCase: true
source: true
target: true

entries.description: Count the source and target segments in each translatable block.

doc.overview:
The **Segment Count** tool records how many segments each translatable block
contains. The source segment count is always recorded on the block's
properties. When a target locale is configured and the block has a translation
for it, the number of target segments is recorded as well.

Counting segments — rather than words or characters — measures the granularity
of a block after segmentation, which is useful for verifying that source and
target segmentation line up and for segment-based scoping.
limitations: Target segment counts require a configured target locale and an existing translation for the block.
Counts follow the active segmentation; results depend on how the text was segmented.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.

examples.title: Count segments
examples.description: The tool takes no parameters; add it to a flow with an empty configuration.

entries.description: Split block text into sentence-level segments using SRX-style rules.

sourceSrxPath.description: Path to an SRX 2.0 rules file for source text (srx engine)

targetSrxPath.title: Target SRX Rules Path

threshold.description: Boundary probability threshold for the sat engine (0 = model default)

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.title: Treat Isolated Codes as Whitespace
treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: Treat isolated inline codes as whitespace during segmentation

trimLeadingWhitespace.title: Trim Leading Whitespace
trimLeadingWhitespace.description: Exclude leading whitespace from each segment span

trimTrailingWhitespace.title: Trim Trailing Whitespace
trimTrailingWhitespace.description: Exclude trailing whitespace from each segment span

doc.overview:
The **Segmentation** tool splits a block's text into sentence-level segments.
Segmentation determines the unit of translation and of translation-memory
matching, so consistent segmentation is important for leverage and review.
By default the tool segments source text using built-in SRX-style rules that
handle common sentence boundaries and abbreviations; rules can also be loaded
from an SRX file.

The number of segments produced is recorded on each block. Already-segmented
text is left alone unless re-segmentation is requested. Target text can be
segmented independently, with its own rules file.

overwriteSegmentation.help:
By default text that already has more than one segment is left untouched.
Enable this to discard the existing segmentation and re-segment.

renumberCodes.description: Renumber inline-code IDs within each segment.
renumberCodes.help:
When enabled, inline-code IDs are renumbered to start at 1 within each
resulting segment.

segmentSource.help:
When enabled, the source text is split into segments using the source
rules. If neither source nor target segmentation is enabled, source
segmentation is applied by default.

segmentTarget.help:
When enabled, existing target text for the configured locale is split
into segments using the target rules.

sourceSrxPath.description: Path to an SRX rules file for the source.
sourceSrxPath.help:
Path to an SRX segmentation rules file used for the source text. When
empty, the built-in default rules are used.

targetSrxPath.description: Path to an SRX rules file for the target.
targetSrxPath.help:
Path to an SRX segmentation rules file used for the target text. When
empty, the built-in default rules are used.

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: Treat isolated inline codes as whitespace while segmenting.
treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.help:
When enabled, an isolated inline code is treated like whitespace for the
purpose of finding segment boundaries.

limitations: The built-in rule set targets common Latin-script sentence boundaries; non-Latin scripts may need a custom SRX file.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
The resulting segment count is written to a block property.

examples.description: Split source into sentences using the built-in rules.

examples.title: Re-segment with a custom SRX file
examples.description: Replace existing segmentation using project-specific rules.

segmentTarget.description: Segment existing target text using SRX rules for the processed locales.

checkSegments.description: Verify that target segments match source segments when target content exists.

trimSrcLeadingWS.title: Trim Source Leading Whitespace
trimSrcLeadingWS.description: Maximum leading whitespace characters to trim from source segments (-1 for default).

trimSrcTrailingWS.title: Trim Source Trailing Whitespace
trimSrcTrailingWS.description: Maximum trailing whitespace characters to trim from source segments (-1 for default).

trimTrgLeadingWS.title: Trim Target Leading Whitespace
trimTrgLeadingWS.description: Maximum leading whitespace characters to trim from target segments (-1 for default).

trimTrgTrailingWS.title: Trim Target Trailing Whitespace
trimTrgTrailingWS.description: Maximum trailing whitespace characters to trim from target segments (-1 for default).

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.title: Treat Isolated Codes as Whitespace
treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: Treat isolated inline codes as whitespace during segmentation.

doNotSegmentIfHasTarget.title: Skip Segmentation if Target Exists
doNotSegmentIfHasTarget.description: Do not segment text units that already have target content.

toolMeta.description: Apply SRX segmentation to the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Segments the content of extracted text units into smaller parts (typically sentences) using SRX 2.0 segmentation rules. The step processes filter events and applies regular-expression-based break/non-break patterns defined in an SRX document. Text units flagged as non-translatable, empty, or already segmented (unless overwrite is enabled) are skipped.

checkSegments.description: Verify that target segments match source segments when target content exists.
checkSegments.help:
Verifies that each source segment has a corresponding target segment when target content exists. Checks that both source and target have the same number of segments by matching segment IDs.

This verification does **not** check whether the target content is actually a translation of the source — it only validates structural correspondence.

deepenSegmentation.description: Keep existing segmentation but split segments further using the current SRX rules.
deepenSegmentation.help:
When a text unit is already segmented, keeps the existing segmentation but attempts to split existing segments further using the current SRX rules. Creates additional segments only if the new rules provide higher granularity.

This is one of three mutually exclusive behaviors for pre-segmented input — see also `overwriteSegmentation`.

dependsOn.condition: conflicts with — only one of overwriteSegmentation or deepenSegmentation should be true

doNotSegmentIfHasTarget.description: Do not segment text units that already have target content.
doNotSegmentIfHasTarget.help: Skips segmentation entirely for text units that already have target content. Useful when pre-translated content should be preserved as-is without being broken into segments.

forceSegmentedOutput.description: Force output formats to show segmentation regardless of filter configuration.
forceSegmentedOutput.help:
Forces file formats that support segmentation representation to show segments in the output, overriding the filter's own configuration.

For example, the XLIFF Filter normally only outputs segments for entries that had segments in the input. With this option enabled, segments are shown even for text units that had no prior segmentation.

If disabled, segment display depends on the filter configuration.

overwriteSegmentation.description: Re-segment already-segmented text units, replacing previous segmentation.
overwriteSegmentation.help:
When a text unit is already segmented, re-segments it using the current SRX rules. The previous segmentation is completely replaced.

This is one of three mutually exclusive behaviors for pre-segmented input — see also `deepenSegmentation`.

dependsOn.condition: conflicts with — only one of overwriteSegmentation or deepenSegmentation should be true

renumberCodes.description: Renumber inline code IDs in each segment to start at 1.
renumberCodes.help:
Reassigns inline code IDs within each resulting segment so they start at `1`. Useful when working with translation memories that expect sequential code IDs restarting per segment.

Use the **Restore original IDs to renumbered codes** option in the Desegmentation Step to reverse this when merging back.
notes: **Warning:** This option cannot work with documents where the ID values are not numeric, or not sequential.
Using this option when creating a translation kit requires a separate Desegmentation Step when merging back, and may not be possible with some segment-based formats like TTX.

segmentSource.description: Segment the source text of text units using SRX rules.
segmentSource.help:
Controls whether the source text of each text unit is segmented using the configured SRX rules.

The SRX document path is specified separately and supports `${rootDir}` and `${inputRootDir}` variables.
notes: Text units flagged as non-translatable or with no content are never segmented, regardless of this setting.

segmentTarget.description: Segment existing target text using SRX rules for the processed locales.
segmentTarget.help:
Controls whether existing target text is segmented using SRX rules. Only target locales currently being processed are affected — other locales in the document remain untouched.

The target SRX document can be the same as the source SRX document.
notes: Only applies when a target text already exists for the locale being processed.

treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.description: Treat isolated inline codes as whitespace during segmentation.
treatIsolatedCodesAsWhitespace.help: Treats isolated inline codes (codes not part of a paired open/close sequence) as whitespace for segmentation purposes. This can improve segmentation accuracy when inline codes appear at segment boundaries.

trimSrcLeadingWS.description: Maximum leading whitespace characters to trim from source segments (-1 for default).
trimSrcLeadingWS.help:
Controls trimming of leading whitespace from source segments.

- `-1` — use default behavior (no explicit trimming)
- `0` — do not trim
- Positive integer — trim up to that many whitespace characters
trimSrcLeadingWS.values: `-1` for default, `0` to disable, or a positive integer for the max characters to trim

trimSrcTrailingWS.description: Maximum trailing whitespace characters to trim from source segments (-1 for default).
trimSrcTrailingWS.help:
Controls trimming of trailing whitespace from source segments.

- `-1` — use default behavior (no explicit trimming)
- `0` — do not trim
- Positive integer — trim up to that many whitespace characters
trimSrcTrailingWS.values: `-1` for default, `0` to disable, or a positive integer for the max characters to trim

trimTrgLeadingWS.description: Maximum leading whitespace characters to trim from target segments (-1 for default).
trimTrgLeadingWS.help:
Controls trimming of leading whitespace from target segments.

- `-1` — use default behavior (no explicit trimming)
- `0` — do not trim
- Positive integer — trim up to that many whitespace characters
trimTrgLeadingWS.values: `-1` for default, `0` to disable, or a positive integer for the max characters to trim

trimTrgTrailingWS.description: Maximum trailing whitespace characters to trim from target segments (-1 for default).
trimTrgTrailingWS.help:
Controls trimming of trailing whitespace from target segments.

- `-1` — use default behavior (no explicit trimming)
- `0` — do not trim
- Positive integer — trim up to that many whitespace characters
trimTrgTrailingWS.values: `-1` for default, `0` to disable, or a positive integer for the max characters to trim

limitations: Strict implementation of SRX syntax in Java is not possible — see the **SRX and Java** wiki page for details.
Using **Renumber code IDs** when creating a translation kit requires a separate Desegmentation Step when merging back, and may not be possible with segment-based formats like TTX.
Text units flagged as non-translatable or with no content are never segmented.
processingNotes: Segmentation uses SRX 2.0 rules — regular expressions define break and non-break positions.
By default, already-segmented text units are not re-segmented. Use `overwriteSegmentation` or `deepenSegmentation` to change this behavior.
The three pre-segmented behaviors (keep, overwrite, deepen) are mutually exclusive — `overwriteSegmentation` and `deepenSegmentation` are both false by default, meaning existing segmentation is kept.
When `forceSegmentedOutput` is enabled, it modifies the downstream filter's output option to represent segments even if the filter would not normally do so.

examples.title: Default sentence segmentation
examples.description: Segments source text into sentences using SRX rules, verifying target segments match. This is the default configuration.
examples.config:
```yaml
segmentSource: true
segmentTarget: false
checkSegments: true
forceSegmentedOutput: true
```

examples.title: Re-segment with new rules
examples.description: Overwrites any existing segmentation with new SRX rules, useful when migrating to updated segmentation standards.
examples.config:
```yaml
segmentSource: true
overwriteSegmentation: true
forceSegmentedOutput: true
```

examples.title: Deepen existing segmentation
examples.description: Keeps existing segmentation but applies finer-grained SRX rules to split segments further where possible.
examples.config:
```yaml
segmentSource: true
deepenSegmentation: true
forceSegmentedOutput: true
```

entries.description: Convert each aligned segment pair (for all target locales) to its own complete text unit Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Segments to Text Units Converter
schema.description: Convert each aligned segment pair (for all target locales) to its own complete text unit Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Convert each aligned segment pair (for all target locales) to its own complete text unit Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Converts segmented text units (paragraphs) into multiple text units, one per aligned sentence pair, for each target locale. This is useful for steps like the Diff Leverage Step which require un-segmented text units. Text units that reference other `IResource` objects or are referents are passed through unchanged.
limitations: Using this step may prevent merging, so it should only be used in pipelines that do not require translated translation kits to be merged.
Text units that refer to another `IResource` or are referents are passed through as-is, even if they contain segments.
processingNotes: Each segmented text unit (paragraph) is split into multiple text units, one per aligned segment pair.
The conversion is applied for all target locales.
Referent and reference text units are passed through unchanged to avoid complex dependency handling.

examples.title: Pipeline with Diff Leverage Step
examples.description:
Place this step before the Diff Leverage Step to convert segmented paragraphs into individual text units, which the Diff Leverage Step requires.

1. Segmentation Step segments paragraphs into aligned sentence pairs
2. Segments to Text Units Converter splits each segment pair into its own text unit
3. Diff Leverage Step performs leverage on the individual text units

entries.description: Aligns sentences within text units (paragraphs). Produces sentence alignments as bilingual text units or a TMX file.

schema.title: Sentence Alignment
schema.description: Aligns sentences within text units (paragraphs). Produces sentence alignments as bilingual text units or a TMX file.

generateTMX.description: If generateTMX is false generate bilingual TextUnits, otherwise (true) output a TMX file
