collapseWhitespace.title: Collapse whitspace
collapseWhitespace.description: Collapse whitespace (space, newline etc.) to a single space before segmentation and alignment

segmentSource.title: Segment Source Content
segmentSource.description: Segment the source content before alignment, overriding any existing segmentation

useCustomSourceRules.title: Custom Source Segmentation Rules
useCustomSourceRules.description: Use a custom SRX file for source segmentation instead of the built-in default rules

customSourceRulesPath.title: SRX path for the source
customSourceRulesPath.description: Full path of the SRX document to use for the source

segmentTarget.title: Segment Target Content
segmentTarget.description: Segment the target content before alignment, overriding any existing segmentation

useCustomTargetRules.title: Custom Target Segmentation Rules
useCustomTargetRules.description: Use a custom SRX file for target segmentation instead of the built-in default rules

customTargetRulesPath.title: SRX path for the target
customTargetRulesPath.description: Full path of the SRX document to use for the target

forceSimpleOneToOneAlignment.title: Force Simple One to One Alignment
forceSimpleOneToOneAlignment.description: If the number of sentences are the same then align one to one. Otherwise collapse the sentences and align

toolMeta.description: Aligns sentences within text units (paragraphs). Produces sentence alignments as bilingual text units or a TMX file.

doc.overview: This step aligns sentences within text units (paragraphs) from two synchronized documents. It uses a character-length-based algorithm that accounts for language-specific expansion/contraction ratios, producing alignment match types such as 1-1, 2-1, 1-2, 0-1, 1-0, 2-3, 3-2, etc. The step can optionally segment content using default or custom SRX rules before alignment, and outputs either bilingual text units or a TMX file.

collapseWhitespace.help: Normalizes all whitespace characters (spaces, newlines, tabs, etc.) to a single space before segmentation and alignment. This can improve alignment accuracy when source and target documents have inconsistent whitespace formatting.

customSourceRulesPath.help: Full path to an SRX document defining segmentation rules for the source language.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this path to be used

customTargetRulesPath.help: Full path to an SRX document defining segmentation rules for the target language.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this path to be used

forceSimpleOneToOneAlignment.help:
For each paragraph: if source and target have the **same number of sentences**, aligns them 1-to-1. If the counts differ, joins all sentences in the paragraph back together and outputs the aligned paragraph as a single unit.

This provides a simpler, more conservative alignment strategy.
notes: When enabled, this **overrides** the *Output 1-1 matches only* option.

dependsOn.condition: overrides this option when forceSimpleOneToOneAlignment is enabled

generateTMX.help:
Controls the output mode of the step:

- **Enabled (default)**: Produces a TMX file at the specified path. The step passes through the original events (possibly re-segmented).
- **Disabled**: The step returns bilingual text units corresponding to the alignment (also segmented), replacing the original events in the pipeline.
notes: When generating TMX, the original filter events are passed through unchanged (except for possible re-segmentation). The aligned data goes only to the TMX file.

outputOneTOneMatchesOnly.help: Filters the output to include only 1-to-1 sentence alignments, discarding all other match types (2-1, 1-2, 0-1, 1-0, etc.). Useful when you need high-confidence aligned pairs for TM building.

dependsOn.condition: overrides this option when enabled

segmentSource.description: Segment the source content before alignment, overriding any existing segmentation
segmentSource.help:
Segments source content before alignment using SRX rules. If disabled, the content is expected to be **already segmented** — the aligner will use existing segment boundaries.

If enabled and the content is already segmented, the existing segmentation will be **reset** and replaced with new segmentation.
notes: Enabling this will override any existing segmentation on the source content.

segmentTarget.description: Segment the target content before alignment, overriding any existing segmentation
segmentTarget.help:
Segments target content before alignment using SRX rules. If disabled, the content is expected to be **already segmented** — the aligner will use existing segment boundaries.

If enabled and the content is already segmented, the existing segmentation will be **reset** and replaced with new segmentation.
notes: Enabling this will override any existing segmentation on the target content.

tmxOutputPath.help: Path where the aligned TMX document will be written. If the file already exists it will be **overwritten**.
notes: Warning: Existing files at this path will be overwritten without confirmation.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this path to be used

useCustomSourceRules.description: Use a custom SRX file for source segmentation instead of the built-in default rules
useCustomSourceRules.help: Uses a specified SRX file instead of the built-in default rules for source segmentation.
notes: The default rules are **hard-coded within the step** — they are NOT the rules from the `config` sub-directory of the installation directory.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for custom rules to take effect

useCustomTargetRules.description: Use a custom SRX file for target segmentation instead of the built-in default rules
useCustomTargetRules.help: Uses a specified SRX file instead of the built-in default rules for target segmentation.
notes: The default rules are **hard-coded within the step** — they are NOT the rules from the `config` sub-directory of the installation directory.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for custom rules to take effect

limitations: Currently the events sent by this step are the same as the events it received — aligned text units are not yet passed through the pipeline (only written to TMX). This may be implemented in a future version.
Source and target text units **must be perfectly synchronized** (paragraph-aligned). If the source document has more text units than the target, an error will be generated.
Entries set as non-translatable are not processed.
processingNotes: The alignment algorithm uses character-length ratios with internal parameters that account for language-specific expansion/contraction.
When processing bilingual documents (e.g., TMX, PO), use only a single input list.
The default SRX segmentation rules are **hard-coded within the step** — they are not the rules from the `config` sub-directory of the installation directory.

examples.title: Align and export a TMX
examples.description: Segment both sides with the built-in rules, align sentences, and write the aligned pairs to a TMX file. The original events pass through unchanged apart from re-segmentation; the aligned data goes only to the TMX.
examples.config:
```yaml
generateTMX: true
tmxOutputPath: ./aligned.tmx
segmentSource: true
segmentTarget: true
```

examples.title: High-confidence pairs with custom source rules
examples.description: Use a project-specific SRX file for the source, collapse inconsistent whitespace before aligning, and keep only 1-to-1 sentence matches. The custom-rules path only takes effect when both `segmentSource` and `useCustomSourceRules` are enabled.
examples.config:
```yaml
generateTMX: true
tmxOutputPath: ./aligned.tmx
segmentSource: true
useCustomSourceRules: true
customSourceRulesPath: ./rules/source.srx
collapseWhitespace: true
outputOneTOneMatchesOnly: true
```

entries.description: Simple and fast batch leveraging step that delegates to connectors

schema.title: Simple Batch Leveraging Step
schema.description: Simple and fast batch leveraging step that delegates to connectors

toolMeta.description: Simple and fast batch leveraging step that delegates to connectors

doc.overview: The Batch TM Leveraging step queries a translation-memory connector for matches, but sends text units in batches rather than one at a time. It accumulates text units, dispatches them to the connector's batch-leverage call once a fixed number have been collected (and again for any remainder at the end of each document), attaches the returned matches as alternate-translation annotations, and can fill the target with the best match above a threshold. It is a simpler and faster alternative to the per-segment Leveraging step for connectors that support batch queries.
processingNotes: Text units are buffered and leveraged in batches; the buffer is flushed when it reaches the internal batch size and at the end of each document, so events are emitted as multi-events rather than strictly in place.
Non-translatable text units, entries with no source text, entries already approved (target `approved` property set to `yes`), and entries already handled by Diff Leveraging are skipped.

When target filling is enabled, the best match for a segment is copied into the target only if its combined score meets `fillTargetThreshold` and the existing target is eligible (per `fillIfTargetIsEmpty` / `fillIfTargetIsSameAsSource`).
When `copySourceOnNoText` is enabled, segments whose source has no text (codes/whitespace only) have their source copied into the corresponding target segment.
The connector is instantiated by class name and configured from the step's resource parameters; if it is a TM query it is opened with a fixed maximum of five hits per entry.
Requires source and target locales and the pipeline root directory; these are supplied by the pipeline rather than configured on the step.

entries.description: Annotates each text unit source with a total word count and gives total source word counts for batches Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Simple Word Count
schema.description: Annotates each text unit source with a total word count and gives total source word counts for batches Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Annotates each text unit source with a total word count and gives total source word counts for batches Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Performs word counts on the source content of text units across a set of documents. Designed for fast, simple counting — for more sophisticated counts, see the Word Count Step. Word counts are saved as annotations that can be consumed by downstream steps such as the Translation Comparison Step.
limitations: No known limitations.
processingNotes: Word counts are stored as **annotations** on each text unit, making them available to downstream steps (e.g., Translation Comparison Step can include word counts in its report).
Only counts **source** content — target content is not counted.
This is a lightweight counting step; for locale-aware or customizable counting logic, use the **Word Count Step** instead.

examples.title: Using word count annotations in a pipeline
examples.description:
Place the Simple Word Count step before the Translation Comparison Step so that word count annotations are available for the comparison report.

```
Raw Document → Filter → Simple Word Count → Translation Comparison
```

The Translation Comparison Step will automatically pick up the word count annotations produced by Simple Word Count.

entries.description: Compare and fix spaces around inline codes in target based on the source. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Space Quality Check
schema.description: Compare and fix spaces around inline codes in target based on the source. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Compare and fix spaces around inline codes in target based on the source. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Fixes spaces around target inline codes within text units so that spacing matches the source. If there is a mismatched number of inline codes between source and target, unmatched codes are skipped and the remainder of the text unit is still processed.
processingNotes: When inline code counts differ between source and target, unmatched codes are skipped and the rest of the text unit is still processed — no error is raised.
Spaces are either inserted or removed in the target to match the source spacing pattern around each inline code.

examples.title: Basic space correction around inline codes
examples.description: If the source has `Hello world` (no space before ``) but the target has `Bonjour monde` (extra space before ``), the step removes the extra space in the target to match the source pattern.

entries.description: Reclassify generic markup inline codes into semantic vocabulary types.

doc.overview:
The **Span Classify** tool reclassifies generic `code:markup` inline codes —
placeholders and paired open/close codes — into proper semantic vocabulary
types such as bold, italic, hyperlink, or image. It is designed to run after
readers that emit generic markup codes (for example bridge XLIFF or Okapi
readers), so that later tools and editors see meaningful inline-code types
rather than opaque markup.

Classification uses two strategies: a map of known Okapi code sub-types, and,
failing that, the HTML element name parsed from the code's raw data. When a
semantic type is resolved, the tool also applies the vocabulary's display
form, equivalence text, and editing constraints. Codes it cannot classify are
left as they are. The tool has no configurable parameters.
limitations: Classification covers known Okapi sub-types and common HTML elements; unrecognized codes are left unchanged.
Has no configurable parameters.
processingNotes: Operates on inline-code runs in both source and target segments.
Recurses into plural and select constructs so codes inside them are classified as well.

entries.description: Identify tags and placeholders in source text and mark them for downstream protection.

patterns.title: Protection Patterns
patterns.description: Regex patterns for tags and placeholders to protect

doc.overview:
The **Tag Protect** tool finds tags and placeholders in source text using
regular-expression patterns and records them as an annotation on the block,
along with a count property. Downstream tools — for example an MT or AI
translation step — can then read the annotation to preserve those tokens
rather than translating or reformatting them.

When no patterns are configured, a built-in default set is used, covering
HTML/XML tags, curly-brace placeholders (ICU, Java, .NET), printf-style
placeholders, and `${...}` template expressions. The tool records what it
found; preserving the matches is the job of the consuming tool.

patterns.description: Regular-expression patterns for tags and placeholders to protect.
patterns.help:
A list of regular expressions; any substring of the source matching a
pattern is recorded as a protected tag. When the list is empty, a
built-in default set is used (HTML/XML tags, curly-brace placeholders,
printf-style placeholders, and `${...}` template expressions).
patterns.values: array of regular expressions (default: built-in set)

limitations: Patterns use Go's RE2 syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookaround.
The tool records protected matches as an annotation; it does not itself prevent their modification.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Matches are recorded as a block annotation and the match count as a block property; matches are deduplicated and ordered by offset.

examples.title: Protect the default token set
examples.description: Use built-in patterns for tags and common placeholders.

examples.title: Protect only HTML tags
examples.description: Restrict protection to markup tags.

entries.description: Extract a list of possible terms found in the source content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Term Extraction
schema.description: Extract a list of possible terms found in the source content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

outputPath.title: Output path
outputPath.description: Full path of the output file

autoOpen.title: Open the result file after completion
autoOpen.description: Automatically open the result file in the default application after completion

minWordsPerTerm.title: Minimum number of words per term
minWordsPerTerm.description: A term will be made up at least of that many words

maxWordsPerTerm.title: Maximum number of words per term
maxWordsPerTerm.description: A term will be made up at the most of that many words

minOccurrences.title: Minimum number of occurrences per term
minOccurrences.description: A term will have at least that many occurrences

stopWordsPath.title: Path of the file with stop words (leave empty for default)
stopWordsPath.description: Full path of the file containing stop words

notStartWordsPath.title: Path of the file with not-start words (leave empty for default)
notStartWordsPath.description: Full path of the file containing not-start words

notEndWordsPath.title: Path of the file with not-end words (leave empty for default)
notEndWordsPath.description: Full path of the file containing not-end words

keepCase.title: Preserve case differences
keepCase.description: Treat words with different casing as distinct tokens (e.g. 'Term' vs 'term')

removeSubTerms.title: Remove entries that seem to be sub-strings of longer entries
removeSubTerms.description: Remove candidate terms that are sub-sequences of longer retained terms

sortByOccurrence.title: Sort the results by the number of occurrences
sortByOccurrence.description: Sort output by occurrence count instead of alphabetically

useTerminologyAnnotations.title: Use Terminology annotations
useTerminologyAnnotations.description: Include terms from ITS Terminology annotations attached to text units

useTextAnalysisAnnotations.title: Use Text Analysis annotations
useTextAnalysisAnnotations.description: Include terms from Text Analysis annotation spans on source content

useStatistics.title: Use tokens-grouping statistics
useStatistics.description: Find term candidates using frequency-based statistical analysis of word sequences

toolMeta.description: Extract a list of possible terms found in the source content. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Generates an output file containing a list of possible term candidates found in the source content of processed text units. Terms can be discovered through three methods: Terminology annotations, Text Analysis annotations, or simple statistical analysis on groups of tokens. The step processes all input documents before creating the candidate term list, and passes filter events through unchanged to the next step.

autoOpen.description: Automatically open the result file in the default application after completion
autoOpen.help: Automatically opens the generated result file in the system default application after the extraction process completes.

keepCase.description: Treat words with different casing as distinct tokens (e.g. 'Term' vs 'term')
keepCase.help: Controls whether case differences are preserved during token analysis. When enabled, `Term` and `term` are treated as **different** words. When disabled, they are treated as the same word and grouped together.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

maxWordsPerTerm.help: Sets the upper bound on word count for a token sequence to qualify as a term candidate. Keep this to a reasonable maximum — **6 or 7, or less** is recommended.
notes: High values significantly increase processing time and produce many low-quality candidates.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

minOccurrences.help: A word sequence must appear at least this many times across **all input files** to be retained as a term candidate.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

minWordsPerTerm.help: Sets the lower bound on word count for a token sequence to qualify as a term candidate.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

notEndWordsPath.help:
Path to a plain-text file with one word per line. Not-end words are **excluded from the end** of a term but may appear within or at the beginning.

Leave empty to use the **built-in English** not-end word list.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

notStartWordsPath.help:
Path to a plain-text file with one word per line. Not-start words are **excluded from the beginning** of a term but may appear within or at the end.

Leave empty to use the **built-in English** not-start word list.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

outputPath.help:
The generated file is **tab-delimited UTF-8** with one candidate term per line: occurrence count in the first column, term text in the second column.

Supports the `${rootDir}` variable in the path.

removeSubTerms.description: Remove candidate terms that are sub-sequences of longer retained terms
removeSubTerms.help:
Performs an extra post-processing pass that removes candidate terms that appear to be sub-sequences of longer candidates.

For example, given:
- `gnu free` = 6
- `gnu free document` = 6
- `gnu free document license` = 5

The result becomes:
- `gnu free document` = 1
- `gnu free document license` = 5

`gnu free` is removed because all 6 of its occurrences can be accounted for as sub-sequences of longer retained terms. `gnu free document` is kept with count 1 because it occurs once independently of the longer phrase.
notes: **Warning:** May cause loss of valid entries when they only appear as sub-sequences of longer, less relevant entries.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

sortByOccurrence.description: Sort output by occurrence count instead of alphabetically
sortByOccurrence.help: Controls the sort order of the output term list. Results are sorted by descending occurrence count. If not enabled, results are listed **alphabetically**.

stopWordsPath.help:
Path to a plain-text file with one stop word per line. Stop words **break** a word sequence even if the maximum word count has not been reached.

Leave empty to use the **built-in English** stop word list (embedded in the step JAR).
notes: The default English lists are embedded in the compiled resources at `net/sf/okapi/steps/termextraction` inside `okapi-lib-VERSION.jar`.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when enabled (tokens-grouping method)

useStatistics.description: Find term candidates using frequency-based statistical analysis of word sequences
useStatistics.help:
Finds term candidates using simple statistical analysis on groups of tokens (word sequences). A "term" is defined as a sequence of words meeting the configured minimum/maximum word count and minimum occurrence thresholds.

No linguistic processing is applied — words are **not** reduced to their [stems](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem). When enabled, the word count, occurrence, stop-word, and case parameters below become active.
notes: No stemming or lemmatization is performed — purely frequency-based extraction.

useTerminologyAnnotations.description: Include terms from ITS Terminology annotations attached to text units
useTerminologyAnnotations.help:
Extracts terms from `TermsAnnotation` annotations attached to text unit sources. These annotations are created by filters or other steps — for example, by [ITS Terminology data category](http://www.w3.org/TR/its/#terminology) rules when using the XML Filter.

This is one of three independent extraction methods that can be combined.

useTextAnalysisAnnotations.description: Include terms from Text Analysis annotation spans on source content
useTextAnalysisAnnotations.help:
Extracts terms from spans annotated with the Text Analysis annotation on text unit source content. Such annotations are created by steps like the Enrycher Step.

This is one of three independent extraction methods that can be combined.

limitations: None known (per wiki documentation).
processingNotes: The step processes **all** input documents before creating the term list — results are not available until the entire batch completes.
Non-translatable entries are skipped — no term extraction is performed on them.
Filter events pass through to the next step **unchanged**.
The output file is always **UTF-8 encoded**, tab-delimited.
The three extraction methods (Terminology annotations, Text Analysis annotations, tokens-grouping statistics) are independent and can be combined — results are merged into a single output.

examples.title: Basic statistical term extraction
examples.description: Extract multi-word terms appearing at least 3 times, using default English stop words.
examples.config:
```yaml
outputPath: ${rootDir}/terms.txt
useStatistics: true
useTerminologyAnnotations: false
useTextAnalysisAnnotations: false
minWordsPerTerm: 2
maxWordsPerTerm: 5
minOccurrences: 3
removeSubTerms: true
sortByOccurrence: true
```

examples.title: Output format
examples.description: The generated tab-delimited file lists occurrence count and term on each line.
examples.output:
```
12	user interface
8	source document
5	file format
3	translation memory
```

examples.title: Sub-term removal behavior
examples.description: Demonstrates how `removeSubTerms` adjusts counts and removes fully-accounted-for sub-sequences.
examples.input:
```
6	gnu free
6	gnu free document
5	gnu free document license
```
examples.output:
```
1	gnu free document
5	gnu free document license
```

entries.description: Verify that required target terms appear when their source terms are present.

doc.overview:
The **Terminology Check** tool checks each translation against a glossary of
source/target term pairs. For every entry, if the source term occurs in the
block's source text, the tool verifies that the required target term occurs in
the translation; when it does not, the block is flagged and the missing
expectations are recorded on its properties. A target locale is required, and a
termbase or glossary supplies the term pairs.

The glossary is supplied programmatically rather than as a schema field, so the
tool is typically wired up by the host application or a project that binds a
termbase. The only schema-level option controls whether term matching respects
letter case.

caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, source and target terms must match exactly, including letter
case. When disabled, matching is case-insensitive, so a glossary term
matches regardless of how it is capitalised in the text.

limitations: The glossary of term pairs is configured programmatically; it is not exposed as a schema parameter.
Matching is substring-based; it does not account for inflection or morphology.
Requires a configured target locale and a termbase; blocks without a translation are skipped.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Only entries whose source term is present in the block's source are checked.

examples.title: Case-insensitive term matching
examples.description: Match glossary terms regardless of capitalisation.

entries.description: Leverage terminology into the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Terminology Leveraging
schema.description: Leverage terminology into the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

leverage.title: Leverage status:
leverage.description: Indication that leveraging should be done

annotateSource.title: Annotate source:
annotateSource.description: Indication that source segment annotation should be done

annotateTarget.title: Annotate target
annotateTarget.description: Indication that target segment annotation should be done

connectorClassName.title: Connector class
connectorClassName.description: Full class name of the terminology connector

connectorParameters.title: Connector parameters
connectorParameters.description: String representation of parameters specific to terminology connector

toolMeta.description: Leverage terminology into the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: This step leverages glossary terms into extracted text units by querying a terminology resource connector. It sends segment content to the resource, which returns matching glossary entries that are then annotated onto source and/or target segments. Non-translatable text units are automatically skipped. Requires both source and target languages to be set.

annotateSource.help:
Annotates the source segment text where a glossary term is found. The connector locates terms within the source content and the matching glossary entries are attached as inline annotations on the source.

Enable this when downstream steps or output formats need source-side terminology markup.

annotateTarget.help:
Annotates the target segment text where a translation of a glossary term is found. Annotation is applied when the connector locates a known term translation within the target content.

Requires a target to be present on the text unit; non-translatable units are skipped.

connectorClassName.help: Fully qualified class name of the terminology resource connector to query. The connector must extend `BaseTerminologyConnector` or implement the `ITerminologyQuery` interface, and the class must be available on the runtime classpath.

connectorParameters.help: Serialized parameter string passed to the connector named by `connectorClassName`. The format and meaning of the string are defined entirely by that connector (for example, endpoint URLs or credentials).

dependsOn.condition: interpreted by the connector named here

leverage.help:
Enables or disables terminology leveraging for the step. When disabled, the step is a no-op, which allows it to be left in a pipeline and toggled on or off without removing it.

When enabled, segment content is sent to the configured connector and matching glossary entries are annotated onto the segments.

limitations: It is **not** possible to chain several steps to leverage against multiple terminology resources.
processingNotes: Text units flagged as non-translatable are automatically skipped.
Source annotation occurs when a glossary term is found within the source segment text.
Target annotation occurs when a translation of a glossary term is found within the target segment text.
The terminology resource connector defines its own strategy for generating glossary entry IDs and glossary translation IDs.

examples.title: Annotate source segments from a connector
examples.description: Enable leveraging and annotate source text where glossary terms are found, naming the terminology connector to query. The connector class must extend `BaseTerminologyConnector` or implement `ITerminologyQuery`.

examples.title: Annotate both sides with connector parameters
examples.description: Annotate source and target segments, passing the connector its own settings as a serialized parameter string. Target annotation is applied when a translation of a term is found in the target text.
examples.config:
```yaml
leverage: true
annotateSource: true
annotateTarget: true
connectorClassName: net.sf.okapi.connectors.example.GlossaryConnector
connectorParameters: "endpoint=https://terms.example.org/api"
```

entries.description: Apply various modifications to the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Text Modification
schema.description: Apply various modifications to the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

applyToBlankEntries.title: Modify Blank Entries
applyToBlankEntries.description: Apply modifications also to text units without text content.

expand.title: Expand Text
expand.description: Expand text length to simulate translation growth for UI testing.

script.title: Replacement Script
script.description: Script variant used for character replacement modes.

type.title: Type of Change
type.description: Select the kind of text modification to apply to each text unit.

addPrefix.title: Add Prefix
addPrefix.description: Add a prefix string at the start of each text unit.

prefix.title: Prefix Text
prefix.description: The prefix text to prepend to each text unit.

addSuffix.title: Add Suffix
addSuffix.description: Add a suffix string at the end of each text unit.

suffix.title: Suffix Text
suffix.description: The suffix text to append to each text unit.

applyToExistingTarget.title: Modify Existing Translations
applyToExistingTarget.description: Apply modifications also to text units that already have a translation.

addName.title: Append Item Name
addName.description: Append the name of each text unit at the end of its value.

addID.title: Append Extraction ID
addID.description: Append the extraction ID of each text unit at the end of its value.

markSegments.title: Mark Segment Boundaries
markSegments.description: Add '[' and ']' delimiters around segments within each text unit.

toolMeta.description: Apply various modifications to the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Modifies the content of text units by applying various transformations such as character replacement, pseudo-localization, prefix/suffix addition, and text expansion. Text units marked as non-translatable are skipped. Useful for pseudo-localization testing, generating placeholder translations, and verifying that UI layouts can accommodate longer strings.

addID.description: Append the extraction ID of each text unit at the end of its value.
addID.help: Appends the **extraction ID** of each text unit at the end of its value. Extraction IDs are assigned by the filter and are filter-specific. Useful for debugging filter output or tracing text units back to their source location.

addName.description: Append the name of each text unit at the end of its value.
addName.help: Appends the **name** (resource key) of each text unit at the end of its value. If the text unit has no name, the extraction ID is appended instead. Useful for identifying which resource key corresponds to which visible string during testing.

addPrefix.description: Add a prefix string at the start of each text unit.
addPrefix.help: Adds the configured prefix string at the start of each text unit. Commonly used with bracket markers like `{START_` to visually identify translated/pseudo-localized strings in the UI.

dependsOn.condition: provides the prefix text to prepend

addSuffix.description: Add a suffix string at the end of each text unit.
addSuffix.help: Adds the configured suffix string at the end of each text unit. Paired with a prefix, this helps testers visually identify which strings have been processed.

dependsOn.condition: provides the suffix text to append

applyToBlankEntries.description: Apply modifications also to text units without text content.
applyToBlankEntries.help: Controls whether modifications are applied to text units that have no actual text content — i.e., entries that are empty or contain only whitespace and/or inline codes. Disable to skip blank entries.

applyToExistingTarget.description: Apply modifications also to text units that already have a translation.
applyToExistingTarget.help: By default, text units that already have a translation are left unchanged. Enable this to apply modifications even when a target translation exists (e.g., in multilingual file formats like XLIFF or PO files with existing translations).

expand.description: Expand text length to simulate translation growth for UI testing.
expand.help:
Expands the text to simulate translation length growth, which is useful for testing UI layout capacity:

- Content **≤ 30 characters**: expanded by **50%** (minimum 1 character added)
- Content **> 30 characters**: expanded by **100%**
- Empty strings are **not** expanded
notes: Expansion is applied in addition to any other modifications (prefix, suffix, character replacement, etc.).

markSegments.description: Add '[' and ']' delimiters around segments within each text unit.
markSegments.help: Wraps each segment within a text unit with `[` and `]` delimiters. The delimiters are placed around the segment text itself — after any prefix and before any appended name, ID, or suffix. If the text unit is not segmented, the delimiters wrap the full content.

prefix.description: The prefix text to prepend to each text unit.
prefix.help: The text to prepend to each text unit when prefix addition is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this value to be used

script.description: Script variant used for character replacement modes.
script.help: Internal parameter that tracks which script variant is selected for character replacement. This corresponds to the script-specific replacement modes (Extended Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Chinese) within the `type` parameter.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when type is set to a character replacement mode (3–6)

suffix.description: The suffix text to append to each text unit.
suffix.help: The text to append to each text unit when suffix addition is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this value to be used

type.description: Select the kind of text modification to apply to each text unit.
type.help:
Controls which text transformation is applied to each text unit's content:

- **0** — Keep the original text (no modification to content itself; prefix/suffix/name/ID options still apply)
- **1** — Replace letters with `X` and digits with `N` (masks content while preserving length)
- **2** — Remove all text but keep inline codes intact
- **3** — Replace ASCII characters with Extended Latin characters
- **4** — Replace ASCII characters with Cyrillic characters
- **5** — Replace ASCII characters with Arabic characters
- **6** — Replace ASCII characters with Chinese characters

Options 3–6 perform visual character substitution for pseudo-localization — the result has no linguistic meaning.
type.values: Integer `0`–`6`. Each value selects a different transformation mode.
notes: Character substitution (types 3–6) does **not** perform translation, transliteration, or any meaningful linguistic operation — it simply swaps characters for visual testing purposes.

limitations: None known per the Okapi wiki.
processingNotes: Text units marked as non-translatable are always skipped, regardless of settings.
Text units with existing translations are only modified when **Modify Existing Translations** is enabled.
Prefix is added before segment markers; suffix, name, and ID are added after segment markers.
Character substitution modes (Extended Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Chinese) produce visually distinct but linguistically meaningless output.

examples.title: Pseudo-localization with prefix/suffix
examples.description: Wraps all text units with bracket markers and replaces ASCII with Extended Latin characters to simulate a foreign-language translation for UI testing.
examples.config:

entries.description: Set the non-translatable flag to the text units accepted by the text unit filter specified in step's parameters. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Text Unit Filtering
schema.description: Set the non-translatable flag to the text units accepted by the text unit filter specified in step's parameters. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

toolMeta.description: Set the non-translatable flag to the text units accepted by the text unit filter specified in step's parameters. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: The Text Unit Filtering step applies a pluggable text-unit filter to each text unit and, by default, marks the accepted ones as non-translatable. The filter is supplied as a Java class name implementing the framework's text-unit filter interface, letting a pipeline exclude text units that match arbitrary criteria from later translation steps. It expects filter events and passes them through unchanged apart from clearing the translatable flag on matching text units.
processingNotes: Each text unit is offered to the configured filter; accepted units have their translatable flag cleared (set non-translatable), while rejected units are passed through unmodified.
The filter is instantiated by class name (`tuFilterClassName`); if no class is configured the step fails at the start of the batch.

Subclasses can override the handling of accepted text units to do more than clear the translatable flag (for example drop the event or replace it), but the default behaviour is the non-translatable marking described above.

entries.description: Import text into a new or existing Pensieve TM. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: TM Import
schema.description: Import text into a new or existing Pensieve TM. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

tmDirectory.title: Directory of the TM where to import
tmDirectory.description: Full path of directory of the TM where to import

overwriteSameSource.title: Overwrite if source is the same
overwriteSameSource.description: Replace all existing TM entries that have the same source text as the imported entry

toolMeta.description: Import text into a new or existing Pensieve TM. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Imports text units from an input document into a Pensieve TM. Each text unit (or its segments, if segmented) is added to the specified TM. If a text unit lacks text for the target locale, it is skipped. When segment counts differ between source and target, the whole text unit is imported instead of individual segments.

overwriteSameSource.description: Replace all existing TM entries that have the same source text as the imported entry
overwriteSameSource.help:
Controls how duplicate source texts are handled during import. When enabled, if an entry being imported has the same source text as one or more existing TM entries, **all matching entries are replaced** by the new one.

Leave disabled to allow multiple translations for the same source text (i.e., to preserve translation variants).
notes: **Warning:** Enabling this removes *all* existing entries with matching source text, not just one. If you have intentional translation variants for the same source, keep this disabled.

tmDirectory.help:
Path to the directory containing the Pensieve TM. If the TM does not exist, it will be created automatically. If it already exists, new entries are added to the existing ones.

Supports the `${rootDir}` variable in the path.
tmDirectory.values: Absolute or relative directory path. Supports `${rootDir}` variable expansion.
examples: `/data/shared-tm` — absolute path to a shared TM

processingNotes: Text units without text for the specified target locale are silently skipped.
For segmented text units where source and target segment counts differ, the entire text unit is imported as a whole instead of segment-by-segment.
Pass-through step: filter events are forwarded unchanged after import.

examples.title: Basic TM import with root directory variable
examples.description: Import text units into a project-relative TM directory, creating the TM if it doesn't exist.

examples.title: Import with overwrite for updated translations
examples.description: When re-importing updated translations, overwrite existing entries that share the same source text to keep only the latest translation.

entries.description: Pre-fill translations from a translation memory using exact and fuzzy matching.

downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.title: Downgrade Identical Exact Matches
downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.description: Reduce score by 1%% when multiple identical exact matches are returned

fillIfTargetIsEmpty.title: Only If Target Is Empty
fillIfTargetIsEmpty.description: Fill the target only when it has no existing content

fillTarget.title: Fill Target with Translation
fillTarget.description: Copy the best translation candidate into the target content

fillTargetThreshold.title: Fill Target Threshold
fillTargetThreshold.description: Minimum match score required to fill the target

makeTmx.title: Generate TMX Document
makeTmx.description: Create a TMX file with all leveraged matches

noQueryThreshold.title: No-Query Threshold
noQueryThreshold.description: Skip TM query if existing candidate scores at or above this value (101 = always query)

tmxPath.title: TMX Output Path
tmxPath.description: File path for the generated TMX document

doc.overview:
The **TM Leverage** tool queries a translation memory (TM) for each block's
source text and pre-fills the target with the best match. It tries an exact
match first, then a fuzzy match above a configurable threshold, recording the
match score and type (`exact` or `fuzzy`) on the block. It requires a target
language and a TM provider; without a TM the tool has nothing to leverage.

Whether a match is written into the target is governed by a fill threshold
and an "only if empty" option, so high-confidence matches can be applied while
weaker ones are recorded but left for review. A no-query threshold lets the
tool skip the TM lookup when an existing candidate already scores high enough.
Optionally, all leveraged matches can be written to a TMX file.

downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.description: Reduce the score when multiple identical exact matches exist.
downgradeIdenticalBestMatches.help:
When enabled, an exact match is scored 99 instead of 100 if the TM returns
multiple identical best matches, flagging the ambiguity for review.

fillIfTargetIsEmpty.description: Only fill the target when it has no existing content.
fillIfTargetIsEmpty.help:
When enabled, a match is written only if the block's target is currently
empty, so existing translations are never overwritten.

fillTarget.description: Copy the best match into the target.
fillTarget.help:
When enabled, the best qualifying match is written into the block's
target content. When disabled, matches are scored and recorded but the
target is not filled.

fillTargetThreshold.description: Minimum match score required to fill the target.
fillTargetThreshold.help:
A match must score at least this value to be written into the target.
Matches between the fuzzy threshold and this value are recorded but not
filled.

dependsOn.condition: filling must be enabled for this threshold to apply

fuzzyThreshold.description: Minimum score for a fuzzy match to be considered.
fuzzyThreshold.help:
The lowest fuzzy-match score (0-100) the TM lookup will return. Matches
below this score are ignored.

makeTmx.description: Generate a TMX document of leveraged matches.
makeTmx.help:
When enabled, all leveraged matches are written to a TMX file at the
configured output path.

dependsOn.condition: set the output path when generating TMX

noQueryThreshold.description: Skip the TM query when an existing candidate scores high enough.
noQueryThreshold.help:
If the block already carries a match score at or above this value, the TM
lookup is skipped. The default of 101 means the TM is always queried.

tmxPath.description: Output path for the generated TMX document.
tmxPath.help: File path where the TMX document is written when TMX generation is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: TMX generation must be enabled

limitations: Requires a target language and a TM provider; with no TM there is nothing to leverage.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks with a non-empty source; other parts pass through unchanged.
Exact matches are tried before fuzzy matches; the match score and type are recorded as block properties.

examples.title: Apply only high-confidence matches
examples.description: Fill the target from matches scoring 95 or above.

examples.title: Leverage and export to TMX
examples.description: Lower the fuzzy threshold and write matches to a TMX file.

entries.description: Extracts tokens from the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Tokenization Step
schema.description: Extracts tokens from the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

tokenizeSource.title: Tokenize Source
tokenizeSource.description: Create selected tokens for source locale

tokenizeTargets.title: Tokenize Targets
tokenizeTargets.description: Create selected tokens for target locales

filteredLocales.title: Locales to Filter
filteredLocales.description: Locale names or patterns used that are excluded from tokenization

filterLocales.title: Locales to Filter
filterLocales.description: Locale names or patterns excluded from tokenization.

WORD.title: WORD
WORD.description: A run of characters constituting a word of the given language.

HYPHENATED_WORD.title: HYPHENATED_WORD
HYPHENATED_WORD.description: A word including hyphens of various types

NUMBER.title: NUMBER
NUMBER.description: Numbers, including any commas or points symbols.

WHITESPACE.title: WHITESPACE
WHITESPACE.description: Whitespace characters as defined by the Unicode Consortium standards.

PUNCTUATION.title: PUNCTUATION
PUNCTUATION.description: Punctuation characters as defined by the Unicode Consortium standards.

TIME.title: TIME
TIME.description: Time separated by either ":" or "." (24 hour, 12 hour with AM or PM).

ABBREVIATION.title: ABBREVIATION
ABBREVIATION.description: Limited types of English abbreviations like pct in 3.3pct, U.S., USD.

MARKUP.title: MARKUP
MARKUP.description: A run that begins with "<" and ends with ">" like in HTML and XML.

INTERNET.title: INTERNET
INTERNET.description: An Internet address (URI or IP): http://www.somesite.org/foo/index.html, 192.168.0.5.

EMOJI.title: EMOJI
EMOJI.description: All emoji characters defined in Unicode

IDEOGRAM.title: IDEOGRAM
IDEOGRAM.description: Ideograms as defined by the Unicode Consortium standards.

toolMeta.description: Extracts tokens from the text units content of a document. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: The Tokenization step creates annotations containing different tokenized forms of text units in a document. It recognizes 18+ token types (words, numbers, URLs, emails, emoji, CJK characters, etc.) using Unicode-aware and language-specific rules. Tokenization annotations are attached to filter events and can be consumed by downstream pipeline steps or external tools.

ABBREVIATION.help: Matches a limited set of English-style abbreviations such as `pct` in `3.3pct`, `U.S.`, and `USD`.
notes: Recognition is limited to English abbreviation patterns. Non-English abbreviations may not be matched.

CURRENCY.help: Matches monetary sums in various currencies.
notes: The wiki and HTML help mention this is limited to **US dollars**, though the schema description says "various currencies". Actual coverage may vary by Okapi version.

DATE.values: Only the `MM/DD/YYYY` format is recognized. Other date formats (e.g., `YYYY-MM-DD`, `DD/MM/YYYY`) are **not** matched.
notes: Only US-style `MM/DD/YYYY` dates are recognized. International date formats require custom tokenization.

EMOJI.help: Matches all emoji characters as defined in the Unicode standard, including multi-codepoint sequences.

HYPHENATED_WORD.help: Matches words that include hyphens of various types (e.g., `well-known`, `state-of-the-art`). Captures the entire hyphenated compound as a single token rather than splitting at hyphens.

IDEOGRAM.help: Matches ideographic characters as defined by Unicode — primarily CJK unified ideographs (Chinese characters / Kanji). Each ideogram is typically a single token.

INTERNET.help: Matches Internet addresses including URIs and IP addresses.

KANA.help: Matches Japanese Hiragana and Katakana character runs. Useful for CJK text processing where you need to distinguish phonetic scripts from ideographic content.

MARKUP.help: Matches runs of text that begin with `<` and end with `>`, capturing HTML/XML-style tags as single tokens.
notes:
This performs simple delimiter matching, not full XML parsing. Self-closing tags like `
` and tags with attributes are matched.

NUMBER.help: Matches numeric values including those with comma or period separators (e.g., `1,234.56`, `3.14`).

OTHER_SYMBOL.help: Matches Unicode symbols in the `S` general category — mathematical operators, technical symbols, and other miscellaneous symbols not covered by other token types.

PUNCTUATION.help: Matches punctuation characters as classified by Unicode general category `P` (connectors, dashes, open/close brackets, quotes, etc.).

TIME.help: Matches time expressions separated by `:` or `.` in both 24-hour and 12-hour (with AM/PM) formats.

WHITESPACE.help: Matches whitespace characters as classified by Unicode — spaces, tabs, line breaks, and other separator characters.

WORD.help: Matches runs of characters that constitute a word in the given language. Word boundaries are determined by the locale's tokenizer rules, so results vary by language.

tokenizeSource.help: Tokenizes the content of the **source** locale. At least one of `tokenizeSource` or `tokenizeTargets` should be enabled for the step to produce output.

tokenizeTargets.help: Tokenizes the content of all **target** locales. Useful when you need token-level analysis of translated content for quality checks or alignment.

limitations: Only the `MM/DD/YYYY` date format is recognized by the DATE token type.
ABBREVIATION recognition is limited to English-style patterns.
The wiki mentions COMPANY and STOPWORD token types that are not exposed in the current schema — these may be available only in certain Okapi versions or through direct configuration.
processingNotes: Token annotations are attached to the filter events and passed downstream — they do not modify the text content itself.
All token type booleans default to `false`. If no token types are selected, all available token types are generated.
The step requires both source and target languages to be configured in the pipeline.
The wiki documents additional token types (`COMPANY`, `STOPWORD`) and a locale filter parameter not present in the current schema.

examples.title: Extract words and numbers from source
examples.description: Tokenize the source content to extract word and number tokens for downstream analysis.
examples.config:
```yaml
tokenizeSource: true
tokenizeTargets: false
WORD: true
NUMBER: true
```

examples.title: Full tokenization of source and targets
examples.description: Enable all common token types on both source and target content for quality assurance or alignment.
examples.config:
```yaml
tokenizeSource: true
tokenizeTargets: true
WORD: true
NUMBER: true
PUNCTUATION: true
WHITESPACE: true
INTERNET: true
EMAIL: true
EMOJI: true
```

examples.title: CJK-focused tokenization
examples.description: Tokenize Japanese/Chinese content focusing on ideographic and kana script detection.
examples.config:
```yaml
tokenizeSource: true
WORD: true
KANA: true
IDEOGRAM: true
```

entries.description: Compare two target translations of the same source and report whether they match.

caseSensitive.title: Case Sensitive
caseSensitive.description: Take case differences into account when comparing

document1Label.title: Document 1 Label
document1Label.description: Label for the first translation in reports

document2Label.title: Document 2 Label
document2Label.description: Label for the second translation in reports

genericCodes.title: Use Generic Codes
genericCodes.description: Use generic numbered tags (e.g. <1>...</1>) instead of original inline codes in reports

punctuationSensitive.title: Punctuation Sensitive
punctuationSensitive.description: Take punctuation differences into account when comparing

whitespaceSensitive.title: Whitespace Sensitive
whitespaceSensitive.description: Take whitespace differences into account when comparing

doc.overview:
The **Translation Comparison** tool compares the translations of a block in two
target locales (or two versions) and records the result on the block's
properties: a status — identical, different, or one of the missing-translation
cases — and a human-readable diff description. When a translation is absent for
one or both locales, the status reflects which side is missing.

Comparison sensitivity is configurable: case, whitespace, and punctuation can
each be ignored so that cosmetic differences do not register as a mismatch. The
two locales being compared are supplied programmatically; the parameters below
control how the comparison is performed and how the translations are labelled
in the report.

caseSensitive.description: Take case differences into account when comparing.
caseSensitive.help:
When enabled, translations that differ only in letter case count as
different. Disable it to compare case-insensitively.

document1Label.description: Label for the first translation in reports.
document1Label.help:
The name used to identify the first translation in the diff description. If
left empty, the first locale's identifier is used instead.

document2Label.description: Label for the second translation in reports.
document2Label.help:
The name used to identify the second translation in the diff description. If
left empty, the second locale's identifier is used instead.

genericCodes.description: Use generic numbered tags instead of original inline codes in reports.
genericCodes.help:
When enabled, inline codes are represented in the report as generic numbered
tags (for example `<1>...</1>`) rather than their original markup, which
keeps the diff readable.

punctuationSensitive.description: Take punctuation differences into account when comparing.
punctuationSensitive.help:
When enabled, punctuation differences count as a mismatch. When disabled,
punctuation characters are stripped from both sides before comparison.

whitespaceSensitive.description: Take whitespace differences into account when comparing.
whitespaceSensitive.help:
When enabled, differences in whitespace count as a mismatch. When disabled,
runs of whitespace are collapsed to a single space and trimmed before
comparison, so spacing differences are ignored.

limitations: Compares two target locales of the same block; it does not compare against an external reference file.
A block is only compared when it carries translations for the configured locales; otherwise a missing-translation status is recorded.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
The two locales to compare are configured programmatically, not through the parameters above.

examples.title: Ignore cosmetic differences
examples.description: Treat translations that differ only in case, whitespace, or punctuation as identical.
examples.config:
caseSensitive: false
whitespaceSensitive: false
punctuationSensitive: false

examples.title: Label the compared translations
examples.description: Use meaningful labels for the two translations in the diff output.
examples.config:
document1Label: Vendor A
document2Label: Vendor B

tmxPath.description: Full path of the output TMX file

target3Suffix.title: Suffix for target language code of document 3
target3Suffix.description: Suffix appended to the target language code for document 3 entries in TMX output

document1Label.description: Label used in reports to identify the base translation

document2Label.title: Label for the document 2
document2Label.description: Label used in reports to identify the second translation

document3Label.description: Label used in reports to identify the optional third translation

useDataLog.description: Appends average comparison scores to a tab-delimited log file after each run

dataLogPath.title: Log file
dataLogPath.description: Full path for the log file

altTransOrigin.title: Value in origin attribute
