useAltTrans.title: Use alt-trans for document 1
useAltTrans.description: Use the XLIFF alt-trans of the trans-unit

toolMeta.description: Compare the translated text units between several documents. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Compares translations between two or three documents, calculating similarity scores (ED-Score based on Levenshtein distance, and FM-Score based on Sørensen-Dice coefficient with 3-grams) for each text unit. The input documents **must have the same number of text units** in the **same order**. Outputs HTML reports with per-segment scores and summaries, and optionally a TMX document and tab-delimited score files.

altTransOrigin.help:
The value of the `origin` attribute on the `<alt-trans>` element to match. Only alt-trans elements with this exact origin value are used as the baseline.

Trans-units without a matching alt-trans are **excluded** from the report.
altTransOrigin.values: String matching the `origin` attribute in `<alt-trans origin="...">`. Common values: `BING`, `Google`, `DeepL`, or custom engine identifiers.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this setting to have any effect

autoOpen.description: Opens the first HTML result file in the default browser after completion
autoOpen.help: Automatically opens the first generated HTML result file in the default browser when processing completes. Useful for quick review of comparison results.

caseSensitive.help: Controls whether uppercase/lowercase differences affect the similarity scores. Disabling this treats `Hello` and `hello` as identical for scoring purposes.

dataLogPath.help:
Full path to the tab-delimited log file where average scores are appended.

Each line contains: UTC timestamp, base document path, second document path, ED-score average, FM-score average.

document1Label.description: Label used in reports to identify the base translation
document1Label.help: Label displayed in the HTML report and TMX output to identify the base (reference) translation. Helps distinguish translations when reviewing comparison results.

document2Label.description: Label used in reports to identify the second translation
document2Label.help: Label displayed in the HTML report and TMX output to identify the second translation being compared against the baseline.

document3Label.description: Label used in reports to identify the optional third translation
document3Label.help: Label displayed in the HTML report and TMX output to identify the optional third translation being compared against the baseline.

generateHTML.help:
Creates one HTML file per input document listing source text, translation text, and comparison scores (both ED-Score and FM-Score).

The HTML file name is the name of the first document plus `.html`.

Also generates a **tab-delimited `.txt` file** with per-entry scores alongside the HTML report.

generateTMX.help:
Creates a single TMX document containing all compared translations and their scores across all input documents.

The TMX path field supports variables `${rootDir}`, `${inputRootDir}`, and locale variables (`${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}`, etc.).

genericCodes.description: Uses generic numbered tags (e.g. <1>...</1>) instead of original inline codes in the HTML report
genericCodes.help:
Controls how inline codes are displayed in the HTML report.

- **Enabled**: uses generic numbered tags like `<1>...</1>`
- **Disabled**: shows the original codes from the document

Original codes from some formats can be long and complicated, making the report harder to read.
notes: Some formats may have very long and complex original inline codes — generic representation significantly improves readability in those cases.

punctuationSensitive.help: Controls whether punctuation differences affect the similarity scores. Disabling this ignores differences like commas, periods, and other punctuation marks.

target2Suffix.description: Suffix appended to the target language code for document 2 entries in TMX output
target2Suffix.help:
Suffix appended to the target language code for document 2 entries in the TMX output. This distinguishes the second translation from the baseline.

If left empty, both target entries will share the same language code and the TMX will only include the last one.
notes: **Important:** If no suffix is provided, both target entries will have the same language code and the TMX document will include only the last one, effectively losing the baseline entry.

target3Suffix.description: Suffix appended to the target language code for document 3 entries in TMX output
target3Suffix.help: Suffix appended to the target language code for document 3 entries in the TMX output. Works the same as the document 2 suffix but for the optional third translation.

tmxPath.help:
Full path for the generated TMX file.

Supports variables:
- `${rootDir}` — project root directory
- `${inputRootDir}` — input root directory
- `${srcLoc}`, `${trgLoc}` — source/target locale variables

useAltTrans.help:
Uses the target from a specific `<alt-trans>` element in an XLIFF document as the baseline translation instead of the main `<target>`.

This enables comparing a human translation with an MT candidate within the **same XLIFF file** — specify the same file for both document 1 and document 2.
notes: Only applies when document 1 is an XLIFF file.
If no matching `<alt-trans>` element is found for a given trans-unit, that trans-unit is excluded from the report.
examples: Compare human translation vs. MT candidate in the same XLIFF: set document 1 and document 2 to the same file, enable this option, and set the origin attribute to the MT engine name (e.g. `BING`).

useDataLog.description: Appends average comparison scores to a tab-delimited log file after each run
useDataLog.help:
Appends average scores for each document to a persistent log file. Useful for tracking comparison results over time across multiple runs.

The log is a **tab-delimited UTF-8 file** with fields: timestamp (UTC), base document path, second document path, edit-distance score, fuzzy score.

whitespaceSensitive.help: Controls whether whitespace differences (extra spaces, tabs, line breaks) affect the similarity scores. Disabling this normalizes whitespace before comparison.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Two similarity metrics are calculated per segment: **ED-Score** (Edit Distance based on Levenshtein distance) and **FM-Score** (Fuzzy Match based on Sørensen-Dice coefficient with 3-grams). Both range from 0 (completely different) to 100 (identical).

The summary section includes an **Edit Effort Score** (0–100) representing post-editing effort: `100 - (Avg ED-Score by word + Avg FM-Score by word) / 2`. Higher values mean more editing effort was needed.
Neither score is very accurate on short segments. Use word count annotations (via the Simple Word Count Step or Word Count Step placed before this step) for per-segment word counts in the report.
Input documents **must have the same number of text units** in the **same order** — different events between text units are acceptable.
When HTML output is enabled, a tab-delimited `.txt` file with per-entry scores is also generated alongside each HTML report.

examples.title: Compare human translation with MT output
examples.description: Compare a human-translated document against a machine translation, outputting both HTML and TMX reports with labeled translations.
examples.config:
```yaml
generateHTML: true
autoOpen: true
genericCodes: true
generateTMX: true
tmxPath: "${rootDir}/comparison.tmx"
target2Suffix: "-mt"
document1Label: "Human"
document2Label: "MT Output"
caseSensitive: true
whitespaceSensitive: false
punctuationSensitive: true
```

examples.title: Compare MT candidate within same XLIFF file
examples.description: Use `alt-trans` to compare the linguist's translation in the main `<target>` against a Bing MT candidate stored in `<alt-trans>` within the same XLIFF file. Specify the same file as both document 1 and document 2.
examples.config:
```yaml
useAltTrans: true
altTransOrigin: "BING"
generateHTML: true
document1Label: "MT Candidate"
document2Label: "Human Translation"
```

examples.title: Track comparison scores over time
examples.description: Append average scores to a persistent log file for tracking translation quality trends across multiple comparison runs.

entries.description: Rebuilds previously split TTX documents into their original documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: TTX Joiner
schema.description: Rebuilds previously split TTX documents into their original documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

suffix.title: Optional suffix to place at the end of the result files
suffix.description: Marker text appended to the output filename to distinguish joined files from the original split parts.

toolMeta.description: Rebuilds previously split TTX documents into their original documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Re-joins multiple TTX documents that were previously split (by the TTX Splitter Step) back into single documents. The step sorts input files by their part markers, groups them by original filename, and concatenates each group into one output TTX file placed in the same directory as the input documents.

suffix.description: Marker text appended to the output filename to distinguish joined files from the original split parts.
suffix.help:
A marker text appended to the end of the output filename (before the `.ttx` extension area). The suffix distinguishes joined output files from the original split parts.

If a file with the resulting name already exists in the output directory, it will be **overwritten without warning**.
notes: **Warning:** Existing files with the same output name will be silently overwritten — no confirmation or backup is made.

limitations: This step is triggered at the **END_BATCH** event, not per-document — all input files must be available before processing begins.
Input files with no part marker or an invalid part marker are **ignored** with a warning.
Existing output files with the same name are overwritten without warning.
processingNotes: The step internally sorts all input files so parts are processed in the correct order, regardless of the order they appear in the input list.
Files are grouped by their original filename (before the part marker), and each group is joined into a single output file.
The output file is written to the same directory as the input documents.
The received pipeline event is passed through unaltered.

examples.title: Joining split TTX files with a custom suffix
examples.description: Given four TTX parts from two original files, the joiner sorts them, groups by original filename, and produces two joined output files with the `_CONCAT` suffix.

entries.description: Splits a TTX document into several ones of roughly equal word count. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: TTX Splitter
schema.description: Splits a TTX document into several ones of roughly equal word count. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

partCount.title: Number of output files
partCount.description: The number of output files to create from one input document, split by roughly equal word count.

toolMeta.description: Splits a TTX document into several ones of roughly equal word count. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Splits a single TTX document into a given number of smaller documents of roughly equal size, based on the total source word count of the original input. The output files are written to the same location as the input document with an extra marker and number appended to the filename. This naming convention is used by the **TTX Joiner Step** to reassemble the split files later.

partCount.description: The number of output files to create from one input document, split by roughly equal word count.
partCount.help:
Controls how many output files are created from one input document. The step calculates roughly equal word-count splits based on the total source word count of the original TTX file.

The output files are written alongside the input document with an extra marker and sequential number appended to the filename. This naming convention is required by the **TTX Joiner Step** to reassemble the parts.
partCount.values: Integer >= 2. Each output file will contain approximately `total_word_count / partCount` source words.
notes: The split is based on source word count, so individual files may differ slightly in segment count.

limitations: Works only on **segmented** TTX files.
The received event is passed through unaltered — the split documents are output as separate files alongside the original.
processingNotes: Output files are written to the same directory as the input document with an appended marker and number suffix.
The filename convention used by the splitter is required by the **TTX Joiner Step** to reassemble documents.
Split size is calculated from total source word count, aiming for roughly equal parts.

examples.title: Split a TTX into 3 parts
examples.description: Splitting a segmented TTX file with 3000 source words into 3 output files. Each output file will contain approximately 1000 source words. The files are named with a marker and number suffix so the TTX Joiner Step can reassemble them.

entries.description: Restore original values into redacted content after processing.

schema.title: Unredact
schema.description: Restore original values into redacted content after processing

toolMeta.description: Restore original values into redacted content after processing

doc.overview:
The **Unredact** tool reverses `redact`: it restores the original values into
the redaction placeholders across a block's source and target runs, then
removes the in-process secret annotation so nothing sensitive reaches the
writer. It is the closing half of the redact → process → unredact cycle, run
after the protected content has been processed (for example, translated).

Restoration uses the recovery record left by `redact`. By default it reads the
in-process secret annotation on the block; in external mode it reads originals
from a sidecar vault (used by the `extract` → `merge` workflow). Both an
ID-based restore (for structure-preserving formats and inline codes) and a
text-based restore (for formats that flattened a placeholder to its visible
string on write) are applied, so placeholders are recovered in either case.
The vault path is supplied programmatically by the caller rather than as a
schema field, so this tool exposes no schema parameters.
limitations: Exposes no schema parameters; the optional sidecar vault path is supplied programmatically by callers (e.g. extract → merge).
Restores only placeholders recorded by a preceding `redact` run; there is nothing to restore without that recovery record.
processingNotes: A source-transform that rewrites both source and target runs to restore originals.
Reads the in-process secret annotation by default, or a sidecar vault in external mode.
Removes the secret annotation after restoring, so sensitive originals never reach the writer.

entries.description: Encodes or Decodes URI escape sequences. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: URI Conversion
schema.description: Encodes or Decodes URI escape sequences. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

conversionType.title: Conversion Direction
conversionType.description: Whether to un-escape (decode) or escape (encode) URI sequences.

updateAll.title: Escape All Extended Characters
updateAll.description: Convert all non-ASCII characters to URI escape sequences.

escapeList.title: Characters to Escape
escapeList.description: The set of characters to convert to URI escape sequences.

toolMeta.description: Encodes or Decodes URI escape sequences. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Converts translatable content of input documents to and from [URI](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/URI) escape notation. Can either un-escape URI escape sequences back to normal text, or escape normal text characters into URI percent-encoded sequences. The set of characters to escape is configurable, with presets based on [RFC 2396](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2396) mark and reserved character classes.

conversionType.description: Whether to un-escape (decode) or escape (encode) URI sequences.
conversionType.help:
Controls the direction of URI conversion:

- **0** — Un-escape URI escape sequences (e.g. `%20` → space). Converts percent-encoded sequences back into normal text.
- **1** — Escape content to URI escape sequences (e.g. `&` → `%26`). Converts characters in the escape list into percent-encoded form.

When escaping, which characters get converted is controlled by `escapeList` and `updateAll`.

escapeList.description: The set of characters to convert to URI escape sequences.
escapeList.help:
The set of characters that will be converted to URI escape sequences when escaping. Each character in the string is escaped individually.

The `%` character is **always** escaped regardless of whether it appears in this list.

Common presets based on [RFC 2396](http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2396):

- **All But Marks** — escape everything except RFC 2396 "marks": `-`, `_`, `.`, `!`, `~`, `*`, `'`, `(`, `)`
- **All But Marks And Reserved** — escape everything except marks and RFC 2396 "reserved" characters: `;`, `/`, `?`, `:`, `@`, `&`, `=`, `+`, `$`, `,`
escapeList.values: A string where each character represents a character to be escaped. The `%` character is always implicitly included.
examples: `%{}[]()&` — default set, escapes braces, brackets, parentheses, ampersand, and percent
Include `<` and `>` to escape angle brackets for HTML-safe URIs

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when conversionType is 1 (escape mode)

updateAll.description: Convert all non-ASCII characters to URI escape sequences.
updateAll.help:
Escapes all non-ASCII (extended) characters to URI escape sequences using UTF-8 percent-encoding.

This applies in addition to the characters specified in the `escapeList`. Useful when the target system only supports ASCII and all extended characters must be percent-encoded.

dependsOn.condition: only meaningful when conversionType is 1 (escape mode)

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The `%` character is always escaped when in escape mode, regardless of the escape list configuration.
Extended (non-ASCII) characters are encoded using UTF-8 percent-encoding when `updateAll` is enabled.
The step operates on translatable content within filter events — non-translatable content passes through unchanged.

examples.title: Decode URI-encoded content
examples.description: Un-escape percent-encoded sequences in translatable text back to readable characters.

examples.title: Encode content for URI safety
examples.description: Escape special characters in translatable text to URI percent-encoded form using the default escape list.

entries.description: Encode or decode URI percent-escape sequences in block text.

doc.overview:
The **URI Convert** tool encodes or decodes URI percent-escape sequences in
block text. Decoding turns `%XX` escapes back into their characters; encoding
replaces reserved characters with percent escapes. Use it to make URL-encoded
content readable for translation, or to re-encode it for output.

By default the tool decodes the target text for the configured locale; a
target locale is required when applying to the target.

applySource.description: Apply the conversion to source text.
applySource.help: When enabled, the source text is encoded or decoded.

applyTarget.description: Apply the conversion to target text.
applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the target text is converted for the configured locale.
Only existing targets are touched.

dependsOn.condition: required when applyTarget is enabled

mode.help:
`decode` converts `%XX` escape sequences back to their characters;
`encode` replaces reserved characters with percent escapes.

targetLocale.help: The locale whose target text is converted. Required when `applyTarget` is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this to take effect

processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
Conversion uses path-style escaping; decoding malformed escape sequences fails the run.

examples.title: Decode percent escapes in the target
examples.description: Make URL-encoded target text readable.
examples.config:
mode: decode
applyTarget: true
targetLocale: fr-FR

examples.title: Encode source text
examples.description: Percent-encode reserved characters in the source.

entries.description: Generate a list of all the characters in the source content of a set of documents. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Used Characters Listing
schema.description: Generate a list of all the characters in the source content of a set of documents. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

outputPath.title: Path of the result file
outputPath.description: Full path of the result file.

autoOpen.title: Open the result file after completion
autoOpen.description: Automatically open the result file in the default editor after processing completes.

toolMeta.description: Generate a list of all the characters in the source content of a set of documents. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Generates a list of all characters used in the translatable text of a given set of documents. The output is a tab-delimited text file listing each character's Unicode code point, its visual representation, and occurrence count. Useful for creating minimal bitmap fonts (e.g., Japanese game interfaces) by including only the characters actually present in the source content.

autoOpen.description: Automatically open the result file in the default editor after processing completes.
autoOpen.help: Automatically opens the result file in the system's default text editor after processing completes. Convenient for quick inspection of the character list during interactive use; disable for batch/automated pipelines.

outputPath.help:
Full path where the tab-delimited character listing file will be written.

The output file contains three tab-separated columns per line:
1. Unicode code point (e.g., `U+2192`)
2. Character representation (e.g., `'→'`)
3. Number of occurrences

The file is always UTF-8 encoded.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Text units marked as non-translatable are ignored — only translatable content is scanned.
The output list is **not in any specific order**.
The step passes filter events through unchanged — it can be inserted into a pipeline without affecting downstream steps.

examples.title: Character listing output format
examples.description: Example of the tab-delimited output file showing Unicode code points, character representations, and occurrence counts.

entries.description: Normalize and fix whitespace issues in target translations, including punctuation spacing.

includeHorizontalWS.title: Horizontal Tabs
includeHorizontalWS.description: Include horizontal tab characters in corrections

includeVerticalWS.title: Vertical White Space
includeVerticalWS.description: Include vertical whitespace (line feeds and carriage returns) in corrections

matchSourceWhitespace.title: Match Source Whitespace
matchSourceWhitespace.description: Copy source leading/trailing whitespace to target

normalizeSpaces.title: Normalize Spaces
normalizeSpaces.description: Collapse multiple spaces to a single space

trimLeading.title: Trim Leading Whitespace
trimLeading.description: Remove leading whitespace from target text

trimTrailing.title: Trim Trailing Whitespace
trimTrailing.description: Remove trailing whitespace from target text

doc.overview:
The **Whitespace Correct** tool cleans up whitespace in target translations.
It can collapse repeated spaces, trim leading and trailing whitespace, match
the source's leading/trailing whitespace onto the target, remove zero-width
characters, and remove whitespace after specific punctuation marks. The last
is particularly useful when translating from a space-delimited language into a
non-space-delimited one such as Chinese or Japanese.

The tool operates only on the target text, so a target locale is required, and
only blocks that already have a target for that locale are processed.

correctComma.help:
When enabled, whitespace following a comma (ASCII `,` or full-width `，`)
is removed, per the included whitespace types.

correctExclamation.help:
When enabled, whitespace following an exclamation mark (ASCII `!` or
full-width `！`) is removed, per the included whitespace types.

correctFullStop.help:
When enabled, whitespace following a full stop (ASCII `.` or full-width
`．`) is removed, per the included whitespace types.

correctQuestion.help:
When enabled, whitespace following a question mark (ASCII `?` or
full-width `？`) is removed, per the included whitespace types.

includeHorizontalWS.help:
When enabled, horizontal tab characters are treated as removable after
punctuation.

includeVerticalWS.help:
When enabled, vertical whitespace — line feeds and carriage returns — is
treated as removable after punctuation.

matchSourceWhitespace.description: Copy the source's leading/trailing whitespace to the target.
matchSourceWhitespace.help:
When enabled, the target's existing leading and trailing whitespace is
stripped and replaced with the source's, so the target mirrors the
source's edge whitespace.

normalizeSpaces.description: Collapse runs of multiple spaces to one.
normalizeSpaces.help: When enabled, consecutive spaces are reduced to a single space.

removeZeroWidthChars.help:
When enabled, zero-width spaces and joiners (and the zero-width no-break
space) are removed from the target.

targetLocale.help:
The locale whose target text is corrected. Required; only blocks with an
existing target for this locale are touched.

trimLeading.description: Remove leading whitespace from the target.
trimLeading.help: When enabled, whitespace at the start of the target text is removed.

trimTrailing.description: Remove trailing whitespace from the target.
trimTrailing.help: When enabled, whitespace at the end of the target text is removed.

processingNotes: Operates on the target text only; a target locale is required and only existing targets are processed.
Punctuation correction removes the whitespace that immediately follows each enabled punctuation mark, limited to the included whitespace types.

examples.title: Normalize spacing for a locale
examples.description: Apply the default whitespace cleanup to a target.

examples.title: Tidy CJK punctuation spacing
examples.description: Remove spaces after punctuation for a non-space-delimited target.
examples.config:
targetLocale: zh-CN
correctFullStop: true
correctComma: true

entries.description: Correct whitespace following segment-ending punctuation when translating from  a space-delimited language (such as English) to a non-space-delimited language (Chinese or Japanese), or vice-versa. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Whitespace Correction
schema.description: Correct whitespace following segment-ending punctuation when translating from  a space-delimited language (such as English) to a non-space-delimited language (Chinese or Japanese), or vice-versa. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

punctuation.title: Punctuation Types
punctuation.description: Punctuation categories to apply whitespace correction to, with full-width CJK to ASCII conversion.

whitespace.title: Whitespace Characters
whitespace.description: Unicode whitespace character categories considered during spacing adjustment.

fullStop.title: Full Stop
fullStop.description: Correct whitespace after full stops (periods) in target text.

comma.title: Comma
comma.description: Correct whitespace after commas in target text.

exclamationPoint.title: Exclamation Point
exclamationPoint.description: Correct whitespace after exclamation marks in target text.

questionMark.title: Question Mark
questionMark.description: Correct whitespace after question marks in target text.

verticalWhitespace.title: Vertical White Space
verticalWhitespace.description: Include vertical whitespace (line feeds, carriage returns) in whitespace to be deleted.

nonbreakingWhitespace.title: Nonbreaking Whitespace
nonbreakingWhitespace.description: Include non-breaking space characters in whitespace to be deleted.

horizontalTabs.title: Horizontal Tabs
horizontalTabs.description: Include horizontal tab characters in whitespace to be deleted.

space.title: Standard White Space
space.description: Include standard space characters in whitespace to be deleted.

other.title: All Other Whitespace
other.description: Include all other whitespace characters in whitespace to be deleted.

toolMeta.description: Correct whitespace following segment-ending punctuation when translating from  a space-delimited language (such as English) to a non-space-delimited language (Chinese or Japanese), or vice-versa. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Simplifies the addition or removal of inter-segment whitespace when translating to or from Chinese or Japanese scripts that do not typically use spaces. When translating from a space-delimited language (e.g., English) to a non-space-delimited language (e.g., Chinese, Japanese), whitespace following segment-ending punctuation is removed. In the reverse direction, whitespace is added. No action is performed between two space-delimited languages or between Chinese and Japanese.

punctuation.description: Punctuation categories to apply whitespace correction to, with full-width CJK to ASCII conversion.
punctuation.help:
Comma-separated list of punctuation categories to apply whitespace correction to. Each category handles conversion between full-width CJK punctuation and their ASCII equivalents:

- **FULL_STOP** — Ideographic Full Stop (U+3002) and Full-width Full Stop (U+FF0E) ↔ period (`.`)
- **COMMA** — Ideographic Comma (U+3001) and Full-width Comma (U+FF0C) ↔ comma (`,`)
- **EXCLAMATION_MARK** — Full-width Exclamation Mark (U+FF01) ↔ `!`
- **QUESTION_MARK** — Full-width Question Mark (U+FF1F) ↔ `?`

The step both converts the punctuation characters and adjusts the trailing whitespace in one operation.
examples: `FULL_STOP,COMMA` — only correct whitespace after periods and commas

whitespace.description: Unicode whitespace character categories considered during spacing adjustment.
whitespace.help:
Comma-separated list of Unicode whitespace character categories that the step will add or remove when adjusting inter-segment spacing. The default includes a comprehensive set of Unicode space characters.

Most users should not need to modify this parameter. Adjust only if you need to restrict which whitespace characters are considered during correction.
whitespace.values: Comma-separated Unicode whitespace category names (e.g., `SPACE`, `NO_BREAK_SPACE`, `IDEOGRAPHIC_SPACE`, `ZERO_WIDTH_SPACE`, etc.)

limitations: Relies on the assumption that each source segment contains a single sentence and has been translated to a single sentence in the target language.
No action is performed when both source and target are space-delimited languages (e.g., English to French).
No action is performed when translating between Chinese and Japanese (both non-space-delimited).
processingNotes: The step converts full-width CJK punctuation to/from ASCII equivalents as part of the whitespace adjustment — it does not only add/remove spaces.
Direction of correction (add vs. remove whitespace) is determined automatically from the source and target locale settings.

examples.title: English to Japanese (remove whitespace)
examples.description: When translating from English (space-delimited) to Japanese (non-space-delimited), trailing whitespace after segment-ending punctuation is removed and ASCII punctuation is converted to full-width equivalents.
examples.output:
```
こんにちは世界。次の文。
```

examples.title: Japanese to English (add whitespace)
examples.description: When translating from Japanese to English, the step adds whitespace after segment-ending punctuation and converts full-width punctuation to ASCII equivalents.
examples.input:
```
こんにちは世界。次の文。
```

entries.description: Count the words in each block's source and target text for scoping and pricing.

countInline.title: Count Inline Codes
countInline.description: Include inline code content in word counts

doc.overview:
The **Word Count** tool tallies the number of words in every translatable
block, reporting per-block counts and a document total. It is the standard
way to scope a localization job — how much text needs translating — and to
drive word-based pricing.

Counting runs over the segmented text. By default both the source and the
target locale are counted, so you can compare untranslated versus translated
volume. Inline codes (placeholders, tags, variables) are excluded from the
count unless you opt in, because they are markup rather than translatable
words.

countInline.description: Include inline-code content in the word counts.
countInline.help:
By default, inline codes — placeholders, variables, and tag content
represented as coded text — are **excluded** from the count, since they
are not translatable words. Enable this only when you specifically want
inline-code text reflected in the totals.

countSource.description: Count words in the source text.
countSource.help:
When enabled, the words in each block's **source** segments are counted.
Disable it if you only need target-side volume (for example, to measure
translated output).

countTarget.description: Count words in the target text.
countTarget.help:
When enabled, the words in each block's **target** segments are counted
for every target locale present. On untranslated content the target
count is zero, which makes this a quick way to see remaining work.

limitations: Counting follows the active segmentation; results depend on how the text was segmented.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.
Target counts are reported per target locale.

examples.title: Count source words only
examples.description: Scope a job by source volume, ignoring any existing translations.

examples.title: Include inline codes
examples.description: Reflect placeholder and tag text in the totals.

entries.description: Count the number of words in the text units of a set of documents or/and in its parts. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

schema.title: Word Count
schema.description: Count the number of words in the text units of a set of documents or/and in its parts. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

countInTextUnits.title: Text Units
countInTextUnits.description: Create word count annotations for text unit resources.

countInBatch.title: Batches
countInBatch.description: Set word count annotation aggregated per batch.

countInBatchItems.title: Batch items
countInBatchItems.description: Set word count annotation per batch item.

countInDocuments.title: Documents
countInDocuments.description: Set word count annotation per document.

countInSubDocuments.title: Sub-documents
countInSubDocuments.description: Set word count annotation per sub-document.

countInGroups.title: Groups
countInGroups.description: Set word count annotation per group.

bufferSize.title: Text Buffer Size
bufferSize.description: Size of the internal text buffer used during word counting (0 for default).

simplifierRules.description: Code simplifier rules for simplifying inline codes before counting.
simplifierRules.title: Simplifier Rules

toolMeta.description: Count the number of words in the text units of a set of documents or/and in its parts. Expects: filter events. Sends back: filter events.

doc.overview: Performs word counts on different parts of a set of documents, saving results as annotations on filter events. Word count annotations are always set on each text unit, with optional aggregation at batch, batch item, document, sub-document, and group levels. The counts follow the [GMX-V 2.0 standard](http://www.xtm-intl.com/manuals/gmx-v/GMX-V-2.0.html) and can be consumed by downstream steps such as the Scoping Report Step.

bufferSize.description: Size of the internal text buffer used during word counting (0 for default).
bufferSize.help:
Controls the size of the internal text buffer used during word counting.

A value of `0` means the default buffer size is used. Only increase this if processing very large text units that exceed the default capacity.
bufferSize.values: `0` = default buffer size; positive integer = explicit buffer size

countInBatch.description: Set word count annotation aggregated per batch.
countInBatch.help:
Adds a word count annotation aggregated across the entire batch. A batch represents the top-level grouping of all input documents in a pipeline run.

The annotation can be consumed by downstream steps such as the **Scoping Report Step** to generate summary reports.

countInBatchItems.description: Set word count annotation per batch item.
countInBatchItems.help:
Adds a word count annotation per batch item. Each batch item typically corresponds to one input file added to the pipeline.

The annotation can be consumed by downstream steps such as the **Scoping Report Step** to generate per-item reports.

countInDocuments.description: Set word count annotation per document.
countInDocuments.help:
Adds a word count annotation per document. A document corresponds to a `START_DOCUMENT` / `END_DOCUMENT` event pair from the filter.

Useful when a single batch item produces multiple document events (e.g., multi-file archives).

countInGroups.description: Set word count annotation per group.
countInGroups.help: Adds a word count annotation per group. Groups are sets of related text units emitted between `START_GROUP` / `END_GROUP` events — for example, table rows, list items, or other structural groupings defined by the filter.

countInSubDocuments.description: Set word count annotation per sub-document.
countInSubDocuments.help: Adds a word count annotation per sub-document. Sub-documents occur when a filter emits nested `START_SUBDOCUMENT` / `END_SUBDOCUMENT` events — for example, embedded content within an HTML or XLIFF file.

limitations: None known (per wiki documentation).
processingNotes: Word count annotations are **always** set on each text unit regardless of the checkbox options — the options control additional aggregation levels.
Word counts follow the **GMX-V 2.0** standard (formerly GMX-V 1.0 / LISA).
Annotations are stored on the filter events and passed downstream — this step does not produce any output files by itself.
Commonly paired with the **Scoping Report Step** which reads the annotations and generates a word count report.

examples.title: Basic word count with scoping report
examples.description: Count words per batch item and document, then generate a report using the Scoping Report Step downstream in the pipeline.
examples.config:
```yaml
countInBatch: true
countInBatchItems: true
countInDocuments: true
countInSubDocuments: false
countInGroups: false
bufferSize: 0
```

entries.description: Join multiple XLIFF documents into one. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: XLIFF Joiner
schema.description: Join multiple XLIFF documents into one. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

inputFileMarker.title: Input file marker
inputFileMarker.description: Marker text in input filenames used to identify and group split XLIFF files for joining.

outputFileMarker.title: Output file marker
outputFileMarker.description: Marker text used to generate the output filename(s) by replacing the input marker and index.

toolMeta.description: Join multiple XLIFF documents into one. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Re-joins multiple XLIFF documents that were previously split (e.g., by the XLIFF Splitter Step) back into one or more combined documents based on filename patterns. Files sharing the same naming pattern (differing only by an index after the marker) are merged by collecting their `<file>` elements into a single output document. The first document in each group provides the surrounding XLIFF structure, while subsequent documents contribute only their `<file>` elements.

inputFileMarker.description: Marker text in input filenames used to identify and group split XLIFF files for joining.
inputFileMarker.help:
A text marker used to identify which input files belong to the same group and should be joined together. The step looks for this marker in each input filename — files whose names match the pattern `{basename}{marker}{index}.xlf` are grouped by `{basename}` and merged.

Files whose names do **not** contain this marker are skipped entirely.
inputFileMarker.values: Any string that appears in the filenames before the numeric index. Common values: `_PART` (default, matches XLIFF Splitter output).
notes: Files are joined in the order they arrive in the pipeline, **not** by their index number. Ensure the pipeline processes files in the desired order.
Files without the marker in their filename are skipped silently.
examples: With marker `_PART`: files `File1_PART001.xlf`, `File1_PART002.xlf` are grouped as `File1`
With marker `-chunk`: files `Doc-chunk1.xlf`, `Doc-chunk2.xlf` are grouped as `Doc`

outputFileMarker.description: Marker text used to generate the output filename(s) by replacing the input marker and index.
outputFileMarker.help:
The text that replaces the input marker (and its trailing index) in the output filename. Each group of joined files produces a single output file named `{basename}{outputMarker}.xlf`.

If an output file with the same name already exists in the output directory, it will be **overwritten without warning**.
outputFileMarker.values: Any string to use as the replacement marker in the output filename. Default: `_CONCAT`.
notes: **Warning:** Existing files with the same output name are overridden without any confirmation or warning.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: The first document in each naming-pattern group provides the XLIFF structure (content before the first `<file>` and after the last `<file>` element). Subsequent documents in the group contribute only their `<file>` elements and any content between multiple `<file>` elements.

Content before the first `<file>` and after the last `<file>` in subsequent documents of the same group is discarded, since the XLIFF Splitter Step duplicates this structural content into every split file.
Files are joined in pipeline arrival order, regardless of the numeric index appended by the XLIFF Splitter Step.
Files whose names do not contain the input file marker are skipped — they pass through unchanged.

examples.title: Rejoin split XLIFF files
examples.description: Joining files previously split by the XLIFF Splitter Step using default markers. Two groups (`File1` and `File2`) are each merged into a single output file, while `File3.xlf` is skipped because it lacks the `_PART` marker.
examples.output:
```
C:\Project\FolderABC\File1_CONCAT.xlf   (joined from File1_PART001 + File1_PART002)
C:\Project\FolderABC\File2_CONCAT.xlf   (joined from File2_PART001 + File2_PART002)
C:\Project\FolderABC\File3.xlf          (skipped — no "_PART" marker)
```

entries.description: Split an XLIFF document into separate files for each <file> element. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: XLIFF Splitter
schema.description: Split an XLIFF document into separate files for each <file> element. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

bigFile.title: Process Large Files
bigFile.description: Use a stream-based parser to handle very large XLIFF files that would not fit in memory with the default DOM parser.

fileMarker.title: File Marker
fileMarker.description: Marker string appended to generated filenames before the numeric counter (e.g. '_PART' produces 'file_PART1.xlf').

updateSDLTranslationStatus.title: Update WorldServer Translation Status
updateSDLTranslationStatus.description: Update translation_type and translation_status flags in <iws:status> elements (WorldServer XLIFF-specific).

translationTypeValue.title: Value for 'translation_type'
translationTypeValue.description: Value to set for the translation_type attribute.

translationStatusValue.title: Value for 'translation_status'
translationStatusValue.description: Value to set for the translation_status attribute.

restoreOriginalNames.title: Restore Original File Names
restoreOriginalNames.description: Use original file names for split output documents instead of generated numbered names.

toolMeta.description: Split an XLIFF document into separate files for each <file> element. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Splits a single XLIFF document into multiple documents, one per `<file>` element. All content outside `<file>` elements is copied as-is into each split document. An XLIFF document with only one `<file>` element is written out unaltered. Use the **XLIFF Joiner Step** to reassemble split files.

bigFile.description: Use a stream-based parser to handle very large XLIFF files that would not fit in memory with the default DOM parser.
bigFile.help: Uses a **stream-based parser** instead of the default DOM-based parser. This allows processing very large XLIFF input documents (e.g. several hundreds of MB) that would not fit into memory with the DOM parser.
notes: **Limitation:** When enabled, any XML parts between `<file>` elements are ignored and not copied into the new files.

fileMarker.description: Marker string appended to generated filenames before the numeric counter (e.g. '_PART' produces 'file_PART1.xlf').
fileMarker.help: A marker string appended to the end of each generated output filename, just before the numeric counter. For example, with the default `_PART`, an input file `document.xlf` produces `document_PART1.xlf`, `document_PART2.xlf`, etc.

restoreOriginalNames.description: Use original file names for split output documents instead of generated numbered names.
restoreOriginalNames.help: Restores the original file names of the split output documents instead of using the generated numbered names with the file marker.

translationStatusValue.help: The value written into the `translation_status` attribute of `<iws:status>` elements in each output document.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this value to be used

translationTypeValue.help: The value written into the `translation_type` attribute of `<iws:status>` elements in each output document.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this value to be used

updateSDLTranslationStatus.description: Update translation_type and translation_status flags in <iws:status> elements (WorldServer XLIFF-specific).
updateSDLTranslationStatus.help: Updates the `translation_type` and `translation_status` flags in `<iws:status>` elements of the output documents. This also **removes** the `target_content` attribute. This is specific to **WorldServer XLIFF** documents.
notes: This option also removes the `target_content` attribute from `<iws:status>` elements.

limitations: When using the **Process large files** option, any XML parts between `<file>` elements are ignored and not copied into the new files.
An XLIFF document with only one `<file>` element is written out unaltered (no splitting occurs).
processingNotes: The received event is unaltered — the step outputs multiple numbered XLIFF documents in the output path.
All content outside `<file>` elements (e.g. `<header>`, processing instructions) is copied as-is to each split document.
The default DOM-based parser requires the entire file to fit in memory; use **Process big file** for very large inputs.

examples.title: Split into numbered parts
examples.description: Split a multi-`<file>` XLIFF into one document per `<file>`, using a custom marker in the output names. With `fileMarker: _chunk`, `document.xlf` produces `document_chunk1.xlf`, `document_chunk2.xlf`, and so on.

examples.title: Stream a large file and stamp WorldServer status
examples.description: Use the stream-based parser for very large inputs and update the `<iws:status>` flags in each split document, which is specific to WorldServer XLIFF. Note that with `bigFile` enabled, XML parts between `<file>` elements are not copied into the outputs.
examples.config:
```yaml
bigFile: true
updateSDLTranslationStatus: true
translationTypeValue: machine_translation
translationStatusValue: unfinished
```

entries.description: Split an XLIFF document into separate documents based on word count.Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: XLIFF Word-Count Splitter
schema.description: Split an XLIFF document into separate documents based on word count.Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

threshold.title: Maximum Word Count Per Part
threshold.description: Maximum word count threshold for splitting XLIFF files into parts.

toolMeta.description: Split an XLIFF document into separate documents based on word count.Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: The XLIFF Word-Count Splitter step divides a single XLIFF 1.2 document into several smaller documents according to a source word-count threshold. It streams the input, tallies the source words of each trans-unit, and once the running count reaches the configured maximum it closes the current part and opens the next, reproducing the surrounding element structure so each part is a well-formed XLIFF file. It expects a raw document and passes it through, writing the numbered part files alongside the input.
limitations: Supports XLIFF 1.2 input only, and the source must be a URI-backed raw document — in-memory raw documents are not accepted.
A split occurs only at a trans-unit boundary once the threshold is reached, so an individual part may exceed the threshold if a single trans-unit pushes the count past it.
processingNotes: Word counting uses the source content of each trans-unit; trans-units marked `translate="no"` and those with a `state-qualifier` of `x-numeric`, `x-alphanum`, or `x-punctuation` are excluded from the count.
Output parts are written as `<input>_PART###.xlf` next to the input, each encoded as UTF-8 with a leading byte-order mark, and DTD processing is disabled on the XML reader for safety.
The source locale is taken from the incoming raw document rather than configured on the step.

examples.title: Split a large XLIFF into ~200,000-word parts
examples.description: Splits the input XLIFF 1.2 document at trans-unit boundaries into sequential `_PART###.xlf` files, starting a new part once the accumulated translatable word count crosses the threshold. Units marked `translate="no"` and numeric/alphanumeric/punctuation-only units are excluded from the count.

entries.description: Generate an analysis report of a set of XML documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: XML Analysis
schema.description: Generate an analysis report of a set of XML documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

outputPath.title: Path of the result file
outputPath.description: Full path of the result file.

autoOpen.title: Open the result file after completion
autoOpen.description: Automatically open the result file after the process is complete.

toolMeta.description: Generate an analysis report of a set of XML documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Generates an analysis report on the elements present in a set of input XML documents. The report helps guess localization-related aspects of the input documents, such as which elements should have their content extracted, which elements are likely inline, and whether there are any CDATA sections. Expects raw documents and passes them through unchanged.

autoOpen.description: Automatically open the result file after the process is complete.
autoOpen.help: Automatically opens the generated report file in the system's default browser or HTML viewer after the pipeline completes. Useful during interactive sessions; disable for batch or CI workflows.

outputPath.help: Full path where the HTML analysis report will be written. The report summarizes all XML elements found across the input documents, helping identify localization-relevant structures such as extractable content elements, inline elements, and CDATA sections.
outputPath.values: A file system path ending in `.html`. Can be absolute or relative to the working directory.
examples: `analysis.html` — writes to the current working directory

processingNotes: This step passes raw documents through unchanged — it only reads documents to build the report, it does not modify them.
The output report is HTML format, summarizing XML element usage across all documents processed in the batch.

examples.title: Basic XML analysis
examples.description: Run the XML Analysis step to generate a report of element usage across a set of XML files, automatically opening the result.

entries.description: Fixes invalid characters in XML documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: XML Characters Fixing
schema.description: Fixes invalid characters in XML documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

toolMeta.description: Fixes invalid characters in XML documents. Expects: raw document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Replaces characters that are invalid in XML documents with a configurable marker string. The step processes raw documents, recognizing both raw invalid characters and numeric character references (NCRs) such as `&#000B;` or ``. Output preserves the input encoding, and Byte-Order-Marks are maintained if present.

replacement.help:
The replacement string uses [Java Formatter syntax](http://download.oracle.com/javase/6/docs/api/java/util/Formatter.html). The parameter passed to the formatter is always the **integer value** of the Unicode code-point of the invalid character.

To **remove** invalid characters entirely, leave this field empty.

Special characters like `%` must be escaped (e.g., `%%` to produce a literal `%`).
replacement.values:
A [Java Formatter string](http://download.oracle.com/javase/6/docs/api/java/util/Formatter.html). Common format specifiers:
- `%X` — uppercase hexadecimal
- `%03X` — zero-padded 3-digit hex
- `%d` — decimal integer
- `%%` — literal percent sign
examples: `?` — replaces any invalid character with a literal `?`
`�` — replaces with the Unicode replacement character reference
`%%%d%%` — replaces U+000B with `%11%` (escaped percent signs around decimal value)
Empty string — removes invalid characters entirely

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Input encoding must be specified unless the document has a Byte-Order-Mark (BOM).
Output encoding matches the input encoding; BOMs are preserved if present.
The step recognizes both raw invalid characters and numeric character references (NCRs) like `&#000B;` or ``.

examples.title: Replace invalid characters with hex reference markers
examples.description: Using the default replacement string to convert invalid XML characters into hex-based markers that are visually identifiable in the output.

examples.title: Remove invalid characters
examples.description: Leave the replacement string empty to silently strip all invalid XML characters from the document.

examples.title: Replace with Unicode replacement character
examples.description: Replace invalid characters with an NCR for U+FFFD (the standard Unicode replacement character).

entries.description: Validate XML documents. Expects: raw XML document. Sends back: raw XML document.

schema.title: XML Validation
schema.description: Validate XML documents. Expects: raw XML document. Sends back: raw XML document.

useFoundDTD.title: Use DTD From Input Documents
useFoundDTD.description: Use DTD declarations found in the input documents for validation.

validate.title: Validate Document Structure
validate.description: Perform DTD, W3C, or RelaxNG schema validation in addition to the well-formedness check.

schemaPath.title: Path of the XML Schema
schemaPath.description: Full path of the XML Schema.

toolMeta.description: Validate XML documents. Expects: raw XML document. Sends back: raw XML document.

doc.overview: This step validates XML input documents for well-formedness and optionally performs structural validation against DTD, W3C XML Schema, or RelaxNG schema. Well-formedness checking is always performed regardless of validation settings — any non-XML format will fail this check. The step passes through raw XML documents unchanged.

schemaPath.help:
Path to the XML Schema or RelaxNG schema file used for validation. Can also be a **URL** for W3C and RelaxNG schemas.

If left empty, the step will attempt to locate a schema declaration inside the document itself. If no schema is found in the document, validation will fail.

Only used when **Type of validation** is set to W3C Schema or RelaxNG.
schemaPath.values: Full file path to an `.xsd` file, or a URL pointing to a W3C or RelaxNG schema.
notes: Any schema declared in the documents themselves will also be used in addition to the one specified here.
For DTD validation, the DTD must be specified in the input documents — this path field is not used.

dependsOn.condition: only used when set to W3C Schema or RelaxNG (not DTD)
must be enabled for schema path to have effect

useFoundDTD.description: Use DTD declarations found in the input documents for validation.
useFoundDTD.help:
Controls whether DTD declarations found inside the input documents are used for validation.

When disabled, any `<!DOCTYPE>` declarations in the documents are ignored during processing.
notes: When performing DTD validation, the DTD **must** be specified in the input documents — there is no option to provide an external DTD path.

validate.description: Perform DTD, W3C, or RelaxNG schema validation in addition to the well-formedness check.
validate.help:
Enables structural validation of documents in addition to the well-formedness check that always runs.

When disabled, only XML well-formedness is verified. When enabled, the step performs a full DTD, W3C Schema, or RelaxNG validation depending on the selected **Type of validation**.
notes: Well-formedness is **always** checked regardless of this setting. Non-XML content will fail even with validation disabled.

validationType.help:
Selects which validation method to use:

- **DTD** — Validates against DTD declarations. The DTD must be specified in the input documents.
- **W3C Schema** — Validates against a W3C XML Schema (`.xsd`). You can specify a schema path/URL or rely on schema declarations in the document.
- **RelaxNG Schema** — Validates against a RelaxNG schema. You can specify a schema path/URL or rely on schema declarations in the document.
validationType.values: Integer: `0` = DTD, `1` = W3C Schema, `2` = RelaxNG Schema

dependsOn.condition: must be enabled for this setting to have effect

limitations: None known per the Okapi wiki.
processingNotes: Well-formedness checking is **always** performed, even when validation is not selected. Any non-XML content will most likely fail.
The step passes through raw XML documents unchanged — it does not modify content.
For DTD validation, the DTD must be declared within the input documents themselves.
For W3C and RelaxNG schema validation, the schema path can be a URL.

examples.title: Well-formedness check only
examples.description: The simplest usage: just check that documents are valid XML without any schema validation.

examples.title: DTD validation
examples.description: Validate documents against DTD declarations embedded in the input files. The DTD must be declared in the document via `<!DOCTYPE>`.

examples.title: W3C Schema validation with external schema
examples.description: Validate against a specific W3C XML Schema file. Any schema declared in the documents will also be used.

examples.title: RelaxNG validation via URL
examples.description: Validate against a RelaxNG schema specified as a URL.

entries.description: Check that the source and/or target text of each block is well-formed XML.

checkSource.title: Check Source
checkSource.description: Validate source text for XML well-formedness

checkTarget.title: Check Target
checkTarget.description: Validate target text for XML well-formedness

wrapRoot.title: Wrap in Root Element
wrapRoot.description: Wrap text in a root element before validating

doc.overview:
The **XML Validation** tool checks the source and, optionally, target text of
each translatable block for XML well-formedness and records the result on the
block's properties: a validity flag and, when invalid, an error message
identifying whether the source or target failed and why. Empty text is treated
as valid.

Source validation is on by default. To validate the target as well, enable the
target check and supply a target locale. Because block text is often an XML
fragment rather than a complete document, the tool can wrap the text in a root
element before parsing so a fragment with multiple top-level nodes still
validates.

checkSource.description: Validate source text for XML well-formedness.
checkSource.help:
When enabled, each block's source text is parsed as XML and any
well-formedness error is reported.

checkTarget.description: Validate target text for XML well-formedness.
checkTarget.help:
When enabled, the target translation is parsed as XML in addition to the
source. A target locale must be supplied when this is enabled.

dependsOn.condition: a target locale is required when target checking is enabled

locale.help:
The locale whose translation is validated when target checking is enabled.
Required when `checkTarget` is on; ignored otherwise.

dependsOn.condition: only used when target checking is enabled

wrapRoot.description: Wrap text in a root element before validating.
wrapRoot.help:
When enabled, the text is wrapped in a synthetic root element before
parsing, so a fragment with several top-level nodes (which is not a valid
standalone document) still validates. Disable it to require a single
well-formed document.

limitations: Checks well-formedness only; it does not validate against a DTD or schema.
Empty source or target text is treated as valid.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks; non-translatable structure passes through unchanged.

examples.title: Validate source only
examples.description: Confirm the source text of each block parses as XML.

examples.title: Validate target as well
examples.description: Also validate the French translation, treating each block as a fragment.

entries.description: Apply an XSLT template to an XML document. Expects: raw XML document. Sends back: raw document.

schema.title: XSL Transformation
schema.description: Apply an XSLT template to an XML document. Expects: raw XML document. Sends back: raw document.

xsltPath.title: XSLT Template Path
xsltPath.description: Full path of the XSLT template to apply on input documents.

paramList.title: Template Parameters
paramList.description: Parameters passed to the XSLT template, one per line in name=value format.

useCustomTransformer.title: Use Custom Transformer
useCustomTransformer.description: Use custom XSLT transformer and XPath factory classes instead of JVM defaults.

factoryClass.title: Transformer Factory Class
factoryClass.description: Fully qualified class name of the TransformerFactory implementation to use.

xpathClass.title: XPath Factory Class
xpathClass.description: Fully qualified class name of the XPathFactory implementation to use.

passOnOutput.title: Pass Output to Next Step
passOnOutput.description: Pass the transformation output as the raw document for subsequent pipeline steps.

toolMeta.description: Apply an XSLT template to an XML document. Expects: raw XML document. Sends back: raw document.

doc.overview: Applies an XSLT template to input XML documents, producing a transformed raw document for the next pipeline step. Input documents must be well-formed XML. The step supports parameterized templates with built-in variables for input/output paths and locale information.

factoryClass.description: Fully qualified class name of the TransformerFactory implementation to use.
factoryClass.help: Fully qualified Java class name of the `TransformerFactory` implementation to use. The class must be available on the Java classpath.
examples: `net.sf.saxon.TransformerFactoryImpl` — use Saxon as the XSLT processor

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this value to take effect

paramList.description: Parameters passed to the XSLT template, one per line in name=value format.
paramList.help:
Parameters passed to the XSLT template, one per line in `name=value` format.

Available variables for parameter values:

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `${inputPath}` | Full path of the first input document |
| `${inputURI}` | URI of the first input document |
| `${outputPath}` | Full path of the first output document |
| `${srcLang}` | Source language code (e.g. `de` from `de-ch`) |
| `${trgLang}` | Target language code (e.g. `ja` from `ja-jp`) |
| `${inputPath2}`, `${inputPath3}` | Paths of 2nd/3rd input documents |
| `${inputURI2}`, `${inputURI3}` | URIs of 2nd/3rd input documents |

`${inputPath1}`, `${inputURI1}`, and `${outputPath1}` are aliases for the non-numbered variants.
paramList.values: One parameter per line in `name=value` format. Values may contain `${variable}` placeholders.
examples: `inputFile=${inputPath2}` — use a variable to reference the second input document

passOnOutput.description: Pass the transformation output as the raw document for subsequent pipeline steps.
passOnOutput.help: Controls whether the transformation output is passed forward as the raw document for subsequent pipeline steps. When enabled (default), the transformed result replaces the input document in the pipeline.

useCustomTransformer.description: Use custom XSLT transformer and XPath factory classes instead of JVM defaults.
useCustomTransformer.help:
Overrides the default JVM XSLT transformer and optionally the XPath engine with custom factory classes. The default transformer depends on your Java Virtual Machine implementation and version (see [`TransformerFactory.newInstance()`](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/javax/xml/transform/TransformerFactory.html#newInstance()) for the lookup mechanism).

Enable this to use a specific XSLT processor such as **Saxon**, which provides XSLT 2.0/3.0 support beyond the JVM's built-in XSLT 1.0 processor.

xpathClass.description: Fully qualified class name of the XPathFactory implementation to use.
xpathClass.help: Fully qualified Java class name of the `XPathFactory` implementation to use. Allows overriding the default JVM XPath engine alongside the custom transformer. The class must be available on the Java classpath.

dependsOn.condition: must be true for this value to take effect

xsltPath.description: Full path of the XSLT template to apply on input documents.
xsltPath.help: Full path to the XSLT stylesheet (`.xsl` or `.xslt`) to apply to each input document. The template is applied to the raw XML input and the transformation result replaces the document for subsequent pipeline steps.

limitations: None known.
processingNotes: Input documents must be well-formed XML — malformed XML will cause the transformation to fail.

The default XSLT processor is determined by the JVM's `TransformerFactory.newInstance()` lookup, which typically provides XSLT 1.0 only. Use a custom transformer (e.g. Saxon) for XSLT 2.0/3.0 support.
The step operates on raw documents — it does not use Okapi's filter event model.

examples.title: Basic XSLT transformation
examples.description: Apply a simple XSLT template to transform XML documents with language parameters passed to the stylesheet.

examples.title: Using Saxon for XSLT 2.0
examples.description: Configure the step to use Saxon as the XSLT processor, enabling XSLT 2.0/3.0 features.
examples.config:
```yaml
xsltPath: /path/to/transform-v2.xslt
useCustomTransformer: true
factoryClass: net.sf.saxon.TransformerFactoryImpl
xpathClass: net.sf.saxon.xpath.XPathFactoryImpl
```

examples.title: Using pipeline variables in parameters
examples.description: Pass document paths and locale information to the XSLT template using built-in variables.

entries.description: Apply regex-based tag and text transformation rules to block text.

passOnOutput.title: Pass Output to Next Step
passOnOutput.description: Pass the transformation output to subsequent pipeline steps

rules.title: Transformation Rules
rules.description: Regex-based tag and text transformation rules

doc.overview:
The **XSLT Transform** tool applies a sequence of regular-expression
transformation rules to block text. Each rule has a pattern and a replacement
(with `$1`, `$2` backreference support), and the rules are applied in order.
Since Go has no built-in XSLT engine, this provides a lightweight,
regex-based equivalent for tag and text rewriting. It does not execute XSLT
stylesheets; for stylesheet-based transforms use the okapi-bridge transform
step.

By default the rules are applied to source text. When the rules list is
empty, blocks pass through unchanged.

applySource.description: Apply the transformation to source text.
applySource.help:
When enabled, the rules are applied to the source text. If neither source
nor target is set, source is used by default.

applyTarget.description: Apply the transformation to target text.
applyTarget.help:
When enabled, the rules are applied to the target text of every target
locale present on the block.

passOnOutput.description: Pass the transformed output to subsequent pipeline steps.
passOnOutput.help:
When enabled, the transformation output is forwarded to later steps in
the pipeline.

rules.description: The regex transformation rules to apply, in order.
rules.help:
A list of rules, each with a `pattern` (a regular expression) and a
`replace` string supporting `$1`, `$2` backreferences. Rules are applied
sequentially to the text.

limitations: Transformation is regex-based; full XSLT stylesheets are not executed.
Patterns use Go's RE2 syntax, which does not support backreferences or lookaround within the match.
processingNotes: Operates on translatable blocks only; non-translatable blocks pass through unchanged.
With no rules configured, blocks pass through unchanged.

examples.title: Rewrite a tag in source text
examples.description: Replace one element name with another using a regex rule.
